**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a way to enhance large language models by inserting small, trainable quantum circuits into classical transformer layers. By using Cayley-parameterized adapters on real IBM quantum hardware, the authors achieved measurable improvements in model perplexity and reasoning capabilities.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The scaling bottleneck of classical LLM memory and the lack of practical demonstrations of quantum-enhanced LLMs on real, large-scale hardware.

**Main result.** The implementation of Cayley Unitary Adapters on IBM hardware improved Llama 3.1 8B perplexity by 1.43% using only 6,000 additional parameters and demonstrated qualitative reasoning wins over classical baselines.

**Method.** Insertion of block-diagonal Cayley-parameterized unitary adapters into frozen transformer layers, using amplitude encoding and a sign-correction technique to handle QPU measurement constraints.

**Model / system.** The study utilizes the Llama 3.1 8B and SmolLM2 135M transformer architectures executed on the IBM Quantum System Two (specifically the IBM Heron r2 superconducting processor).

**Key observables.** Perplexity (PPL) on WikiText, MMLU accuracy, Operator Schmidt Rank (OSR), and operator entanglement entropy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 2-qubit and multi-qubit block dimensions, 156-qubit IBM hardware, 8,192 measurement shots, and depolarizing error rates.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The current implementation is limited to 2-qubit blocks due to hardware coherence limits; larger unitaries require approximate compilation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the CUA architecture; other figures show monotonic perplexity improvement with block dimension and qualitative success in math/logic tasks.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to scaling bottlenecks, description of the CUA architecture and Cayley transform, implementation details on IBM hardware, performance evaluation on Llama and SmolLM models, and analysis of noise and entanglement capacity.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Large language models (LLMs) have transformed artificial intelligence, yet classical architectures impose a fundamental constraint: every trainable parameter demands classical memory that scales unfavourably with model size. Quantum computing offers a qualitatively different pathway, but practical demonstrations on real hardware have remained elusive for models of practical relevance. Here we show that Cayley-parameterised unitary adapters -- quantum circuit blocks inserted into the frozen projection layers of pre-trained LLMs and executed on a 156-qubit IBM Quantum System Two superconducting processor -- improve the perplexity of Llama 3.1 8B, an 8-billion-parameter model in widespread use, by 1.4% with only 6,000 additional parameters and end-to-end inference validated on real Quantum Processing Unit (QPU). A systematic study on SmolLM2 (135M parameters), chosen for its tractability, reveals monotonically improving perplexity with unitary block dimension, 83% recovery of compression-induced degradation, and correct answers to questions that both classical baselines fail -- with a sharp noise-expressivity phase transition identifying the concrete path to quantum utility at larger qubit scales.

</details>

### [Entanglement generation in a two-body Schrödinger--Newton model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06577v1)

**Authors:** Marcin Płodzień, Julia Osęka-Lenart, Maciej Lewenstein, Michał Eckstein  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06577v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) (blue) and ρ2(x) (red); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) (blue) and ρ2(x) (red); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.</sub>

Despite being initialized from stationary profiles, the LL/RR and LR/RL components acquire different phases and accelerations under the pair potential, producing SvN ≈1.5. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) and ρ2(x); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel, with snapshots at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.</sub>

The entanglement entropy oscillates around SvN ≈1.28, reflecting deformation of the two same-side components under the common self-field and pair attraction. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) and ρ2(x); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel, with snapshots at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.</sub>

The mutual gravitational attraction drives the disjoint spatial components together, increasing the entanglement entropy to SvN ≈1.67. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) and ρ2(x); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel, with snapshots at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how entanglement arises in a two-body system governed by the Schrödinger-Newton equation. It demonstrates that entanglement is driven by the inter-particle pair potential rather than self-interaction, and that spatial dispersion and mass asymmetry significantly enhance this entanglement generation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides fundamental insights into how semiclassical gravity-like interactions can generate quantum correlations and explores the boundary between mean-field dynamics and entanglement-rich many-body evolution.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the mechanism of entanglement generation in a two-body Schrödinger-Newton model and distinguishing between the effects of nonlinear self-localization and nonseparable Newtonian pair potentials.

**Main result.** The authors show that while nonlinear self-fields preserve the Schmidt spectrum, entanglement is driven specifically by the nonseparable pair potential, with growth being amplified by spatial delocalization and mass asymmetry.

**Method.** Analytical derivation of the Schmidt spectrum evolution and numerical simulations using second-order Strang splitting and FFT-based potential calculations in a 1D regularized geometry.

**Model / system.** A two-body Schrödinger-Newton model featuring both local nonlinear self-gravitational potentials and a nonseparable Newtonian pair potential between two particles.

**Key observables.** von Neumann entropy, Schmidt eigenvalues, Wigner function negativity, Linear entropy, and Participation Ratio.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Self-gravitational coupling (kappa), pair-gravitational coupling (gamma), mass ratio (mu1/mu2), and regularization scale (epsilon).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a 1D regularized geometry with a softened kernel to avoid singularities; the derivation of the mean-field limit assumes a product-state (Hartree) ansatz.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate collision dynamics of Gaussian vs. stationary profiles, the evolution of joint and marginal densities, Wigner function fragmentation, and the impact of mass asymmetry on entanglement growth.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the two-body SN model, provides an analytical proof regarding the preservation of the Schmidt spectrum by self-fields, presents numerical results on entanglement growth for various initial states and mass ratios, and concludes with a variational derivation of the mean-field (Hartree) limit.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Schrödinger--Newton (SN) equation provides a semiclassical framework for the evolution of self-gravitating of massive quantum systems. We propose a two-body Schrödinger--Newton model that separates local nonlinear self-localization from the nonseparable Newtonian pair potential. Analytically, we show that the nonlinear self-field preserves the Schmidt spectrum, whereas direct entanglement generation arises from the nonseparable pair potential. Using numerical simulations in a regularized one-dimensional geometry, we find that entanglement generation depends sensitively on the initial spatial configuration and on the mass ratio. Highly localized, self-bound wavepackets experience minimal entanglement growth during scattering. Spatial delocalization and kinetic dispersion broaden the interaction region, amplifying the entangling power of the pair potential and exciting higher-order spatial modes. For dispersive Gaussian initial states, mass asymmetry shatters the lighter particle, producing Wigner negativity and rapid entanglement growth, whereas stationary SN profiles strongly suppress this effect. Stationary SN profiles isolate the bare pair-potential contribution; dispersive Gaussian initial states inflate it.

</details>

### [Ablation Removal of Transport-Blocking Defects in Surface-Electrode Ion Traps](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06312v1)

**Authors:** Toby Maddock, Parsa Rahimi, Matthew Aylett, Rares Barcan, Sebastian Weidt, Winfried Karl Hensinger  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06312v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**

The measurement is consistent with height estimates based on high resolution images, indicating a defect height of approximately 65 µm.</sub>

<img src="2605.06312_figures/2605.06312_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

The combined guide and ablation laser beams are delivered through a side window into the UHV chamber. Both beams operate at the same wavelength (532 nm) to enable reliable alignment and to avoid chro- matic aberrations that could lead to systematic misalignment. Beam alignment is performed in the XZ plane using a micrometer trans- lation stage, while focusing is achieved with a lens of focal length F = 150.</sub>

</details>

Panels a) and d) show an overview of the chip region containing the defect, while panels b), c), e), and f) provide magnified views of the defect location and the chip surface after defect removal. Electrodes are numbered sequentially starting from the central gap. The defect is located at the edge of electrode 7. The electrode width is indicated for scale.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.06312_figures/2605.06312_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. a) Micromotion compensation voltages required to zero photon correlation modulation indices to zero. Yellow denotes the quadratrically expanding voltages required to compensate prior to defect removal - further data points could not be collected due to guaranteed ion loss, as denoted by the blue shaded region. Green de- notes the micromotion voltages required following removal of the defect. b) Sampling further along the trap, highlighting the next maxima of compensation force required to zero photon correlation signals.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a method to repair surface-electrode ion traps in situ using pulsed laser ablation to remove physical defects. This technique avoids the need to break vacuum, significantly reducing downtime for complex, cryogenic quantum computing architectures. The process successfully restores ion transport capabilities while maintaining low levels of micromotion.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a practical engineering solution for maintaining the reliability and uptime of large-scale, modular ion-trap quantum computers, which is critical for scaling up quantum information processing.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Large particulate defects on surface-electrode ion trap chips block ion transport and induce micromotion, but traditional repair methods like vacuum venting and rebaking cause significant experimental downtime.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully demonstrated in situ removal of a transport-blocking defect using pulsed laser ablation, restoring near-unit shuttling success rates without damaging surrounding electrodes.

**Method.** A 532 nm nanosecond-pulsed Nd:YAG laser was used for targeted ablation of the defect, with thermal management achieved through short bursts and inter-pulse delays.

**Model / system.** A two-module quantum processor experiment utilizing surface-electrode ion traps operated at cryogenic temperatures (47-50 K) in an ultra-high vacuum environment.

**Key observables.** Ion shuttling success rates, in-plane micromotion levels, RF pseudopotential distortions, and DC voltage perturbations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Defect dimensions of approximately 65 micrometers; ablation fluence of 5.6 J/cm^2; 532 nm wavelength; 1.5 ns pulse duration.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumed an ideal Gaussian beam profile and a beam perfectly parallel to the trap surface for fluence calculations on neighboring electrodes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a side-view and magnified view of the defect with EMCCD data for height estimation; Figure 5 illustrates micromotion compensation voltages before and after ablation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of transport-blocking defects, describes the experimental setup and laser ablation hardware, details the characterization and ablation process, presents the results regarding shuttling success and micromotion, and discusses the implications for scalable architectures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We demonstrate in situ removal of a transport-blocking defect on a surface-electrode ion trap device using a Q-switched Nd:YAG 532 nm pulsed ablation laser. This approach eliminates the need to vent and rebake the vacuum system, providing a low-overhead defect-remediation technique well suited for ion-shuttling architectures where system modifications typically incur substantial downtime - particularly in shuttling focussed experiments operating at temperatures that necessitate bakes. Additionally, the hardware used is readily available in many ion trap laboratories, making this solution attractive to experiments operating in such regimes. Following ablation, we observe near-unit shuttling success rates across the previously obstructed region and measure micromotion levels that remain within acceptable limits. This technique enables rapid, reliable restoration of transport pathways without interruption to experimental operation.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.06372"></a>
### [Coherence limitations of a Fourier-engineered $\cos(2\varphi)$ transmon qubit](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06372v1)

**Authors:** Nataliia K. Zhurbina, Siddharth Singh, Lukas J. Splitthoff, Eugene Y. Huang, Figen Yilmaz, A. Mert Bozkurt, Christian Kraglund Andersen  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06372v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06372_figures/2605.06372_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Qubit circuit and device design. (a) Idealized circuit implementing a cos(2φ)-protected qubit. The cross element enables the tunneling of pairs of Cooper pairs, generating an effective cos(2φ) potential. The tunneling element is addition- ally shunted by a large capacitor. (b) Top: Interference-based implementation of the cos(2φ) element using SNS junctions. Bottom: Schematic of the fabricated device, where the left SQUID arm forms an effectively fixed-energy SNS junction using two Josephson junctions in series. The right arm also implements an effective SNS junction with a single Josephson junction in series with a SQUID loop, such that the effective SNS junction parameters are...</sub>

</details>

(c–f) Line cuts of (a) for Φctrl equal to 0.406 Φ0, 0.395 Φ0, 0.378 Φ0, and 0.367 Φ0, respectively, overlaid with simulated resonator shifts. **Summary.** This paper experimentally realizes a cos(2phi) transmon qubit using Fourier engineering to suppress charge errors. It demonstrates that while the design successfully suppresses the first harmonic, the qubit remains highly sensitive to 1/f flux noise, which limits its coherence compared to fluxonium qubits.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is relevant to open quantum systems researchers as it provides a detailed experimental study of how specific noise spectral densities (1/f flux noise) couple to a non-standard Hamiltonian and limit the coherence of a protected quantum state.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the coherence limitations of a Fourier-engineered cos(2phi) transmon qubit, specifically identifying why 1/f flux noise limits the qubit lifetime at the flux symmetry point.

**Main result.** The researchers found that while the device achieves a protected regime, the qubit lifetime is limited by 1/f flux noise due to residual first-harmonic fluctuations, making it more sensitive to flux noise than a fluxonium qubit.

**Method.** The authors used two-tone spectroscopy, resonator spectroscopy, and time-domain measurements (Rabi oscillations, T1) combined with a numerical N-level decoherence model and Fermi's Golden Rule to analyze noise contributions.

**Model / system.** A multi-junction superconducting circuit using an interference-based architecture (a 'big' SQUID loop and a 'small' SQUID loop) designed to engineer a cos(2phi) potential via Fourier engineering.

**Key observables.** Qubit transition frequency (f01), energy relaxation time (T1), resonator frequency shifts, and charge matrix elements.

**Important parameters / regimes.** EC/h = 0.21 GHz, EJ/EC >> 50, 1/f flux noise amplitude A1/f = 1.5e-5 Phi0, and effective Josephson energy EJ approx 40 GHz.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the two flux noise channels are uncorrelated, uses the Born-Oppenheimer approximation for internal mode energy, and assumes the system is in thermal equilibrium at 10 mK.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the circuit architecture and device SEM; Fig 2 shows resonator frequency shifts; Fig 3 displays the transition spectrum and visualized wavefunctions; Fig 4 compares measured T1 to modeled noise contributions; Fig S4 compares 2-level vs N-level models.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the protected qubit concept, describes the experimental fabrication and device architecture, presents spectroscopic characterization of the engineered potential, analyzes T1 relaxation as a function of flux, compares noise sources via modeling, and concludes with a comparison to fluxonium.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Intrinsically protected superconducting qubits are a promising route toward enhancing coherence times and advancing hardware towards applications in quantum computing. The $\cos(2\varphi)$ qubit achieves protection against qubit relaxation by allowing only the coherent tunneling of pairs of Cooper pairs, resulting in Cooper-pair parity symmetry and thereby suppressing charge-induced errors. In this work, we experimentally realize a $\cos(2\varphi)$ qubit by Fourier engineering the energy-phase relation in a multi-junction superconducting circuit. Using an interference-based architecture, we are able to suppress the odd harmonics of an effective qubit potential and we observe good agreement between the measured transition spectrum and the effective theoretical model. We further investigate the energy relaxation time as a function of external flux and find that the qubit lifetime at the flux symmetry point is limited by $1/f$ flux noise. This strong sensitivity arises from residual fluctuations in the first harmonic, which possesses a large prefactor despite being nominally canceled. In contrast, a fluxonium qubit with a similar energy spectrum and noise amplitude is less affected by flux noise, highlighting a key challenge for interference-based protection schemes.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.06224"></a>
### [Modular wedge localization, Majorana fields and the Tsirelson limit of the Bell-CHSH inequality](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06224v1)

**Authors:** J. G. A. Caribé, M. S. Guimaraes, I. Roditi, S. P. Sorella  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06224v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06224_figures/2605.06224_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Behavior of the Bell-CHSH correlator ⟨0∣C∣0⟩as a function of the parameters (a,b). The blue surface denotes the classical bound: 2. The orange surface above the blue one corresponds to the values of (a,b) for which violations of the Bell-CHSH inequality take place. Near-Tsirelson values occur for small values of b and a ≈0, where the spectral weight is concentrated near ω = 0.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06224_figures/2605.06224_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2: Behavior of the Bell-CHSH correlator ⟨0∣C∣0⟩as a function of the parameter b. Large violations take place for small values of b, with the Tsirelson value approached as b →0.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how the Bell-CHSH inequality is violated in the vacuum state of a 1+1 dimensional massive Majorana field. It shows that the maximum violation, the Tsirelson bound, is a supremum reached when the spectral weight of the modular operator concentrates near zero. The work provides a concrete realization of how modular localization relates to quantum non-locality.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper provides a deep connection between the algebraic structure of relativistic QFT (Type III von Neumann algebras) and the fundamental limits of quantum correlations (Tsirelson bound), which is highly relevant for understanding the foundations of quantum information in relativistic settings.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the violation of the Bell-CHSH inequality within the framework of relativistic Quantum Field Theory and determining how the spectral properties of the modular operator influence the approach to the Tsirelson bound.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that the Bell-CHSH correlator in the vacuum state can approach the Tsirelson bound of 2√2 as the spectral weight of the modular operator concentrates near zero, linking the maximum violation to the Type III_1 nature of local algebras.

**Method.** The study utilizes Tomita-Takesaki theory, modular localization, and the Bisognano-Wichmann theorem, implementing the Summers-Werner construction directly in rapidity space.

**Model / system.** A massive Majorana field in 1+1 dimensions, utilizing wedge regions and their causal complements in Minkowski spacetime.

**Key observables.** Bell-CHSH correlator/value

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass (m), rapidity (θ), modular operator eigenvalue (λ), and the spectral weight (h^2(ω))

**Assumptions / limitations.** Requires the Majorana field to exhibit bounded analytic extension in a specific strip and uses a fermion-parity twist to ensure commuting Bell observables.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 is a 3D surface plot showing the Bell-CHSH correlator as a function of Gaussian parameters, identifying the classical and violation regions. Figure 2 shows the correlator behavior as a function of parameter b, demonstrating the approach to the Tsirelson value as b approaches 0.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from the quantization of the Majorana field to the definition of the 1-particle Hilbert space, followed by the application of modular theory to wedge regions, the derivation of a spectral formula for the Bell-CHSH correlator, and finally an analysis of the limit approaching the Tsirelson bound.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The massive Majorana field in $1+1$ dimension is employed to investigate the violation of the Bell-CHSH inequality in relativistic Quantum Field Theory. We give an explicit rapidity-space realization of the Summers-Werner modular-localization construction and reduce the vacuum Bell-CHSH correlator to a single spectral weight $h^2(ω)$ for the modular operator. The resulting analytic families approach the Tsirelson bound in the vacuum state as their spectral weight concentrates near $ω\approx0$, corresponding to the eigenvalue $λ^2 \approx 1$ of the modular operator.

</details>

**Authors:** Pablo Aramburu Sanchez, Trevyn F. Q. Larson, Anthony P. McFadden, Constantin Schrade, Joshua Combes, András Gyenis

<sub>Fig. 4 shows, this approach has several benefits: (1) the qubit has a higher frequency and experiences less of a 1/f flux noise amplitude (Fig. 4a), (2) the magnitude of the matrix elements of the sin( ˆφi) and sin( ˆφi/2) operators get slightly reduced (Fig. 4b), (3) the charge sensitivity of the device be- comes more suppressed (Fig. 4c), and (4) the curvature of the transition with respect of flux is lowered (Fig. 4d). These ob- servations suggest that the modified version of the rhombus, which we have nicknamed the soft-rhombus qubit, can poten- tially outperform the ideal case of the rhombus, as long as the noise associated with the ˆni operators is not the dominant source of loss at...</sub>

The asymmetry increases the transition frequency, leading to lower 1/f flux noise amplitude at f01. (b) Var- ious matrix elements of the computational states as a function of α. While asymmetry increases the charge matrix element, other matrix elements reduce. (c) The charge dispersion of the qubit transition, which is calculated as the change in frequency when n(1) g is changed by half a period. The qubit becomes less charge-noise sensitive as the asymmetry is increased. (d) The curvature of the qubit frequency vs. flux at frustration...</sub>

**Summary.** The researchers revisit the rhombus qubit by introducing junction asymmetry to create a 'soft' version that functions as a biased-noise qubit. This approach allows for higher transition frequencies that mitigate 1/f flux noise, achieving significantly improved relaxation times. The work identifies an optimal operating regime for these circuits by balancing the trade-offs between different noise channels.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides insights into engineering noise-resilient qubits by intentionally breaking symmetries to move into a biased-noise regime, which is highly relevant for open quantum systems and the development of hardware-efficient error correction.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the trade-off in rhombus qubits between charge-parity protection and susceptibility to relaxation from flux and quasiparticle noise, specifically seeking to mitigate 1/f flux noise.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that a 'soft' rhombus qubit with intentional junction asymmetry can operate as a biased-noise qubit, achieving long relaxation times (T1 approx 500 us) by increasing transition frequency and reducing noise sensitivity.

**Method.** The study combines circuit quantization and numerical simulations of a multi-mode Hamiltonian with experimental characterization using one- and two-tone resonator spectroscopy and Ramsey/echo measurements.

**Model / system.** A multi-mode superconducting rhombus circuit consisting of four Josephson junctions in a loop, featuring an asymmetric junction energy (alpha < 1) and large shunting capacitors.

**Key observables.** Relaxation time (T1), Ramsey dephasing time (Tr), energy-flux dispersion, and resonator transmission.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Junction asymmetry ratio (alpha), external magnetic flux (Phi_ext), charging energy (Ec), Josephson energy (Ej), and quasiparticle density (x_qp approx 10^-8).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes negligible correlations between tunneling events across different junctions and that the loss contribution of a junction can be neglected near the frustration point.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the protected array concept and wavefunctions; Fig 2 illustrates interference principles and tunneling probability; Fig 3 shows potential energy; Fig 5 displays the device image, energy spectrum, and 3D phase wavefunctions; Fig 6/7/8 show noise contributions, experimental wiring, and circuit schematics.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the rhombus qubit concept, proposes the 'soft' asymmetric modification, details the circuit quantization and Hamiltonian modeling, presents experimental fabrication and spectroscopic results, performs a detailed noise analysis (flux, charge, and quasiparticle), and concludes with the identification of an optimal operating regime.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we revisit the idea of using an interferometer of pairs of Josephson junctions as a protected rhombus qubit. Unlike in the original proposal, where the qubit states are encoded into odd and even parity charge states, here, we intentionally alter the energy of one of the junctions to investigate the soft version of the rhombus qubit. This approach allows us to directly probe the qubit transitions over several GHz and reduce the potential drawbacks of the interferometer-based protection. Away from a half flux quantum external field, the large shunting capacitors of the circuit ensure localized qubit states in different phase valleys, leading to a biased-noise qubit. In the realized circuit, we measure an average $T_1\approx500\,μ$s relaxation time in the biased-noise regime (with a Ramsey dephasing time of $T^{R}_\varphi\approx90\,$ns), while an average $T_1\approx27\,μ$s relaxation time at frustration (with $T^{R}_\varphi\approx670\,$ns). Our loss analysis on this multi-mode circuit indicates that at low frequencies, flux noise and quasiparticle tunneling limit the relaxation times, pointing toward the presence of an optimal operating regime of around a few GHz.

</details>

Radial distortions (red path) involve amplitude fluctuations associated with finite energy gaps. (b) Representative examples of ordered states characterized by a nonzero order parameter, such as spin-density-wave and Cooper-pair condensates, illustrating spontaneous breaking of continuous...</sub>

The RKKY interaction oscillates in sign and magnitude as a function of interatomic distance, leading to competing ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic couplings between dilute magnetic moments. These randomly distributed interactions generate magnetic frustration. The inset shows a lattice with dilute magnetic atoms embedded in a nonmagnetic matrix, illustrating the presence of quenched disorder and spatial inhomogeneity. The interplay between magnetic frustration, disorder, and localization gives rise to spin freezing in metallic spin glasses. Adapted from the ref. [52] with permission.</sub>

</details>

The inset illustrates a rugged free-energy landscape comprising a multitude of metastable minima (solid lines), representative of frozen yet fluctuating spin configurations. The field-cooled path (dashed line) reflects traversal through a convex envelope of these states, highlighting the absence of full thermal equilibration below the freezing temperature. (b) Free-energy branches in rotating superfluid 3He (bottom), where discrete vortex states appear as local minima in angular momentum...</sub>

(a) Temperature dependence of the spin-lattice relaxation rate below Tg, 1/T1 for Li2RhO3, measured under applied magnetic fields of 4.23 T and 6.89 T. A broad maximum and subsequent rapid drop in 1/T1 signify the onset of spin freezing. Inset: Field-independent magnetic specific heat Cm/T vs T data up to 9 T, highlighting the robust low- energy spin dynamics characteristic of topological spin freezing in Kitaev magnets. (b) Relative NMR line width δH = ∆H/H at 70 and 114 MHz as a function of temperature, showing significant broadening in Li2RhO3 compared to...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This review explores how topological constraints and geometric frustration in quantum materials lead to an unconventional form of spin freezing known as topological spin glass behavior. It bridges experimental observations from neutron scattering and NMR with theoretical models like the spin jam formalism. The work provides a unified perspective on how complexity and ergodicity breaking emerge from the interplay of topology and frustration.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study of topological defects, constrained dynamics, and the emergence of rigidity from geometric constraints is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the study of fragmented Hilbert spaces in quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the emergence of unconventional 'topological' spin glass dynamics in frustrated quantum materials, aiming to distinguish this behavior from conventional spin glasses driven by random disorder.

**Main result.** The authors establish a unified framework showing that topological constraints and geometric frustration can drive spin freezing and create non-hierarchical energy landscapes, effectively creating 'topological spin glasses' or 'spin jam' states.

**Method.** The study utilizes a comprehensive review of experimental probes including NMR, muon spin relaxation (muSR), neutron scattering, and thermodynamic measurements, alongside theoretical frameworks like hydrodynamic theory and the spin jam formalism.

**Model / system.** The primary systems are frustrated quantum magnets on Kagome, triangular, square, pyrochlore, and Kitaev lattices, including models like the Edwards-Anderson and Sherrington-Kirkpatrick models.

**Key observables.** Spin dynamics, short-range spin correlations, specific heat signatures, magnetic susceptibility, and emergent low-energy excitations (Goldstone and Higgs modes).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Freezing temperature (Tg), energy landscape hierarchy, magnetic percolation threshold, and the role of quantum fluctuations and chemical pressure.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The paper addresses the unresolved question of whether the spin glass transition constitutes a true phase transition and notes the difficulty in identifying ground states in the absence of Bragg peaks.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the progression from symmetry-breaking (Goldstone/Higgs modes) to constrained manifolds (spin folds, monopoles, Z2 fluxes) and finally to non-hierarchical spin jam energy landscapes; Figure 2 depicts the RKKY interaction and its role in driving spin freezing.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from fundamental principles of symmetry and topology to the specific manifestations in frustrated lattices, connecting microscopic quantum phenomena to macroscopic glassy dynamics and concluding with a unified framework for understanding topological spin freezing.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Competing interactions, non-trivial electronic band topology, quantum fluctuations, and the interplay between emergent degrees of freedom in frustrated quantum materials can give rise to a wide range of exotic phenomena. Glassy dynamics, originally studied in amorphous materials and biological systems, has recently attracted considerable interest in quantum condensed matter, particularly in relation to the collective behavior of spins, quasiparticle excitations, and topological spin textures. Here, we investigate the emergence of unconventional glassy spin dynamics in a broad class of frustrated quantum materials, where spin freezing exhibit distinct signatures in both thermodynamic and microscopic measurements. Using a comprehensive set of experimental probes, including thermodynamic, NMR, ($μ$SR), and neutron scattering, we identify characteristic signatures of topological spin-glass behavior and these complementary techniques reveal unconventional spin dynamics, short-range spin correlations, emergent low-energy excitations, and glassy behavior of topological origins, distinguishing these states from conventional spin glasses and disordered magnets. Furthermore, we discuss the role of hydrodynamic spin modes in governing glassy dynamics and the emergence of spin-jam states in frustrated lattices, providing a unified framework for understanding unconventional spin freezing of topological origin and bridging experimental observations with theoretical models. This review aims to advance our understanding of collective many-body phenomena arising from competing interactions, topological defects, collective excitations, quantum entanglement, and symmetry constraints. Such insights may facilitate the discovery and design of novel quantum materials and help address fundamental questions in contemporary condensed matter physics, with potential implications for future quantum technologies.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Summary.** This paper develops a framework to understand how transitioning from a perfectly flat band to a narrow dispersive band affects quantum emission. It provides quantitative scaling laws to tune the system between coherent population trapping and Markovian decay. This is particularly relevant for engineering light-matter interactions in structured photonic platforms.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a unified theoretical framework for controlling quantum emission dynamics, offering a toolkit for designing photonic environments like moire photonic crystals for applications in quantum memory or single-photon sources.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Bridging the gap between idealized flat-band coherence and realistic narrow-band dissipation in quantum emission, specifically understanding how the transition from a dispersionless to a dispersive band alters light-matter coupling.

**Main result.** The authors derive scaling laws for the crossover from non-Markovian population trapping to Markovian decay, showing that the decay rate depends on the competition between coupling strength and engineered bandwidth.

**Method.** The study uses a Green's function formalism with Lehmann spectral representation, self-energy analysis, and direct numerical simulations of the excited-state population.

**Model / system.** A single quantum emitter coupled to a one-dimensional Lieb lattice consisting of three sublattices (A, B, and C), where an on-site potential on the C sublattice can be used to break symmetry and create a narrow dispersive band.

**Key observables.** Excited-state population P(t), steady-state population, spectral function A(omega), bound-state residues (Zs), and decay rate (Gamma).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Emitter-bath coupling strength (g), nearest-neighbor hopping (J), emitter detuning (Delta), and engineered bandwidth (W) or on-site potential (V0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis utilizes a weak coupling limit (g << W << J) for certain regimes and assumes a rectangular spectral model to approximate transient dynamics.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the band structure transition from flat to narrow bands, the spectral function A(omega) showing bound states and continua, and the temporal evolution of the emitter population.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the 1D Lieb lattice model, analyzes sublattice-dependent coupling (B vs. C), explores the effect of detuning on emission dynamics, investigates the symmetry-breaking transition to narrow bands, and concludes with scaling laws for engineering quantum emission.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a comprehensive theoretical framework that unifies quantum emission dynamics in one-dimensional Lieb lattices, bridging the gap between ideal flat-band coherence and realistic narrow-band dissipation. By coupling an emitter to sublattices with finite flat-band wavefunction overlap, we activate a collective, size-independent interaction fundamentally distinct from dispersive-band processes. Controllably breaking lattice symmetry transforms the flat band into a narrow dispersive band, enabling a continuous crossover from non-Markovian to Markovian dynamics governed by the competition between coupling strength and engineered bandwidth. Crucially, we derive explicit scaling laws that provide a quantitative blueprint for tuning spontaneous emission from coherent trapping to Markovian decay. Our work provides a unified framework that connects idealized flat-band physics to emerging narrow-band platforms such as moir$\rm\acute{e}$ photonic crystals, offering a practical toolkit for interpreting experiments and engineering quantum emission in structured photonic environments.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05754"></a>
### [Thermodynamic incompleteness in non-Markovian Majorana transport I: Island dynamics and missing transport statistics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05754v1)

**Authors:** Yang Tian  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05754v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

</details>

</details>

<img src="2605.05754_figures/2605.05754_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the non-Markovian dynamics of a Majorana island do not contain enough information to fully determine the transport statistics of its connected leads. It establishes a thermodynamic completeness criterion and shows that different reservoir configurations can produce identical island-state evolution while yielding different heat and charge noise. This highlights a fundamental loss of information when tracing out environmental degrees of freedom.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental limit on the reconstructibility of open quantum system dynamics, specifically showing that observing the system's internal state is insufficient to infer the full thermodynamic record of its environment.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the non-Markovian dynamics of a Majorana island are sufficient to reconstruct the full thermodynamic transport statistics (charge, heat, and noise) measured in the connected leads.

**Main result.** The author proves that island-state dynamics are thermodynamically incomplete because the memory kernel discards the specific identity of reservoir channels, meaning two devices with identical island relaxation can exhibit different transport noise.

**Method.** The study uses a Schrieffer-Wolff transformation to derive a cotunneling Hamiltonian, a Born memory expansion for the non-Markovian master equation, and the counting field method to track transport statistics.

**Model / system.** A floating superconducting Majorana island in the Coulomb blockade regime, featuring M Majorana zero modes coupled to structured reservoirs via tunneling contacts.

**Key observables.** Charge transfer, heat transfer, charge noise, heat noise, and mixed charge-energy correlations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coulomb charging energy (Ec), gate voltage (ng), cotunneling regime, and Majorana bilinear operators.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to the weak-cotunneling regime and assumes Gaussian reservoirs and analytic counting-field generating functionals.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of thermodynamic incompleteness, defines the Majorana island model in the cotunneling regime, presents a completeness theorem and a geometric/topological criterion for reconstructibility, and concludes with experimental predictions and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the complete knowledge of the non-Markovian island-state dynamics of a floating Majorana island does not, in general, determine the thermodynamic transport statistics measured in the leads. We demonstrate this statement in a Coulomb-blockaded island with $M$ Majorana zero modes coupled to structured reservoirs. In the cotunneling regime, a Schrieffer-Wolff transformation gives reservoir-assisted transitions generated by Majorana bilinears. After the reservoirs are traced out, the island state determines the memory kernel associated with each bilinear, and this is enough to predict all island-state observables within the cotunneling approximation. It is not enough to determine which lead or detector channel supplied the electron, absorbed the electron, or carried the corresponding energy exchange. This is a genuine loss of thermodynamic information, not an error in the island equation. We formulate the result as a thermodynamic completeness criterion: an island memory equation determines a transport observable only when that observable is constant over all assignments of reservoir channels that give the same island memory kernel. The criterion gives a measurable prediction. Two structured-reservoir Majorana devices can have identical island-state tomography and relaxation, but different charge noise measured separately in the leads, heat noise, and mixed charge-energy correlations. The geometry of the projection from reservoir records to island kernels and the topology of the network of tunnel contacts identify which transport information is absent from island-state dynamics.

</details>

**Authors:** Ayan Patra, Manju, Aditi Sen De, Matteo G. A. Paris  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06263v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

The left panel (a) corresponds to Λc = 2 and ω = 1 (Markovian evolution). For right panel (b), Λc = 1 and ω = 7 with ξ = 0.3 and α = 0.1 (Non-Markovian dynamics).</sub>

</details>

The parameters are ξ = 0.3, Λc = 1, ω = 5, β = 0.2, α = 0.025, kBT/¯hΛc = 4, γ = 0.07 and n = 0.4. There exists a time region in which F with β ̸= 0 under Markovian dynamics can be higher than that of non-Markovian evoluion without β. It shows that the Hamiltonian engineering can sometime be more beneficial than the non-Markovian dynamics.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.06263_figures/2605.06263_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5: Classical Fisher information (CFI) as a function of time. The CFI corresponding to homodyne and heterodyne measurements are shown in gray (dashed) and black (dot-dashed), respectively, while the dyne-optimal CFI is depicted by the red (continuous) curve. The dyne-optimal CFI coincides with the maximum of the homodyne and heterodyne curves. The parameters are ω = 2.1, γ = 0.05, n = 0.1, and β = 0.3 −0.5i, with α = i.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how squeezing and environmental memory can be used to improve frequency estimation precision in noisy continuous-variable systems. It demonstrates that squeezing can change the fundamental scaling of precision with time and that non-Markovianity can temporarily recover lost information. The work also identifies the limits of Gaussian measurements in high-squeezing regimes.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a dual-strategy approach (Hamiltonian engineering and environmental control) to combat decoherence, which is highly relevant for developing robust quantum sensors and understanding the metrological utility of non-Markovianity in open quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** How to mitigate the detrimental effects of environmental noise and decoherence on the precision of frequency estimation in continuous-variable quantum systems.

**Main result.** The study finds that Hamiltonian engineering via squeezing can enhance QFI scaling to $t^3$ in the short-time regime, while non-Markovian memory effects can induce information backflow to temporarily restore estimation precision; however, strong squeezing may eventually require non-Gaussian measurements to saturate the QFI.

**Method.** The authors use the Gaussian quantum state formalism, calculating Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) and Classical Fisher Information (CFI) using the first and second moments of the system's state under various master equations.

**Model / system.** A single-mode harmonic oscillator (continuous-variable system) initially in a coherent state, subject to a Hamiltonian that can include squeezing, and interacting with either a Markovian thermal bath or a non-Markovian Quantum Brownian Motion environment.

**Key observables.** Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), Classical Fisher Information (CFI), and the Symmetric Logarithmic Derivative (SLD).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Frequency ($\omega$), squeezing parameter ($eta$), damping rate ($\gamma$), thermal photon number ($n$), and the non-Markovianity ratio ($r = \Lambda_c/\omega$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis primarily focuses on the Gaussian regime and Gaussian measurement strategies (homodyne, heterodyne, and optimized dyne).

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows time-dependent dissipation and diffusion coefficients; Figure 3 compares QFI under unitary, non-Markovian, and Markovian dynamics; Figure 5 shows CFI for different dyne measurements; Figure 6 illustrates the growing gap between QFI and CFI with increasing squeezing; Figure 7 shows the failure of fixed quadrature measurements in non-Markovian regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of frequency estimation in noisy CV systems, develops the mathematical framework for Gaussian states under Markovian and non-Markovian dynamics, analyzes the impact of squeezing on QFI scaling, evaluates the role of environmental memory, and concludes by assessing the optimality of various Gaussian measurement strategies.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum metrology promises precision beyond classical limits, yet environmental noise typically degrades the quantum resources required for such enhancement. In this work, we investigate frequency estimation in noisy continuous-variable systems, focusing on two complementary strategies to mitigate decoherence: Hamiltonian engineering and the exploitation of non-Markovian dynamics. By embedding squeezing directly into the system Hamiltonian, we show that the quantum Fisher information (QFI) may acquire a tunable higher-order time dependence, leading to enhanced sensitivity in the short-time regime. Moving beyond the Markovian approximation, we employ the quantum Brownian motion model to demonstrate that structured environments with finite memory can induce information backflow, temporarily restoring and even improving estimation precision relative to the unitary limit. We further assess the achievability of these bounds via Gaussian measurements, identifying regimes where homodyne, heterodyne, and optimized general-dyne measurements saturate the QFI, and noting that stronger squeezing widens the gap, potentially requiring non-Gaussian measurement strategies. Our results establish that jointly tailoring system Hamiltonian and environmental memory offers a viable route toward robust quantum-enhanced frequency estimation in open systems.

</details>

### [Comparative Study of Potts Machine Dynamics and Performance for Max-k-Cut](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06425v1)

**Authors:** Bjarke Almer Frederiksen, Robbe De Prins, Peter Bienstman  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06425v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **1/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06425_figures/2605.06425_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Time traces for each studied model on a 20-vertex Max-3-Cut problem from the ER50 set. We visualize the evolution of the amplitudes (a1 - d1, f1), phases (a2 - d2, e1), and the cut value (a3 - d3, e2 - f2) of the system. For the amplitude and phase plots, curves of different spins are shown in different colors, while the gray dashed lines indicate the discrete Potts phase values θ = 2πk/q. For cut value plots, the red dashed line indicates the optimum cut value of the problem. For the Reference IM model, an inset shows its time trace from t = 0 to t = 3 to better visualize the initial dynamics. Default parameters listed in the Supp. §S6 are used for each model.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06425_figures/2605.06425_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Relative optimality gap distributions for each model on (a) Max-3-Cut problems and (b) Max-4-Cut problems across GSet benchmark graphs G1-G5. Each model was evaluated using hyperparameters optimized for minimum mean optimality gap. Box plots show the distribution of relative optimality gaps (%) across 100 independent runs per problem-model combination. Lower values indicate better performance, with 0% representing the optimal solution (green horizontal line). The reference Ising machine consistently achieves the smallest optimality gaps, followed by the NEC Potts machine model. See Supp. §S7 for detailed results, including hyperparameter tuning heatmaps for each model and graph,...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.06425_figures/2605.06425_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Success rate distributions for each model on ER50 Max-3-Cut benchmark problems (5–50 vertices, 10 instances per size). Each model was evaluated using hyperparameters optimized for maximum success rate, with 100 independent runs per graph-model combination. Box plots show the distribution of success rates across graphs of each size. The reference IM and NEC model achieve consistently high success rates across all graph sizes, while the q-PDC and q-SHIL models show lower performance, particularly for larger graphs. See Supp. §S6 for hyperparameter sweep ranges.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a systematic benchmark of various Potts Machine models against Ising Machines for solving multi-state optimization problems. It reveals that current Potts Machine dynamics actually underperform compared to binary Ising approaches and identifies the specific dynamical features needed to improve them.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is interesting for researchers in many-body dynamics and non-equilibrium systems because it links the macroscopic optimization performance of complex dynamical systems to microscopic dynamical properties like bifurcation structures and phase discretization.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study evaluates whether Potts Machines (PMs) outperform Ising Machines (IMs) on multi-state combinatorial optimization problems like Max-k-Cut, specifically investigating if native multi-state representation overcomes the overhead of binary encoding.

**Main result.** Surprisingly, the reference Ising Machine outperforms all tested Potts Machines, and this performance gap increases when moving from Max-3-Cut to Max-4-Cut. The authors identify that performance correlates with specific dynamical properties like connected origin-to-solution branches and amplitude homogeneity.

**Method.** The authors use a unified dynamical systems framework of coupled ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and numerical integration (Euler-Maruyama) to simulate adiabatic annealing on GSet and random (ER50) graphs.

**Model / system.** The study compares several models: Non-Equilibrium Condensate (NEC), q-photon downconversion (q-PDC), q-th sub-harmonic injection locking (q-SHIL), a Polynomial PM, and a Sigmoid PM, against a reference Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) model.

**Key observables.** Relative optimality gap, success rate, spin amplitudes, spin phases, and cut values.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Phase discretization strength (gamma), inter-spin coupling strength (beta), linear amplitude gain (alpha), nonlinearity order (n), and annealing rates.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study excludes certain discrete-spin PMs and phase-only models with non-Kuramoto-type coupling; it also notes a Hamiltonian mismatch between the planar Potts model and the target Max-k-Cut problem for k > 3.

**Figures summary.** Figures include time traces of spin evolution, box plots of optimality gaps and success rates across different graph sizes, heatmaps showing hyperparameter sensitivity, and convergence plots for simulation time and integration time steps.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the optimization problem, defines the mathematical framework for various PM and IM models, presents a comparative benchmarking on graph datasets, analyzes the correlation between dynamics and performance, and concludes with a sensitivity analysis of hyperparameters.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Combinatorial optimization problems in logistics, finance, energy, and scheduling routinely involve multi-state decision variables. Ising machines (IMs) require binary expansions (e.g., one-hot encoding) to encode such variables, whereas Potts machines (PMs) represent them natively. By doing so, PMs are expected to outperform IMs on multi-state problems. To the best of our knowledge, no systematic study of PM models has yet assessed whether this expectation holds. We therefore benchmark five representative PMs against a reference IM on Max-3-Cut and Max-4-Cut, using 800-vertex GSet graphs and random graphs of up to 50 vertices. Surprisingly, the reference IM still outperforms every PM, and the IM supremacy increases significantly in going from Max-3-Cut to Max-4-Cut. These results provide clear evidence that current PM dynamics underperform relative to binary approaches, even in regimes where they are presumed advantageous. We provide a way forward by quantifying the underperformance of current PMs, as well as by identifying three dynamical properties that correlate strongly with their performance ranking. Our work stresses the need for more systematic assessments of algorithmic performance in order to guide the design of more effective Potts machines.

</details>

**Authors:** Charlotte Myin, Suropriya Saha, Benoît Mahault  
**Type:** both · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05448v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(2) exhibit unphysical solutions [64]. (b) Homogeneous uniform chiral solution as a function of the noise η for ∆χ = 0.6 (black line in (a)). (c,d) Time series of the uniform (c) and modulated (d) chiral solutions. Parameters are...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that chiral order in nonreciprocal active matter is unstable and breaks down into a state of spatiotemporal chaos driven by topological defects. It reveals that the coupling between density and orientation leads to non-universal scaling exponents that deviate from standard KPZ predictions. This provides new insights into how nonreciprocity modifies the large-scale physics of active systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study explores how broken action-reaction symmetry and nonreciprocal interactions drive non-equilibrium phase transitions and unconventional scaling, which is highly relevant to understanding driven-dissipative many-body dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the stability of emergent chiral order in two-dimensional nonreciprocal flocking mixtures and the origin of non-universal scaling behavior in the resulting defect-chaos phase.

**Main result.** Chiral order is found to be generically unstable due to the proliferation of topological defects, leading to a defect-chaos phase with non-universal scaling exponents driven by density-polarity coupling.

**Method.** The authors combine large-scale agent-based simulations with a coarse-grained hydrodynamic continuum description and Floquet stability analysis.

**Model / system.** A binary mixture of two species of Vicsek-like active particles in a 2D plane, characterized by asymmetric (nonreciprocal) alignment interactions and angular noise.

**Key observables.** Global polarity, phase shift between species, density and orientational correlation functions, and correlation length.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nonreciprocity strength (delta-chi), noise strength (eta), and system size (L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The continuum hydrodynamic description is derived near the ordering threshold and may not be valid far from it; stability is assessed via the eigenvalues of the evolution operator.

**Figures summary.** Phase diagrams in the (delta-chi, eta) plane, snapshots of band phases, time series of global polarities, and plots of correlation functions and scaling exponents.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the nonreciprocal flocking model, presents a phase diagram, develops a continuum theory, performs stability analysis of chiral states, and uses large-scale simulations to characterize the defect-chaos phase and its scaling properties.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that chiral order in two-dimensional nonreciprocal flocking mixtures is generically unstable. Combining large-scale agent-based simulations with a coarse-grained continuum description, we demonstrate that rotating chiral states emerging from antisymmetric couplings are destroyed by the proliferation of topological defects. The resulting dynamics is spatiotemporally chaotic and characterized by a finite correlation length that diverges as nonreciprocity vanishes. On length scales below this cutoff, density and orientational order fluctuations remain scale-free, but the associated scaling exhibits nonuniversal exponents. We attribute this atypical behavior to the coupling between density and order, which causes topological defects to act as persistent sources of nonlinear fluctuations.

</details>

### [Operationally Admissible Post-Quantum Correlations from a Standard Quantum Walk](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05477v1)

**Authors:** Marcos C. de Oliveira  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05477v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

Center: entanglement between the coin (two-dimensional Hilbert space) and the walker position generates an effective low-dimensional Schmidt subspace within the large position Hilbert space. Right: two measurement regimes. Schmidt-aligned observables access this subspace and reveal admissible post-quantum CHSH violations, while coarse-grained position measurements fail to resolve...</sub>

</details>

Markers show the discrete maximizers obtained from the directional scan at each sampled ∥r∥, and the solid curve is a guide to the eye connecting these points. Horizontal reference lines indicate the classical CHSH bound |S| = 2 (dashed), Tsirelson’s bound |S| = 2 √</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>FIG. 3. Minimum joint probability mina,b,i,j p(a, b|i, j) for the Schmidt-aligned benchmark, demonstrating admissibility of the post-quantum violations. The dashed horizontal line indicates the boundary min p = 0.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

The distribution remains confined to |x| ≤T and exhibits the familiar interference-broadened profile of a standard Hadamard coined quantum walk.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05477_figures/2605.05477_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Final-time walker position distribution P(x) at T = 60 for the coarse-grained witness of Table I. The distribution remains confined to |x| ≤T and shows the familiar interference-broadened profile of a standard Hadamard coined quantum walk.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that a standard quantum walk can generate correlations that exceed the Tsirelson bound if the initial coin state is prepared using an extended, non-positive framework. However, it reveals that these post-quantum features are practically hidden from observers using realistic, coarse-grained position measurements as the system evolves. The work highlights a fundamental gap between the existence of post-quantum resources and their operational accessibility.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a controlled theoretical platform to study the boundary between quantum and post-quantum regimes and highlights how measurement resolution and structure can fundamentally limit the observation of non-classical resources in many-body-like dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether post-quantum correlations (violating the Tsirelson bound) can be operationally accessed within a standard quantum walk framework and distinguishing between their existence in a state versus their accessibility under realistic measurement constraints.

**Main result.** The study finds that while standard quantum walk dynamics can support post-quantum correlations via an extended coin preparation, these correlations are strongly suppressed and become inaccessible under physically natural, coarse-grained position measurements as the walk time increases.

**Method.** The authors use a numerical approach involving randomized searches over preparation and measurement directions, comparing optimized Schmidt-aligned measurements against coarse-grained position binning, and employing a quasiprobability reconstruction to emulate non-positive coin preparations.

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional, discrete-time, coined quantum walk on a lattice (Z) consisting of a two-level coin and a high-dimensional walker subspace, utilizing standard Hadamard coin and nearest-neighbor shift dynamics.

**Key observables.** CHSH Bell parameter (S), joint probabilities p(a, b|i, j), and Schmidt coefficients.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bloch vector magnitude of the coin preparation (||r||), walk time (T), and position binning threshold ratio (x0/T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes that physical consistency can be maintained via admissibility (non-negative probabilities) and no-signaling at the level of observed statistics, even if the underlying state preparation is non-positive.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 summarizes the separation between existence and accessibility; Figure 2 shows CHSH values exceeding Tsirelson's bound for Schmidt-aligned measurements; Figure 3 shows the admissibility margin; Figure 6 and 7 demonstrate the suppression of Bell violations in coarse-grained measurements as walk time increases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of post-quantum correlations, defines the extended preparation framework and quantum walk model, presents a benchmark using optimized measurements to show existence, then explores the accessibility gap using coarse-grained measurements, and concludes by discussing the scaling effects and experimental implications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

It is shown that a standard one-dimensional coined discrete-time quantum walk can generate operationally admissible post-quantum correlations in a coin-position Bell scenario, without any modification of its unitary nearest-neighbor dynamics. Post-quantum features enter exclusively through an extended operational preparation of the coin, described by a complementarity-violating Hermitian trace-one operator, while physical consistency is enforced solely at the level of observable statistics via admissibility and no-signaling. The extended preparation admits an experimental emulation through a two-component quasiprobability reconstruction over physical coin states, at the price of an increased sampling overhead. The walk-generated coin-position entanglement can support CHSH values exceeding Tsirelson's bound, even though the walk dynamics remains fully standard. We also show that physically natural coarse-grained position measurements can render such post-quantum correlations operationally inaccessible, strongly suppressing observable Bell violations. The purpose here is to contrast the separation between the existence of post-quantum behavior and its accessibility under realistic measurement constraints.

</details>

### [Learning Gaussian optical states with quantum computers](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05325v1)

**Authors:** Spencer Dimitroff, John Kallaugher, Ashe Miller, Mohan Sarovar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05325v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `Tavis-Cummings & cavity-many-emitter` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<img src="2605.05325_figures/2605.05325_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to use quantum computers to efficiently learn the properties of multi-mode Gaussian optical states. It demonstrates that by mapping optical information onto qubits, one can achieve a sample complexity that scales logarithmically with the number of modes and more efficiently with energy than classical methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** It bridges quantum information theory with quantum optics by providing a rigorous scaling analysis for using quantum hardware to perform high-precision sensing and imaging of electromagnetic fields.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the sample complexity required to learn all parameters (means and covariances) of an n-mode Gaussian optical state using a quantum computer.

**Main result.** The authors establish that the number of required state copies scales logarithmically with the number of modes n, providing a polynomial advantage in energy dependence compared to existing CV classical shadow protocols.

**Method.** The protocol uses a Jaynes-Cummings interaction to transduce optical modes into a qubit register, followed by an iterative algorithm and classical shadows to extract Gaussian moments from Pauli measurements.

**Model / system.** An n-mode continuous-variable bosonic electromagnetic field interacting with an n-qubit register via a local Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** One- and two-qubit Pauli observables (e.g., X, Y, Z, XX, YY) on the transduced qubit register.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of modes (n), maximum energy per mode (E_max), target additive error (epsilon), and failure probability (delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The optical state is Gaussian; the Jaynes-Cummimng interaction is kept in the perturbative regime; and the transduction process is ideal (ignoring experimental noise).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the transduction schematic; Figure 2 illustrates the inductive proof for state selection; Figure 3 demonstrates the rapid convergence of the error reduction algorithm.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Gaussian state learning, defines the QCIS transduction model via Jaynes-Cummings interaction, provides a mathematical reduction of the n-mode problem to a two-mode problem, presents an iterative algorithm for parameter extraction, and concludes with a complexity analysis comparing the results to CV classical shadows.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent results have established dramatic advantages in learning properties of quantum states when a quantum computer is available to process or jointly measure multiple copies of the unknown quantum state. Learning tasks can be accomplished with exponentially fewer copies of the state when compared to optimized classical learning strategies that are restricted to measuring one copy of the state at a time. While these results were established in abstract settings and for artificial learning tasks, they motivate the application of quantum computers to imaging and sensing of weak electromagnetic fields since these settings are ultimately concerned with the learning of unknown quantum states. In this work we apply these new results in quantum learning to the problem of learning Gaussian states of the electromagnetic field, which are germane since they describe most fields used in imaging and sensing. In order to connect with quantum learning theory, we consider the transduction of an $n$-mode Gaussian state into a register of qubits on a quantum computer followed by optimized measurements on these qubits to extract the parameters defining the original Gaussian state. We rigorously bound the number of copies of the Gaussian state required to achieve worst-case additive error in parameter estimates. The scaling of this bound with $n$ is exponentially better than naïve strategies for characterizing Gaussian states and matches recently derived bounds for characterization of Gaussian states using continuous-variable (CV) classical shadows. In addition, our bound has a polynomially better dependence on the energy of the multimode Gaussian state compared to the CV shadows protocol.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05345"></a>
### [Squeezed Vibrational States in Superfluid Helium](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05345v1)

**Authors:** Lev A. Melnikovsky  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05345v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **1/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05345_figures/2605.05345_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Wigner quasi-probability distribution in the phase space (ϕ, ρ). The thermal state in (a) is stationary under the circular flow of free harmonic oscil- lator evolution (shown in red). The pump pulse “skews” the distribution such that it is no longer circularly symmetric. Birefringence, given by the average ρ2 , is unchanged by the pump itself, but oscillates subsequently since the squeezed state (b) is no longer stationary.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes that ultrafast laser pulses create squeezed states of quasiparticle pairs in superfluid helium. It shows that the observed birefringence oscillations arise from the interference of multiple vibrational modes, including rotons and maxons.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates a macroscopic manifestation of quantum squeezing in a condensed matter system and provides a framework for understanding how ultrafast pulses drive non-equilibrium quantum states in many-body systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Explaining the origin of ultrafast birefringence oscillations and the nonzero initial phase observed in superfluid helium following intense femtosecond laser pulse excitation.

**Main result.** The pump pulse induces an anisotropic squeezed quantum state of quasiparticle pairs, where the observed birefringence signal is a superposition of contributions from various modes (rotons, maxons, and Pitaevskii's plateau).

**Method.** A quantum mechanical treatment of the permittivity tensor operator and the evolution of density fluctuation operators using Heisenberg equations of motion and the stationary-phase approximation.

**Model / system.** Bulk liquid 4He below the lambda point, modeled as a gas of polarizable quasiparticles (rotons, maxons, and Pitaevskii's plateau) treated as harmonic oscillators under a pump electric field.

**Key observables.** Ultrafast optical birefringence oscillations, polarization change of a time-delayed probe pulse, and local refractive-index modulation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pump intensity ~ 10^12 W/cm^2, pulse duration ~ 70 fs, roton energy ~ 17 K, and low-temperature regime where signal is dominated by zero-point vibrations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The pump electric field is treated classically, the system starts in thermal equilibrium, and the short-pulse approximation is used for the evolution operator.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a Wigner quasi-probability distribution in phase space, comparing a stationary thermal state (circular symmetry) to a skewed squeezed state produced by the pump.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the problem of birefringence oscillations, introduces a Hamiltonian model for quasiparticle squeezing, applies mathematical approximations (stationary-phase, short-pulse) to calculate the response, and analyzes the resulting signal and phase.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ultrafast birefringence oscillations observed in superfluid helium provide evidence for anisotropic quantum squeezing of quasiparticle pairs. The measured response is a superposition of contributions from all vibrational modes, with dominant contributions from rotons, maxons, and Pitaevskii's plateau. The nonzero initial phase follows naturally from multimode interference.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Tight Contraction Rates for Primitive Channels under Quantum $f$-Divergences](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06452v1)

**Authors:** Matthew Simon Tan, Marco Tomamichel, Ian George  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06452v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

</details>

</details>

</details>
