<img src="2605.06452_figures/2605.06452_page5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 5</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.06452_figures/2605.06452_page6.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Low-resolution page preview, page 6</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper establishes new, tight upper bounds on the rate at which quantum channels contract different measures of distinguishability toward a stationary state. By relating f-divergences to chi^2-divergences, the authors provide a framework to calculate exact contraction rates for specific families of quantum divergences. This advances the understanding of information loss in non-commutative quantum processes.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it provides fundamental bounds on how quickly quantum information/distinguishability decays under the influence of noise and environmental interaction.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the tightest possible asymptotic contraction rates for primitive quantum channels under various quantum f-divergences and establishing conditions for the tightness of these bounds in a non-commutative setting.

**Main result.** The authors prove that the asymptotic contraction rate of a primitive channel to its stationary state is upper bounded by the SDPI constant of any non-commutative chi^2_g-divergence, and establish that this bound is tight under quantum detailed balance conditions.

**Method.** The study employs Taylor expansions of f-divergences near the identity to establish a local reverse Pinsker inequality, utilizes spectral analysis of channel eigenvalues, and applies the concept of quantum f-detailed balance.

**Model / system.** The mathematical model consists of primitive, time-homogeneous quantum channels acting on density matrices in a finite-dimensional Hilbert space, assuming a unique, full-rank stationary state.

**Key observables.** Strong Data-Processing Inequality (SDPI) constants, quantum f-divergences, and the asymptotic contraction rate of the channel.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The asymptotic regime (n approaching infinity), the primitivity of the channel, and the smoothness (thrice continuous differentiability) of the function f.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The quantum channels must be primitive, the stationary state must be full-rank, and the function f must be thrice continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of 1.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing a local reverse Pinsker inequality, moves to proving general upper bounds for asymptotic contraction rates, provides conditions for the tightness of these bounds using detailed balance, and concludes with specific applications to Petz, Matsumoto, and Hirche-Tomamichel f-divergences.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Data-processing inequalities capture the phenomenon that two probability distributions can only become less distinguishable under any common post-processing. For more fine-grained inequalities, one turns to strong data-processing inequality (SDPI) constants, which give the strongest inequalities for a given channel and reference state for a fixed measure of distinguishability. These quantities have been used to quantify the rate at which time-homogeneous Markov chains contract towards a fixed point both in the classical and quantum setting. In this work, we establish that quantum $f$-divergences satisfy a local reverse Pinsker inequality, which implies the asymptotic contraction rate of a primitive channel to its stationary state is upper bounded by the SDPI constant of any non-commutative $χ^2$-divergence. Using quantum-detailed balance, we establish a sufficient condition for these bounds to be tight. Finally, we apply these results to Petz, Matsumoto, and Hirche-Tomamichel $f$-divergences, establishing new and strengthening previously known results.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05299"></a>
### [Universal Neural Propagator: Learning Time Evolution in Many-Body Quantum Systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05299v1)

**Authors:** Zihao Qi, Christopher Earls, Yang Peng  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05299v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05299_figures/2605.05299_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Illustration of the Universal Neural Propagator (UNP) framework. For any driving protocol ˆH(t), the corresponding time-evolution quantum propagator ˆU(t) is predicted by a neural-network-operator hybrid UNP architecture. The learned propagator can be further applied to track time evolution starting from arbitrary initial states in a given computational basis. A single, unified UNP model allows for transferring across an infinite-dimensional functional space of driving protocols and an exponentially large space of initial states, predicting time dependent observables at any time in the training interval.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05299_figures/2605.05299_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. The architecture diagram of the UNP model. Driving protocols H(t) are processed by a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) and distilled into a set of context tokens M(t) that encode the temporal structure of driving. The Transformer backbone of UNP processes this information through cross attention to assign an amplitude p(σ; t) and phase ϕ(σ; t) for each spin configuration σ in the doubled space. The phase and amplitude are combined to produce the matrix element Uαβ(t).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05299_figures/2605.05299_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Evaluation of the performance of Universal Neural Propagator for a driven TFIM with system size 4 × 4. Each row corresponds to a different computational-basis product state and a different time-dependent driving protocol. The four columns show: the initial spin configuration, the driving protocol, the state fidelity F(t) between the exactly evolved state and the UNP-predicted evolution, and a representative local observable, respectively. (a) Performance on an in-distribution driving field. (b, c) UNP predictions for out-of-distribution but experimentally realistic driving protocols: a tanh ramp-up and a Gaussian pulse. The same UNP model is used across all panels without...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05299_figures/2605.05299_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Time-evolution starting from the GHZ state (Eq. 34). Because the UNP predicts a propagator, the time evolution of a coherent superposition is obtained by linearly combining the individually propagated computational-basis components. The driving protocols are (a) in the training distribution; (b) a tanh ramp-up; (c) a Gaussian pulse, and are different from the ones in Fig. 3. Agreement with exact evolution from this state shows not only the accuracy of individual columns of eU(t), but also their relative phase structure.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05299_figures/2605.05299_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Mean absolute error (MAE) of local observables, be- fore and after fine-tuning the propagator. (a): MAE of the x-magnetization ⟨X⟩; (b): MAE of the energy per spin ⟨E⟩. Both quantities are averaged over 50 random z-basis prod- uct states that have not been seen during fine-tuning. Fine- tuning using only a small number of initial states consistently improves the propagator’s performance across the entire time interval.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper presents the Universal Neural Propagator (UNP), a machine learning model that learns to predict the time-evolution of many-body quantum systems. Unlike previous methods, it can simultaneously generalize to new driving protocols and new initial states. This approach enables efficient, scalable simulation of driven quantum matter beyond the limits of exact diagonalization.

**Why it may be interesting.** It proposes a paradigm shift from learning individual quantum states to learning the underlying operators, providing a foundation model approach that could be extended to dissipative dynamics via Liouville-space superoperators.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Existing neural network approaches for quantum dynamics are limited to transferring across either Hamiltonians or initial states, but not both simultaneously, requiring new simulations to be re-performed if parameters change.

**Main result.** The introduction of the Universal Neural Propagator (UNP), which achieves simultaneous transferability across driving protocols and initial states, maintaining high accuracy even for system sizes beyond exact diagonalization.

**Method.** A self-supervised hybrid architecture combining a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) to process driving protocols and an autoregressive Transformer to predict the time-evolution propagator in a doubled Hilbert space.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional driven transverse-field Ising model on a square lattice with time-dependent longitudinal and transverse fields.

**Key observables.** State fidelity, local magnetization (z and x directions), energy per spin, and ZZ-correlators.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System sizes of 4x4 and 6x6; driving protocols modeled via random Fourier series; training on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes general initial states can be expressed as linear combinations of computational basis states; scalability is limited by VRAM for larger system sizes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the UNP framework; Figure 2 details the FNO-Transformer architecture; Figure 3 demonstrates accuracy and transferability on 4x4 systems; Figure 4 shows fine-tuning efficiency; Figure 5 illustrates error reduction after fine-tuning; Figure 6 compares 6x6 results with tDMRG.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the limitation of single-trajectory simulations, presents the UNP architecture (FNO and Transformer), describes the self-supervised training objective based on the Schrödinger equation, validates the model via 2D Ising model benchmarks (accuracy, transferability, and scalability), and discusses future extensions to open quantum systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Conventional approaches to simulating quantum many-body dynamics produce a single trajectory: if the Hamiltonian or the initial state is changed, the computation must be re-performed. Recent efforts toward foundation models have begun to address this limitation, yet existing methods transfer across either Hamiltonians or initial states, but not both. In this work, we introduce the Universal Neural Propagator (UNP), a single, unified model that learns the functional mapping from driving protocols to time-evolution propagators. Trained in an entirely self-supervised way, a single UNP model predicts dynamics across a function space of driving protocols and an exponentially large Hilbert space of initial states simultaneously. We benchmark on a two-dimensional driven Ising model and demonstrate the UNP's accuracy and transferability across product and entangled initial states, as well as for both in- and out-of-distribution driving protocols. The UNP remains accurate at system sizes beyond exact diagonalization, and can be efficiently fine-tuned across all initial states using observable data. By shifting the object of learning from quantum states to operators, this work opens a route toward transferable simulation of driven quantum matter.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper investigates the probability of finding an empty region in a free fermion gas at finite temperature. It discovers a sharp phase transition in the scaling of this probability at a universal critical temperature, driven by a change in the density profile of an associated effective Coulomb gas.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides exact analytical results for the statistics of fermion density fluctuations, which is directly relevant to understanding the stability and correlations in ultracold Fermi gases and the emergence of macroscopic gaps in quantum systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Characterizing the crossover and phase transition in the counting statistics of free fermions between high-temperature (classical/Poissonian) and low-temperature (quantum) regimes, specifically focusing on the hole probability.

**Main result.** The authors identify a sharp transition of order 3/2(d+1) at a universal critical value u_c = 2/pi, associated with the emergence of a macroscopic gap in the optimal density of an effective Coulomb gas.

**Method.** The problem is mapped to an effective Coulomb gas in momentum space and solved using Fredholm determinants, angular decomposition for d >= 2, and numerical extrapolation of finite-size corrections.

**Model / system.** A system of non-interacting (free) fermions in d dimensions (d=1, 2, 3) in a grand canonical ensemble, described by a determinantal point process with a kernel related to the Fermi factor.

**Key observables.** Hole probability P(R, T), scaling function Phi_d(u), number variance, and entanglement entropy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension d, temperature T, Fermi momentum k_F, radius R, and the dimensionless scaling variable u = 2TR/k_F.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is non-interacting; the analysis focuses on the large radius limit (z -> infinity); the transition is analyzed within the context of the Coulomb gas mapping.

**Figures summary.** Plots of the scaling function Phi_d(u) for different dimensions, the scaled density profile and gap size, and numerical error analysis showing the effectiveness of the z^-2 extrapolation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of hole probability, defines the physical model and regimes, develops the analytical Coulomb gas mapping and scaling functions, provides numerical verification using Fredholm determinants, and discusses the transition and error analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In a free Fermi gas at temperature $T$ much higher than the Fermi temperature one expects that the fluctuations of the number of particles in a given region has Poissonian/classical statistics. On the other hand at low temperature the Pauli exclusion principle leads to non trivial counting statistics. It is of great interest from a theoretical and experimental point of view to characterize the crossover between these two limits. Here we focus on the hole probability $P(R,T)$, i.e. the probability that a region of size $R$ is devoid of particles, in dimension $d$, and on the case of a spherical region of large radius $R$. We show that at low temperature it takes the scaling form $P(R,T)\sim \exp\big[-(k_F R)^{d+1}Φ_d(u=2R\,T/k_F)\big],$ where $k_F$ is the Fermi momentum. By mapping the problem to an effective Coulomb gas, we compute exactly the scaling function $Φ_d(u)$ in any dimension. Remarkably, it exhibits a transition of order $\tfrac{3}{2}(d+1)$ at the universal critical value $u_c=2/π$, signaling a sharp change in the mechanism of rare fluctuations, associated with the emergence of a macroscopic gap in the optimal density of the associated Coulomb gas. Our analytical predictions are supported by precise numerical evaluations of the corresponding Fredholm determinants.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper proposes a two-layer quantum computing architecture that eliminates the need for costly fermionic swap operations when simulating the Fermi-Hubbard model. While this significantly reduces circuit depth, the authors identify that the parallel execution of rotation synthesis creates a new scalability bottleneck. The study provides detailed resource estimates for a regime that is classically intractable.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete architectural blueprint for simulating strongly correlated systems using a hybrid spin-photon platform, specifically addressing the practical overheads of fault-tolerant error correction and gate synthesis.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Estimating the quantum computing resources and optimizing the architecture for fault-tolerant simulation of the 2D Fermi-Hubbard model.

**Main result.** A biplanar SPOQC architecture reduces the depth of Trotter steps by eliminating fermionic swap operations, though rotation synthesis becomes a scalability bottleneck due to exponential failure probabilities in parallelized settings.

**Method.** Architectural co-design using a biplanar honeycomb Floquet code, circuit-level noise modeling, and Clifford+T rotation synthesis analysis.

**Model / system.** A Spin-Optical Quantum Computing (SPOQC) architecture using quantum dot spin qubits and photon-mediated interactions, applied to the 2D Fermi-Hubbard model.

**Key observables.** Logical error rates (spacelike and timelike), T-count, circuit depth, and total runtime/qubit count.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice size L=8, interaction strength U/t=8, physical error rate p=0.01, and 1% diamond norm error budget.

**Assumptions / limitations.** All error sources are treated within a single 1% diamond norm budget; photon loss is treated as a heralded error; rotation synthesis failures are analyzed in a parallelized context.

**Figures summary.** Visualizations of the honeycomb code and biplanar layout, spacetime diagrams for lattice surgery, ternary plots of error contributions, and plots of logical error rates versus physical qubit count.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the biplanar SPOQC architecture, details the Fermi-Hublarb mapping and Trotterization, describes the error model and noise parameters, presents resource estimation results, and concludes with an analysis of rotation synthesis bottlenecks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We estimate the cost of simulating the two-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model on a biplanar spin-optical quantum computing (SPOQC) architecture. Qubits are encoded in the honeycomb Floquet code, and we use a circuit-level noise model with explicit timings for each native physical operation. We benchmark lattice surgery and magic state preparation within each plane, and transversal CNOT gates between corresponding logical qubits across planes. We compile a plaquette-based Trotterization of the time evolution operator, mapping the two spin sectors of the Fermi-Hubbard model onto two physical planes. This architectural co-design eliminates fermionic swap operations and reduces the depth of each Trotter step to $4t_{\mathrm{synth}} + 90$ logical timesteps, where $t_\mathrm{synth}$ is the logical timestep cost of arbitrary-angle rotations, compared to $6t_\mathrm{synth} + 354$ in prior single-plane compilations. All error sources - algorithmic (Trotter), logical noise, magic state infidelity, and rotation synthesis - are treated jointly within a single 1% diamond norm budget. For an $L\times L$ lattice with hopping amplitude $t$ and on-site interaction strength $U$, setting $L=8$ and $U/t=8$, we estimate a total runtime of approximately $2$ hours using $1.35\times 10^6$ physical qubits. We find that fallback-based rotation synthesis methods become a scalability bottleneck: the probability that all $L^2$ parallel rotations succeed on the first attempt vanishes exponentially with system size, causing the failure branch to dominate the expected runtime already at moderate $L$.

</details>

**Authors:** Surachate Limkumnerd, Panat Phanthaphanitkul

Panel (a) compares selected Weyl moments and checks that the plotted evolution is not being di- agnosed only through a final L2 norm. Panels (b) and (c) monitor total variation and sign cancellation, which are the quantities most sensitive to artificial damping, clipping, or hidden loss of signed weight. Panel (d) re- peats the residual-activity accounting used in Fig. 3; this is the same signed activity entering the residual diagnos- tic χQ(t), up to the choice of norm and normalization. Together with the small normalization and boundary er- rors quoted above, these...</sub>

</details>

<sub>FIG. 4. Signed-residual reconstruction of the quartic Wigner evolution. Classical carrier transport alone gives a final Wigner error of order 10−2, while the classical-plus-signed-residual update reduces the error to order 10−5 for the balanced benchmark. No damping, clipping, smoothing, diffusion coefficient, or fitted Langevin noise is introduced.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper addresses the failure of positive Langevin-type methods to capture exact quantum dynamics in anharmonic potentials. It introduces a method to reconstruct the Wigner function using classical trajectories with signed weights to account for quantum interference. This allows for an exact stochastic representation of the Wigner-Moyal equation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous framework for Monte Carlo-style simulations of quantum phase-space dynamics, offering a way to handle the sign problem by isolating non-classicality into a specific, manageable residual term.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The impossibility of representing exact Wigner-Moyal dynamics using a positive, second-order Fokker-Planck/Langevin process for non-quadratic potentials.

**Main result.** The authors propose a weighted phase-space path representation where classical Hamiltonian flow serves as a carrier, and quantum corrections are captured via signed weights or branching events, effectively reconstructing the Wigner function as a weighted empirical measure.

**Method.** A stochastic representation using a weighted empirical measure, splitting the Wigner-Moyal generator into a classical Liouville part and a signed Moyal residual using the Hahn-Jordan decomposition.

**Model / system.** Quantum phase-space dynamics governed by the Wigner-Moyal equation, specifically tested using harmonic (quadratic) and quartic (anharmonic) oscillators.

**Key observables.** Weyl-ordered expectation values, Born density, Moyal residual, interference parity (A_sign), and magnitude factor (A_mag).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Planck's constant (hbar), mass (m), and the anharmonicity parameter (lambda).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the Weyl transform/correspondence to define the target phase-space object.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a null test with the harmonic oscillator where error is at the numerical floor; Figure 2 demonstrates the breakdown of classical transport in a quartic potential; Figure 5 compares Weyl moments between exact and classical-carrier evolution.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the incompatibility of positivity and exact Wigner dynamics, proposes a weighted stochastic reconstruction method, develops a forward-reverse path identity, and validates the approach via numerical benchmarks with harmonic and quartic oscillators.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

A quantum state can be written in phase space, but the resulting object is not generally the probability density of a positive stochastic process on ordinary phase space. We spell this out for Wigner dynamics. If a positive phase-space process is required only to reproduce the Born density after integrating over momentum, the requirement fixes only an integrated current; the local drift and diffusion remain underdetermined. If one instead requires all Weyl-ordered expectation values, the phase-space object is fixed to be the Wigner function. For non-quadratic potentials the Wigner--Moyal generator contains higher-order, signed momentum-transfer terms, so it is not the Fokker--Planck generator of a positive Brownian diffusion. The exact Wigner function must therefore be reconstructed, in a stochastic representation, as a weighted empirical measure \[ \FW(\z,t)=\E_{\Pp}[W_tδ(\z-\z_t)], \qquad \z=(q,p), \] rather than the unweighted density of sampled carrier trajectories. With classical Hamiltonian flow as the carrier, all nonclassical correction beyond classical transport sits in the Moyal residual and can be represented by signed weights or branching events. The same split defines a residual diagnostic that vanishes for quadratic Hamiltonians and measures what classical carrier transport misses in anharmonic dynamics. The formulation also gives a forward--reverse relation for signed Wigner path measures. The ratio of forward and reversed contributions separates into a positive magnitude factor and a sign factor. This sign records the parity of the Wigner interference contribution; it is not a thermodynamic entropy production.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper uses continuous-space quantum Monte Carlo to study bosons in honeycomb and h-BN-like optical lattices. It demonstrates that the standard Bose-Hubbard model fails at higher fillings due to density-assisted tunneling and reveals complex phase diagrams driven by lattice asymmetry. These findings are crucial for the accurate modeling of ultracold atoms in hexagonal geometries.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides critical insights into how continuous-space effects like density-assisted tunneling break the standard tight-binding paradigm in hexagonal geometries, which is essential for designing accurate experiments with ultracold atoms in graphene-like lattices.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the quantum phase diagrams of ultracold bosons in hexagonal and h-BN-like optical potentials and determining the validity limits of the standard Bose-Hubbard model.

**Main result.** The study finds that density-assisted tunneling significantly suppresses higher-order Mott insulator lobes, and a rich phase diagram with various sublattice occupations emerges in asymmetric h-BN-like lattices.

**Method.** Continuous-space exact diagonalization for single-particle band structures and worm-algorithm quantum path-integral Monte Carlo (PIMC) for many-body phase diagrams.

**Model / system.** A 2D gas of identical spinless bosons with repulsive contact interactions in hexagonal (honeycomb) and asymmetric (h-BN) optical lattices, modeled via a continuous-space Hamiltonian and an extended Bose-Hubbard model.

**Key observables.** Local particle density, sublattice occupations (nA, nB), compressibility, superfluid fraction, and superfluid phase-space density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice depth (V0), geometric phase (phi_g), interaction strength (g), chemical potential (mu), and tunneling amplitudes (J, J', J_tilde).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interaction is modeled as a contact (s-wave) interaction, and the evaluation of density-assisted tunneling uses a mean-field approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows lattice geometry and vectors; Figure 2 displays single-particle band dispersions; Figure 4 presents PIMC results for density and superfluidity; Figure 7 illustrates the evolution of the lattice potential with geometric phase; Figure 8 shows the relative correction from density-assisted tunneling.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the hexagonal lattice geometry, presents single-particle band structure calculations via exact diagonalization, details the many-body PIMC simulations, analyzes the phase diagrams for symmetric and asymmetric cases, quantifies deviations from the Bose-Hubbard model, and concludes with the impact of density-assisted tunneling.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hexagonal optical lattices, emulating graphene and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) structures, provide a versatile platform for exploring strongly correlated quantum matter. Using continuous-space exact diagonalization and quantum Monte Carlo simulations, we investigate the phase diagrams of ultracold bosons in honeycomb and h-BN lattices. For the honeycomb lattice, we find significant deviations from the standard Bose-Hubbard model even for strong lattice amplitudes. We observe suppressed Mott insulator lobes and the absence of higher-order insulating phases, attributed to strong density-assisted tunneling effects. In the h-BN case, a rich phase diagram emerges, featuring multiple Mott lobes with various sublattice occupations, driven by the interplay of lattice asymmetry, interactions, and particle filling. Our results highlight the necessity of continuous-space treatments for capturing the full complexity of bosonic quantum phases in hexagonal geometries, paving the way for experimental realizations with ultracold atoms and further theoretical work.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how vertical electric fields can switch electron localization between different sites in MoSe2/WS2 moire superlattices. By tuning the field, the researchers demonstrate control over interlayer charge-transfer states and predict the emergence of various correlated charge-ordered states. This work provides a comprehensive framework for using optical probes to study strongly correlated physics in moire heterostructures.

**Why it may be interesting.** The realization of a highly tunable Fermi-Hubbard model on a honeycomb lattice via electric field control of charge-transfer states provides a platform for studying many-body dynamics and emergent topological or ferroelectric phases.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the nature of emergent optical transitions and the mechanism of electron localization and interlayer charge-transfer in electron-doped MoSe2/WS2 moire superlattices under vertical electric fields.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that vertical electric fields can precisely control interlayer electron localization, switching electrons between M and W sites to realize a tunable Fermi-Hubbard model with emergent charge-ordered states.

**Method.** The work combines experimental optical reflection spectroscopy and Magnetic Circular Dichroism with large-scale first-principles DFT, GW-BSE calculations using the PUMP method, and Monte Carlo simulations.

**Model / system.** Angle-aligned MoSe2/WS2 heterobilayers (moire superlattices) in H-type (60-degree) and R-type (0-degree) configurations, modeled as a Fermi-Hubbard model on an effective honeycomb lattice with tunable band alignment.

**Key observables.** Reflection contrast (RC) spectra, Magnetic Circular Dichroism (MCD) signals, trion intensities (LET, HET, EXT), and electric-field susceptibility (alpha).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electron doping level (n/n0), vertical electric field (E), conduction band offset (delta), and onsite Coulomb repulsion (U).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interacting model treats the system as classical by neglecting coherent electron hopping due to the dominance of the Coulomb scale; layer dependence of Coulomb interactions is neglected.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows device structure, charge density modulation, and optical spectra (RC/MCD) for moire excitons and trions; Fig 2 maps the 2D parameter space of doping vs. electric field for charge-transfer processes; Fig 3 illustrates the R-type superlattice properties and doping-field maps.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the moire system, details the experimental and computational methods, presents the identification of emergent moire excitations, demonstrates the electric-field-driven charge-transfer mechanism, and concludes with Monte Carlo predictions of correlated charge-ordered states.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Moiré superlattices formed by transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) heterobilayers provide a versatile platform for studying strongly correlated electronic, excitonic, and topological phenomena in solids. In particular, angle-aligned MoSe$_2$/WS$_2$ heterobilayers, which have a Type-I band alignment at zero vertical electric field, host rich correlated spin and charge physics. Here, combining large-scale first-principles calculations and optical reflection spectroscopy, we report a thorough study of the emergent moiré excitonic states and interlayer charge-transfer states in angle-aligned electron-doped MoSe$_2$/WS$_2$ moiré superlattices. The moiré excitonic states serve as sensitive optical probes to the localization profile of doped electrons. We observe a series of interlayer charge-transfer transitions from n/n$_0$ = 1 to 4 (where n$_0$ denotes the moiré density) when the vertical electric field switches the heterostructure band alignment from Type-I to Type-II. By tuning the vertical electric field, we can precisely control the interlayer electron localization, realizing a Fermi-Hubbard model with a tunable charge-transfer band on an effective honeycomb lattice. Furthermore, Monte Carlo simulation of the doping dependence of the electric-field susceptibility predicts that multiple correlated charge-ordered states appear at both integer and fractional fillings. Our results provide a holistic understanding of the emergent optical excitations and the correlated charge-transfer states in electron-doped MoSe$_2$/WS$_2$ moiré superlattices.

</details>

### [Quantum-classical solvation hydrodynamics: Hamiltonian functionals and dissipation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05658v1)

**Authors:** François Gay-Balmaz, Cesare Tronci  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05658v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

**Summary.** This paper presents a new hydrodynamic theory for modeling how a quantum molecule interacts with a classical liquid solvent. It overcomes the limitations of standard models by including essential physics like backreaction and solvent inertia, providing a mathematically consistent way to study ultrafast chemical dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous mathematical framework for studying open quantum systems coupled to classical environments, specifically addressing how backreaction and dissipation affect quantum decoherence and non-adiabatic dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a consistent mixed quantum-classical hydrodynamic framework to model non-adiabatic solvation, specifically capturing short-time inertial effects, backreaction, and solvent-induced decoherence.

**Main result.** The derivation of a set of coupled hydrodynamic equations that preserve the underlying Hamiltonian and Poisson structures, extending the model from simple Ehrenfest dynamics to a complex Koopman-based framework including dissipation and polarization.

**Method.** The authors use a moment method from kinetic theory applied to the Koopman model, employing a Hamiltonian functional approach and Lie-Poisson brackets to ensure energy balance and the preservation of Casimir invariants.

**Model / system.** A hybrid system consisting of a quantum solute (e.g., NO molecule) interacting with a classical polar solvent (e.g., supercritical Argon), modeled using a Koopman-based hydrodynamic framework with a Marcus local approximation.

**Key observables.** Fluid density, momentum density, quantum density matrix, polarization, angular momentum, and entropy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** hbar (quantum scale), mass, microinertia constant, electric dipole moment, viscosity, and polarization diffusion coefficient.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The solvent is treated as an ideal polar fluid; the model assumes the validity of the Koopman wavefunction approach and uses a cold-fluid ansatz to simplify the hydrodynamic closure.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing a Hamiltonian baseline for nonpolar solvation, progresses to a complex polar model with orientational backreaction, introduces the Koopman model to capture decoherence, and concludes by integrating dissipative terms for viscosity and polarization relaxation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a mixed quantum-classical hydrodynamic framework to model short-time inertial effects in the non-adiabatic evolution of a quantum solute coupled to a classical polar solvent. Drawing upon the work of Burghardt and Bagchi [Chem. Phys. 329 (2006), 343], we employ the Hamiltonian approach to incorporate consistent backreaction and preserve quantum decoherence beyond standard Ehrenfest dynamics. The solvent is treated as an ideal polar fluid and the quantum solute state is correlated to both the position and molecular orientation coordinates of the liquid. This approach retains essential solute-solvent correlations while significantly reducing the computational complexity of previous approaches. We further incorporate dissipative terms to capture both inertial effects and polarization relaxation. After establishing the general setting for non-local dielectric continua, the Marcus local approximation is integrated into the model thereby extending traditional solvation theory to account for collective fluid sloshing on fast timescales.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides an exact mathematical framework to describe how the timing of cellular activation is shaped by the randomness of molecular release. It demonstrates that the variability in the timing and size of release events can lead to qualitatively different activation behaviors even when mean rates are identical. The work also accounts for the effects of vesicle depletion and replenishment on signaling dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The mathematical framework of non-equilibrium, time-asymmetric stochastic processes and the derivation of exact solutions for threshold-crossing in bursty systems are highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding how the stochastic nature of vesicle-mediated signaling, specifically the temporal statistics of arrival events and burst-size variability, shapes the timing of downstream cellular activation.

**Main result.** The authors derive an exact solution for the time-dependent probability distribution of a batch arrival-degradation model, showing that activation kinetics depend on time-asymmetric fluctuations rather than just mean dynamics.

**Method.** The study employs a master equation approach solved via generating functions, recursion relations, and Bell polynomials, alongside a stochastic sampling algorithm for trajectories.

**Model / system.** A general batch arrival-degradation process modeling vesicle-mediated secretion (e.g., neurotransmission) where molecules are released in discrete bursts and subsequently degraded at a rate proportional to the current count.

**Key observables.** Time-dependent occupation probabilities, first-passage times (FPT) to a concentration threshold, mean and variance of molecule counts, and recurrence times.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Arrival rate (kappa), degradation rate (gamma), burst-size distribution (q_m), inter-arrival time statistics (Poisson vs. fixed-interval), and vesicle replenishment rate (xi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary model assumes a Poisson process for arrivals and linear degradation; the extended model assumes a finite number of docking sites with a specific replenishment rate.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the biological context and the stochastic nature of molecule counts; Figure 2 shows simulated probability distributions; Figure 4 compares fixed and Poisson arrival trains; Figure 7 shows the impact of replenishment on steady-state distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the biological problem, derives the exact solution for the master equation using generating functions, extends the model to include vesicle depletion, compares different arrival statistics (Poisson vs. fixed-interval), and concludes with a discussion on biological implications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Vesicle-mediated secretion of ions or molecules is a central mechanism of cellular communication, for example in processes such as neurotransmission or hormone release. These events are inherently stochastic: vesicle fusions lead to bursts of variable sizes, releasing discrete packets of transmitters that are subsequently cleared or degraded. The dynamics break time-reversal symmetry due to the interplay of spontaneous bursts and continuous degradation. Using generating functions and a recursion relation, we derive an exact solution for the full time-dependent probability distribution of a general batch arrival-degradation model. This framework also enables a full analysis of first-passage times to a concentration threshold representing downstream activation. We show that activation kinetics are not determined by mean dynamics alone, but depend sensitively on the temporal statistics of arrival events, batch-size variability, and degradation. In particular, different arrival processes with identical mean rates can lead to qualitatively distinct first-passage behavior, reflecting the role of time-asymmetric fluctuations. We also discuss extensions incorporating vesicle depletion. Our results provide a transparent link between stochastic release dynamics and activation timing in vesicle-mediated signaling.

</details>

### [Emergent conserved quantities via irreversibility](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05934v1)

**Authors:** Alex Blokhuis, Martijn van Kuppeveld, Daan van de Weem, Robert Pollice  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05934v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `scars & prethermalization` **2/5**

The cycle index c = 1 is given next to its three cycle reactions (highlighted in blue). Thus, no cycles break upon merging collinear reactions: ∆c = 0, ∆ℓ= +1, i.e., a co-production conservation law then results from the co-production law Υ = ∆ℓ−∆c, confirming the numerically found CQ3 marks the detection of a genuine conservation law.</sub>

As the number of simulations increases, convergence occurs to the theoretical values (dotted line) of L(1), L(2), L(3). b) Averaged number of gaps [0], [1], [2] as a function of the number of individual simulated realizations, for trimer (n = 3) adsorption on a lattice of size L = 9. Dotted lines correspond to theoretical expectation values L(1), L(2), L(3), to which the simulated averages converge. Note that the conserved quantities here behave as regular conservation laws on the level of deterministic ODEs. Co-production conservation laws do not constrain...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper identifies a mechanism where irreversible reactions create emergent conservation laws that standard structural analysis misses. By introducing the co-production index, the authors provide a theoretical basis for previously unexplained 'anomalous' quantities in chemical and stochastic models.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a new way to identify hidden symmetries and invariants in non-equilibrium systems, which is directly applicable to understanding dissipation and emergent constraints in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty in predicting the total number of observable conserved quantities in complex chemical reaction networks (CRNs) and Markov chains, specifically addressing why standard structural laws undercount them.

**Main result.** The discovery of a 'co-production law' where irreversible reactions and collinear currents lead to emergent, hidden conservation laws and broken cycles, resolving previously unexplained non-integer conservation laws found via machine learning.

**Method.** A mathematical framework involving a 'reaction merging' procedure for proportional currents, the definition of a 'co-production index', and the use of fundamental subspaces of the stoichiometric matrix.

**Model / system.** The theory is applied to Chemical Reaction Networks (CRNs), Markov chains, and specifically to $n$-mer Random Sequential Adsorption (RSA) on a discrete lattice and atmospheric chemistry models.

**Key observables.** Conserved quantities (left nullvectors of the stoichiometric matrix), reaction currents, co-production index, and gap populations in RSA.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Co-production index (Upsilon), number of broken cycles (Lambda), and reaction rate constants.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation assumes mass-action kinetics for CRNs and first-order transitions for the stochastic-to-deterministic link; it also assumes polynomial flow for numerical verification.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows an atmospheric CRN with collinear and cycle reactions; Figure 2 illustrates trimer adsorption steps and lattice configurations; Figure 3 depicts the reaction merging procedure; Figure 4 reconstructs the atmospheric model; Figure 5 shows the convergence of ensemble averages.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of undercounted conservation laws, proposes the reaction merging technique and the co-production law, validates the theory using atmospheric chemistry and RSA models, and extends the framework to stochastic master equations and non-mass-action kinetics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Conserved quantities increasingly underpin the inference of physical models. Recently new conserved quantities have been found in this context, that currently lack an interpretation. Here, we show that irreversible reactions in CRNs and Markov Chains lead to emergent conservation laws and broken cycles. Linearly dependent currents - characterized by the "co-production index" - arise due to irreversible reactions. We derive a law relating conserved quantities, broken cycles, and co-production. This resolves a recent conundrum posed by a machine-discovered candidate for a non-integer conservation law. Our findings introduce heretofore overlooked extensions to a widely used index law for CRNs and Markov Chains that undercounts conservation laws. This furnishes new tools and immediate applications for the inference and analysis of models based on conservation laws.

</details>

**Authors:** Vishvendra S. Poonia  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06008v1>

**Summary.** This paper proposes using the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction as a diagnostic tool to determine if chiral molecules induce spin selectivity through coherent rotation or incoherent filtering. It demonstrates that a non-zero DM interaction would uniquely certify a coherent quantum process. The study shows that this measurement is achievable with current quantum dot technologies using several candidate molecules.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental 'no-go' theorem for spin-dependent transport and proposes a way to use spin-qubit exchange spectroscopy to probe the quantum coherence of molecular interfaces.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Resolving the long-standing controversy of whether Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity (CISS) is a coherent SU(2) spin rotation or an incoherent spin-dependent filtering process.

**Main result.** The paper proves that the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction serves as a definitive coherence order parameter, being non-zero only in the coherent rotation scenario and identically zero in the incoherent filtering scenario.

**Method.** The author uses Schrieffer-Wolff projection to derive an effective spin Hamiltonian and employs Lindblad analysis to show that dephasing does not generate DM interaction.

**Model / system.** The system consists of two electrons in adjacent gate-defined quantum dots coupled via virtual tunneling through a chiral molecular bridge, modeled either as a unitary spin rotator or a non-unitary spin-diagonal barrier.

**Key observables.** Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (D), singlet-triplet gap (delta E_ST), exchange anisotropy ratio (|D|/JH), and spin polarization (P).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rotation angle (theta_C), helix axis (n), exchange energy (J0), and the 10 kHz exchange spectroscopy threshold.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The virtual tunneling time is much shorter than the molecular dephasing time; the tunneling matrix in the incoherent model is assumed to be Hermitian.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 illustrates the three experimental signatures: the angular dependence of the gap, the enantiomer sign-reversal test, and the sensitivity threshold for detecting rotation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the CISS controversy, defines two competing physical models, derives a symmetry theorem for the DM interaction, proposes an experimental protocol using quantum dots, and evaluates the feasibility for specific candidate molecules.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Whether chirality-induced spin selectivity (CISS) reflects coherent SU(2) spin rotation or incoherent spin-dependent filtering is a central unresolved question in molecular spintronics, with implications ranging from asymmetric chemistry to quantum information. We show that these two scenarios are distinguishable by a sharp symmetry criterion on the superexchange interaction mediated by a chiral molecular bridge. Coherent CISS, implemented as a unitary spin rotation of the tunneling electron, generates a giant Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction with ratio |D|/JH up to 3, which is two orders of magnitude beyond intrinsic Rashba spin-orbit coupling in Si/SiGe. Incoherent CISS, represented by any Hermitian (non-unitary but spin-diagonal) tunneling matrix, produces D = 0 identically; we prove this as a structural theorem, reinforced by a Lindblad argument that dissipative spin filtering cannot modify virtual-tunneling-mediated superexchange. The DM interaction thus serves as a coherence order parameter, nonzero only when quantum amplitudes for opposite-spin transmission maintain a fixed relative phase. We derive closed-form angular, enantiomeric, and sensitivity signatures and show that the critical coherent rotation angle lies two orders of magnitude below current transport-inferred values and is accessible to existing 10 kHz exchange spectroscopy in gate-defined quantum dots. Five candidate molecules are predicted to exceed this threshold by one to two orders of magnitude even in a conservative interface-amplification scenario. The proposed measurement converts a long-standing transport controversy into a binary spin-qubit experiment with quantum-amplitude resolution.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.06424"></a>
### [Finite-Time Optimal Control by Noisy Traps](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06424v1)

**Authors:** Luca Cocconi, Henry Alston, Thibault Bertrand  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06424v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06424_figures/2605.06424_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1: Schematic representation of a passive probe controlled by a noisy harmonic trap, here visualised as an intermittent optical tweezer. In the transport scenario, a deterministic pro- tocol translates the trap centre along the coordinate x, while the trap stiffness k(t) fluctuates stochastically and continuously exchanges work with the probe.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06424_figures/2605.06424_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2: Optimal transport with a noisy trap under steady drag- ging. (a) Work per unit distance in an intermittent trap ob- tained from Eq. (12). Here, D = 1, κ = 1 and r = 1. (b) Op- timal protocol duration for unconstrained [Eq. (22)] and con- strained [Eq. (27)] optimal transport, showing the transition to zero-time optimality. Here, D = 1, ¯k = 1 and λT = 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.06424_figures/2605.06424_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 3: Optimal stiffening with a noisy trap. (a) Mean work as a function of the optimisation parameters (c, T), showing convergence in the large-c regime. (b) Optimal protocol du- ration for unconstrained [Eq. (43)] and constrained [Eq. (47)] optimal stiffening, showing the transition to zero-time opti- mality at σk,c = (λT −λ0)/2. Here, D = 1, λ0 = 1, λT = 3 and ϵ = 10−10.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that when a controller is dissipative and violates detailed balance, the traditional preference for infinitely slow (quasistatic) protocols breaks down. It identifies a phase transition where the optimal control duration vanishes above a certain noise threshold. This provides new insights into how non-equilibrium fluctuations in control parameters can fundamentally alter the landscape of optimal transport.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether finite-time optimal control is required in passive systems when the controller itself is dissipative and violates detailed balance.

**Main result.** The study reveals a breakdown of quasistatic optimality where the optimal protocol duration collapses to zero above a critical fluctuation strength in unconstrained transport, though endpoint constraints prevent this collapse.

**Method.** The authors use a variational approach with Euler-Lagrange equations, a Fokker-Planck derivation for joint probability density, and small-T expansions to identify phase transitions.

**Model / system.** A passive Brownian particle in a harmonic trap where the trap stiffness fluctuates stochastically according to a continuous-time Markov chain, representing a dissipative controller.

**Key observables.** Mean work (cost functional), optimal protocol duration (T*), and dissipated work per unit distance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Stiffness fluctuation variance (sigma_k^2), critical variance threshold (sigma_k,c^2), transport velocity (v), and the fast-switching regime.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The stiffness dynamics are modeled as a Markov chain; the system is in the overdamped Langevin regime; and the fast-switching approximation is used to analyze the stiffness dynamics.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows a schematic of a particle in a noisy harmonic trap; Fig 2 illustrates the transition of optimal duration toward zero at a critical noise level and work vs. velocity plots.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of dissipative control, defines the stochastic model and Langevin dynamics, applies a variational method and multiscale expansion, analyzes the zero-time transition under different constraints, and concludes with implications for steady dragging.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The optimal control of passive systems in equilibrium typically favours quasistatic (infinite-time) protocols. We show that a breakdown of quasistatic optimality occurs when the controller itself is dissipative. Concretely, we study a Brownian particle confined by a harmonic trap with stochastically fluctuating stiffness, driven by an external protocol. When these fluctuations violate detailed balance, the probe-controller coupling continuously exchanges work with the system, altering the optimisation landscape. In this regime, optimal protocols are characterised by a finite duration which vanishes above a critical fluctuation strength. This transition can be directly observed in a short-time expansion of the mean work functional. When imposing an endpoint constraint, the transition to zero duration disappears and finite duration protocols remain optimal for all values of the controller fluctuations. These results demonstrate that finite-time optimality can emerge in passive systems under nonequilibrium control.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.06543"></a>
### [A Rayleigh criterion for mechanical instability: inducing activity by chemo-mechanical coupling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06543v1)

**Authors:** Aaron Beyen, Francesco Casini, Christian Maes  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06543v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

</details>

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical framework to understand how chemical energy can be converted into mechanical motion through feedback. It identifies a specific phase-relation criterion, analogous to Rayleigh's criterion for acoustic instability, that predicts when chemical driving leads to negative friction and sustained active motion.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the conditions under which non-equilibrium chemical driving generates mechanical instability, such as negative friction and sustained rotational motion, through chemo-mechanical coupling.

**Main result.** The authors derive a Rayleigh-like criterion for the onset of activity based on the phase relation between entropic and frenetic contributions, showing that negative friction can emerge when the frenetic (time-symmetric) component dominates.

**Method.** The study uses a reduction/integration approach (Born-Oppenheimer-like) to integrate out fast chemical degrees of freedom, applying non-equilibrium linear response theory and singular perturbation theory in the weak coupling limit.

**Model / system.** A Newtonian mechanical probe (underdamped particle) coupled to a chemical bath of $N$ independent Markov jump processes. The coupling is mediated by a potential $U(x, \sigma)$ that influences both the mechanical force and the chemical transition rates.

**Key observables.** Effective friction coefficient (specifically the frenetic and entropic parts), induced mean force (rotational/streaming term), noise amplitude, and stationary velocity/position distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Chemical driving strength (affinity $\Delta\mu$ or $w$), coupling constant $\lambda$, phase angle $\phi$ between potential and reactivity, and the large driving limit $w 	o \infty$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Time-scale separation between fast chemical and slow mechanical dynamics, weak coupling limit ($\lambda \ll 1$), and the assumption of semi-reciprocity and local detailed balance.

**Figures summary.** Plots of the friction coefficient as a function of position and driving strength; velocity distributions showing transitions from Maxwellian to bimodal/asymmetric; and position distributions showing U-shaped profiles in the active regime.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of chemo-mechanical instability, defines the mathematical framework of the coupled system, derives the reduced Langevin dynamics for the probe, analyzes the onset of negative friction and rotational forces using a Rayleigh-like criterion, explores the large driving limit, and concludes with numerical simulations of the nonlinear/saturation regimes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Instabilities in thermodynamic systems are often undesirable, as they can lead to loss of control or even catastrophic behavior. Yet, the same mechanisms can also generate rich nonequilibrium behavior and may play a constructive role in living systems. We introduce a theoretical framework, inspired by Rayleigh's analysis of thermoacoustic instabilities, to study the emergence of mechanical activity. In particular, we derive Rayleigh-like criteria governing the onset of activity and the generation of rotational motion in a slow Newtonian probe coupled to driven chemical processes, described by Markov jump processes. These criteria are expressed in terms of the phase relation between entropic and frenetic contributions, providing a transparent condition for when chemical driving results in sustained rotational or active mechanical motion.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05394"></a>
### [BARFI-Q: Quantum-Enhanced Block Attention Residual Fusion Framework for Multivariate Time-Series Forecasting in Atom Interferometry](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05394v1)

**Authors:** Muhammad Bilal Akram Dastagir, Omer Tariq, Safaa Alqrinawi, Shaikha Al-Naimi, Ahmed Farouk, Saif Al-Kuwari  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05394v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05394_figures/2605.05394_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Representative Transformer-based time-series forecasting models and attention mechanisms, including Vanilla Transformer, Informer, Autoformer, ETSformer, NSTransformer, and Reformer. Most existing models improve forecasting mainly through modified attention, decomposition, or tokenization, while largely preserving standard additive residual propagation [4].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05394_figures/2605.05394_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Overview of the proposed BARFI-Q architecture. The model consists of patch embedding, dual BAR Transformer branches, hierarchical fusion blocks, a quantum feature mapping module, and a forecasting head for future phase-related prediction.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05394_figures/2605.05394_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Structure of the proposed Dual-Branch BAR Transformer module. Each branch contains a normalized temporal attention pathway, rotary temporal encoding, a linear attention kernel, adaptive Block Attention Residual (BAR) aggregation, and a sparse mixture-of-experts feed-forward block. Unlike standard Transformer layers that reuse only the immediately preceding hidden state through fixed additive residuals, BAR adaptively retrieves and reuses informative summaries from all preceding blocks.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05394_figures/2605.05394_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Hierarchical fusion block used in BARFI-Q. Multiple branch-level feature streams are concatenated and projected into a common latent space, followed by multiscale channel attention and spatial attention refinement. The block progressively integrates complementary temporal, channel-wise, and local structural cues before producing the final fused representation.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05394_figures/2605.05394_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Quantum Feature Mapping (QFM) block used in BARFI-Q. The fused latent feature is first projected into a compact latent vector and then processed by multiple quantum feature-mapping heads. Each head encodes the projected representation into parameterized quantum rotations, applies entangling operations, and returns measurement-based features. The resulting measurement maps are aggregated through a residual mixer and refined by a lightweight post-processing block before forecasting.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces BARFI-Q, a hybrid quantum-classical framework designed to forecast complex, multivariate time-series data from atom interferometers. By using quantum feature mapping and an adaptive residual mechanism, the model effectively handles the periodic and heterogeneous nature of interferometric signals. The results show that this approach significantly improves the accuracy of phase reconstruction compared to standard deep learning models.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates a practical application of hybrid quantum-classical machine learning to improve the predictive capabilities of quantum sensors, specifically addressing the non-linear and periodic nature of interferometric signals.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Accurate multivariate time-series forecasting of heterogeneous signals in atom interferometry, specifically addressing phase evolution, fringe dynamics, and complex interactions among multiple sensing sources.

**Main result.** The BARFI-Q framework consistently outperforms strong classical Transformer-based baselines in predicting wrapped residual phase errors, with the quantum-enhanced version providing superior accuracy and stability.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical framework (BARFI-Q) utilizing patch-based embedding, a dual-branch temporal architecture with adaptive block-attention residual aggregation, and a Quantum Feature Mapping (QFM) module for latent enhancement.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a light-pulse atom interferometer used for precision sensing (e.g., gravimetry or inertial navigation), generating multivariate temporal streams of phase, fringe behavior, and control variables.

**Key observables.** Wrapped residual phase error, population ratio, fringe amplitude, and fringe contrast.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Historical window size (L), number of qubits (N), circuit depth (L), and encoding strategy (Angle, Amplitude, or Phase).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on a one-step-ahead forecasting setting and assumes the availability of a real-time classical phase estimate to resolve phase ambiguity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares existing Transformer models; Figure 2 shows the BARFI-Q architecture; Figure 3 details the dual-branch transformer; Figure 6 compares error metrics across window sizes; Figure 9 shows the quantum weight landscape; Figure 10 provides a qualitative comparison of fringe reconstruction accuracy.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of atom interferometry forecasting, proposes the BARFI-Q architecture (including patch embedding, dual-branch modeling, and adaptive residuals), details the quantum feature mapping module, presents ablation studies on fusion and quantum configurations, and concludes with performance comparisons against classical baselines.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Atom interferometry generates heterogeneous multivariate temporal streams governed by phase evolution, fringe dynamics, control variables, and auxiliary sensing measurements. Accurate forecasting of these signals is important for predictive monitoring, phase correction, and intelligent quantum sensing, but it requires effective modeling of long-range temporal dependencies and interactions among multiple sensing sources. This paper proposes BARFI-Q, a Quantum-Enhanced Block Attention Residual Fusion framework for multivariate time-series forecasting in atom interferometry. BARFI-Q integrates patch-based embedding, dual-branch temporal modeling, hierarchical fusion, adaptive block-attention residual aggregation, and a quantum feature-mapping module. Unlike conventional Transformer-based forecasting models with fixed additive residual paths, BARFI-Q adaptively reuses cross-depth information and enhances the fused latent representation through quantum feature mapping. To respect phase periodicity, the forecasting target is represented in circular space using sine and cosine components. Experiments show that BARFI-Q consistently outperforms strong baseline models across repeated runs and different historical window sizes. Fusion ablation results further confirm the benefit of jointly modeling channel-wise and spatial feature interactions. These results indicate that multiscale temporal learning, hierarchical fusion, adaptive residual routing, and quantum-enhanced latent transformation provide an effective framework for atom-interferometric time-series forecasting.

</details>

### [Frustrated Fields: Statistical Field Theory for Frustrated Brownian Particles on 2D Manifolds](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05366v1)

**Authors:** Igor Halperin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05366v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The theoretical 1/N scaling (red dashed) and the power-law fit N−1.69 (green dotted) are shown. Drot decreases with N, but faster than the theoretical 1/N.</sub>

The Gaussian fit (red) gives µ = 90.0◦(equatorial), σ = 4.8◦, FWHM = 11◦. Nearly all particles (399/400) lie within the ring. (b) Distribution of longitudinal angular velocity ωϕ (velocity along the ring about ˆn): symmetric Gaussian with mean = 0.002 ± 0.121 and σ = 15.5, consistent with zero drift (|mean/std| = 10−4). (c) Mean ωϕ per snapshot: fluctuates about zero with no trend. (d) Ring normal ˆn(t) from the inertia tensor at each snapshot: slow diffusive evolution. (e) Ring width σθ is stable at ≈4.8◦throughout the NESS. (f)...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05366_figures/2605.05366_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Comparison of the effective SO(3) model SDE (red) with the particle simulation (blue), N = 400, T = 0.4, t = 200. Both start from the same initial ˆn; the RP2-NLSM SDE uses Drot = 0.0031 measured from the particle simulation’s autocorrelation, with the same fine time step dt = 0.005 and recording cadence. Top row: components (a) nx(t), (b) ny(t), (c) nz(t) from both simulations, showing matching volatility and similar diffusive character. Bottom row: (d) autocorrelation q(τ) matching e−2Drotτ with τc = 164; (e) MSD saturating at 2; (f) power spectrum of nz, both matching the Markovian ω−2.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper derives a new statistical field theory (the F2 model) for frustrated Brownian particles on curved surfaces. It demonstrates that quenched disorder can cause particles to spontaneously concentrate on lower-dimensional manifolds, such as rings on a sphere, with dynamics governed by a non-linear sigma model. The theory is shown to be highly predictive, reproducing simulation results without adjustable parameters.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a statistical field theory to describe the large-N thermodynamic limit of Brownian particles with quenched random interactions on 2D Riemannian manifolds.

**Main result.** The derivation of the F2 model, a non-linear field theory that predicts adiabatic dimension reduction (e.g., particles concentrating on great-circle rings on a sphere) and identifies the low-energy dynamics as a (0+1)-dimensional non-linear sigma model on the real projective plane.

**Method.** The authors use the MSRJD path-integral formalism, disorder averaging via Hubbard-Stratonovich transformations, and a Born-Oppenheimer-style adiabatic approximation to reduce the many-body dynamics to an effective single-field theory.

**Model / system.** A system of N Brownian particles on a compact 2D Riemannian manifold subject to overdamped Langevin dynamics and quenched random pairwise interactions (spin-glass type) with various long-range potentials.

**Key observables.** Density field (rho), orientation vector (n), rotational diffusion coefficient (D_rot), and two-time density-density and current-current correlators.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Large-N limit, coupling variance (J), friction coefficient (gamma), temperature (T), and the rotational diffusion coefficient (D_rot).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The large-N limit, the adiabatic separation between slow orientation and fast density fluctuations, and the neglect of certain functional determinant fluctuations in the leading-order approximation.

**Figures summary.** The notes do not provide specific figure descriptions, but imply the presence of particle simulations showing density concentration on rings and validation of the effective theory against simulation diagnostics.

**Paper structure.** The paper proceeds from defining the many-body Langevin dynamics and quenched disorder, to constructing the MSRJD path integral, performing disorder averaging to derive the F2 model, applying adiabatic dimension reduction on specific manifolds like S2, and finally validating the effective theory using particle simulations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a statistical field theory that describes the large-N limit of a system of Brownian particles with quenched random pairwise interactions on a compact two-dimensional Riemannian manifold. The resulting Frustrated Fields (F2) model is a non-linear field theory for a smooth self-interacting density field $ρ$ on the manifold, with local and non-local (in space and time) self-interactions characteristic of spin-glass dynamics. Particle simulations show \emph{adiabatic dimension reduction}: on $S^2$, the density concentrates on a slowly precessing great-circle ring whose orientation is a director ($\hat{\mathbf{n}} \sim -\hat{\mathbf{n}}$, even profile). Conditioned on this simulation-supported ring saddle and on a Born-Oppenheimer separation between the slow orientation and the gapped density fluctuations, symmetry fixes the low-energy dynamics to be the nonlinear sigma model (NLSM) on the real projective plane $S^2/\mathbb{Z}_2 = \mathbb{RP}^2$ (the $\mathbb{RP}^2$ NLSM on the projective rotor space) in $(0+1)$ dimensions, governed by a single low-energy constant, the rotational diffusion coefficient $D_{\text{rot}}$. With $D_{\text{rot}}$ and the static ring profile $f_0$ measured from particle simulations, the resulting effective theory reproduces multiple independent orientation- and density-sector diagnostics with no further adjustable parameters.

</details>
