<a id="paper-2605.06579"></a>
### [Practical Log-Depth Quantum State Preparation and Circuit Verification via Tree Tensor Network Compilation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06579v1)

**Authors:** Angus Mingare, Peter V. Coveney  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06579v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06579_figures/2605.06579_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Basic tensors depicted in the tensor network graphical language. A tensor is represented by a node with a number of legs equal to the tensor rank.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06579_figures/2605.06579_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2: A matrix product state (MPS) and a matrix product operator (MPO), each with 4 sites.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.06579_figures/2605.06579_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3: An MPS with N = 6 and χ = 4 in canonical form is mapped directly to a linear-depth staircase circuit with maximum gate size 1 + log (χ).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.06579_figures/2605.06579_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4: A 3-layer TTN with χ = 2 and orthogonality centre at the root is mapped directly to a log-depth staircase circuit.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.06579_figures/2605.06579_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5: A series of local site merges and singular value decompositions brings an MPS into the form of a TTN.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces an analytical method to convert Matrix Product States into quantum circuits with logarithmic depth instead of linear depth. By using a tree tensor network renormalization approach, it allows for much shallower circuits that are more resilient to noise on near-term quantum hardware. The method also extends to verifying unitary operations using Matrix Product Operators.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The high circuit depth of traditional linear-depth (staircase) mappings for loading Matrix Product States (MPS) onto quantum computers makes them impractical for noisy, near-term hardware.

**Main result.** The authors developed an analytical method to decompose MPS and MPO into log-depth quantum circuits using Tree Tensor Network (TTN) renormalization, enabling efficient state preparation and circuit verification.

**Method.** A Tree Tensor Network renormalization procedure uses iterative local merges and Singular Value Decompositions (SVD) to transform MPS into a binary tree structure, which is then mapped to quantum gates.

**Model / system.** The method focuses on N-qubit systems represented by Matrix Product States (MPS) and Matrix Product Operators (MPO) with a bond dimension chi.

**Key observables.** Circuit depth, fidelity (overlap), gate locality, and the squared overlap magnitude |<phi|U|psi>|^2 for verifier circuits.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Circuit depth scaling (O(log N) vs O(N)), bond dimension (chi), system size (N), and noise parameter (delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the MPS can be padded to a bond dimension of 2^k and that the unitary U has an efficient MPO representation; it also assumes a trade-off between fidelity and depth via truncation.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate tensor ranks, the structural difference between MPS and MPO, the linear depth of staircase circuits versus the log-depth of TTN, the SVD renormalization process, and performance metrics like fidelity decay and gate size saturation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of MPS loading, presents the TTN renormalization method, provides an error analysis for truncation, extends the method to MPOs for circuit verification, and demonstrates utility for device calibration.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Matrix product states provide efficient classical descriptions of quantum systems that may be useful as reference states for quantum algorithms such as quantum phase estimation and quantum-selected configuration interaction. Shallow circuit constructions for loading matrix product states onto quantum computers is necessary for this to be practical on near-term hardware. We present a decomposition of matrix product states to log-depth quantum circuits via a simple tree tensor network renormalisation procedure. Our method exposes an explicit parameter which can be used to trade a small amount of fidelity for large savings in circuit depth. We extend this decomposition to the case of matrix product operators allowing us to construct log-depth and ancilla-free circuits to calculate overlaps of the form $\left |\langleφ|U|ψ\rangle\right |^2$. In particular, we demonstrate an interpretation of these circuits as \emph{verifier circuits} with application to circuit-level device calibration.

</details>

### [Pro-Tensor Network](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06661v1)

**Authors:** Gen Yue, Ansi Bai, Linqian Wu, Tian Lan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06661v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `exotic spin models in light-matter` **2/5**

</details>

</details>

<img src="2605.06661_figures/2605.06661_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.06661_figures/2605.06661_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 7: C string-net pro-tensor network</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces 'pro-tensor networks,' a mathematical generalization of tensor networks that uses category theory to study collections of many-body theories simultaneously. By replacing vector spaces with categories, the authors create a framework capable of describing topological order and generalized symmetries without being limited by standard assumptions like finiteness or semisimplicity.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful new mathematical language for studying the renormalization of many-body systems and the emergence of generalized symmetries, which is highly relevant to the study of many-body dynamics and topological phases.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for a rigorous mathematical framework to study the universal properties of entire collections of many-body theories (many-many-body theory) rather than individual models.

**Main result.** The introduction of the 'pro-tensor network,' a categorification of tensor networks using enriched category theory, which generalizes the Levin-Wen model and provides a new classification of topological excitations.

**Method.** The authors use enriched category theory, specifically V-profunctors (distributors) and coend calculus, to create a framework that replaces vector spaces with categories and linear maps with profunctors.

**Model / system.** The framework encompasses a hierarchy of models, including classical tensor networks, the Levin-Wen model (as a uniform pro-tensor network), and string-net models representing topological order.

**Key observables.** Topological order, generalized symmetries, and topological excitations (particles).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The framework specifically addresses regimes beyond the standard constraints of semisimplicity, finiteness, and rigidity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory assumes the base category V is a cosmos (symmetric monoidal closed, complete, and cocomplete), and certain results require the category to be Cauchy complete or rigid.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows two interpretations of classical tensor networks; Figure 2 demonstrates tensor contraction; Figure 3 illustrates the two interpretations of pro-tensor networks; later figures show commutative diagrams for probimonad axioms and module actions.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by introducing the pro-tensor network and its physical motivation, followed by the mathematical construction using enriched category theory and coend calculus, then applies the framework to recover and generalize known models like Levin-Wen, and concludes with technical proofs regarding module equivalences and probimonad structures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce the pro-tensor network, a categorification of the tensor network, as a fully rigorous yet graphically transparent framework for studying the collection of many many-body theories, which we dub many-many-body theory. We provide a comprehensive toolbox for the graphical calculations using pro-tensor networks. As applications, we recover the Levin-Wen model as a "uniform" pro-tensor network and generalize a result of Kitaev and Kong by characterizing particles as modules over promonads. One can also interpret the string-net pro-tensor network as the space of symmetric tensor networks, thus our framework also applies to the study of generalized symmetry and topological holography. Notably, our generalization dispenses with the assumptions of semisimplicity, finiteness, and rigidity, potentially facilitating the exploration of many-body physics beyond these constraints.

</details>

### [Static-Field Tunneling Ionization in Space-Fractional Quantum Mechanics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05617v1)

**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05617v1>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05617_figures/2605.05617_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<img src="2605.05617_figures/2605.05617_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

(a) Negative logarithm of the ioniza- tion rate, −ln Γ, as a function of the inverse field strength 1/F0, comparing 1D-TDSE results (symbols) with the an- alytical ADK tunneling prediction (solid line). (b) Ratio ΓTDSE/ΓADK shown on a logarithmic scale. The ratio re- mains of order unity with only weak field dependence over the field range F0 = 0.04–0.07 a.u., demonstrating that the DC propagation and rate-extraction procedure reliably repro- duce standard tunneling behavior.</sub>

<img src="2605.05617_figures/2605.05617_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4(a) shows the negative logarithm of the ioniza- tion rate, −ln Γ as a function of the inverse field strength 1/F0 over the range F0 = 0.04−0.07 a.u. The TDSE data (symbols) follow an approximately linear dependence on 1/F0, consistent with the exponential tunneling scaling predicted by ADK theory (solid line). A single mul- tiplicative factor is used to align the ADK curve with the numerical data at a reference field of F0 = 0.05 a.u, reflecting the known difference between one-dimensional model calculations and the three-dimensional ADK pref- actor, while preserving the field dependence. Figure 4(b) shows the ratio ΓTDSE/ΓADK, on a logarithmic scale, pro- viding a sensitive...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops an analytical model for tunneling ionization in space-fractional quantum mechanics. It shows that non-local kinetic energy changes the scaling laws of ionization and significantly enhances the ionization rate compared to standard quantum mechanics. The work provides a new benchmark for studying how non-locality affects strong-field physics.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a new theoretical benchmark for non-local quantum dynamics and explores how non-quadratic kinetic energy fundamentally alters the tunneling mechanism, which is relevant to anyone studying non-local transport or fractional dynamics in atomic systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the lack of a standard strong-field benchmark for tunneling ionization in Space-Fractional Quantum Mechanics (SFQM) and seeks to understand how non-local kinetic energy affects the tunneling exponent.

**Main result.** The author derives a closed-form fractional-ADK (fADK) tunneling exponent and demonstrates that decreasing the fractional order alpha enhances ionization rates due to genuine non-local transport effects.

**Method.** The study uses a semiclassical WKB approach to derive the analytical exponent and performs numerical simulations of the 1D time-dependent fractional Schrödinger equation using a split-operator scheme.

**Model / system.** A 1D model of an electron in a static electric field with a soft-core Coulomb potential, governed by a fractional Hamiltonian featuring the Riesz fractional Laplacian of order 1 < alpha <= 2.

**Key observables.** Tunneling ionization rate (Gamma), tunneling exponent, survival probability, and the tunneling action (slope of -ln Gamma vs 1/F0).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fractional order (alpha), ionization potential (Ip), static electric field strength (F0), and the barrier-suppression threshold.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical derivation assumes a triangular exit barrier; the model is restricted to one dimension; and the study focuses on the static-field (quasistatic) limit.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares tunneling rates for different alpha values; Figure 4 benchmarks the 1D-TDSE against standard ADK theory; Figure 5 shows scaling for various alpha values under a fixed potential; Figure 6 compares the numerical 1D-TDFSE results against the derived fADK prediction.

**Paper structure.** The paper reviews conventional ADK/PPT theory, introduces the fractional Hamiltonian framework, provides the analytical derivation of the fADK exponent, presents numerical validation protocols, and concludes with a comparison of numerical results against the new theory.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Tunneling ionization in static or slowly varying electric fields is a cornerstone of strong-field physics and provides the entry point for semiclassical descriptions of above-threshold ionization and high-harmonic generation. In conventional quantum mechanics, the Perelomov--Popov--Terent'ev (PPT) theory and its Ammosov--Delone--Krainov (ADK) form yield an ionization rate whose defining feature is an exponential dependence governed by an under-barrier (imaginary-time) action. Here we develop an analytical ADK-like tunneling model within \emph{space-fractional} quantum mechanics, where the quadratic kinetic energy is replaced by the Riesz fractional Laplacian of order $1<α\le2$. Working in a static electric field in the length gauge, we derive a closed-form tunneling exponent for a triangular exit barrier. The fractional kinetic operator deforms the conventional $I_p^{3/2}$ scaling to $I_p^{1+1/α}$ and introduces a characteristic $\sin(π/α)$ factor encoding the complex-phase structure associated with nonlocal dispersion. We position this benchmark relative to prior tunneling studies in fractional quantum mechanics (primarily scattering through model barriers and fractal potentials) and provide a validation protocol for testing the exponent in time-dependent simulations of the fractional Schrödinger equation under a constant field. The result establishes a transparent reference for static-field ionization in nonlocal quantum dynamics and a baseline for strong-field approaches extensions.

</details>

### [Syndrome resampling enhances quantum error correction thresholds](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06101v1)

**Authors:** Luis Colmenarez, Áron Márton, Markus Müller  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06101v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06101_figures/2605.06101_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Numerical reconstruction of the modified syndrome distribution Qα(s) for the d = 5 unrotated surface code, p = 0.2 and α = 1, 2, 3. We consider 20 stabilizers, which makes a total of 220 possible syndromes. The index one denotes the trivial syndrome. The distribution becomes increasingly peaked around the more probable syndromes as α is increased.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06101_figures/2605.06101_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. The logical error rate as a function of the bit-flip error rate in the vicinity of the respective threshold for (a) α = 1, (b) α = 2, and (c) α = 3. The logical error rates are shown for both MWPM and MLD decoding and for multiple code distances. The insets show the collapsed data for MLD, with the extracted threshold pth and critical exponent ν values listed in Tab. I. The dashed gray lines mark the corresponding RCI threshold values listed in Tab. I.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.06101_figures/2605.06101_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Logical error rate for surface code for bit-flip noise error rate p = 0.08, 0.1, 0.12, 0.14, 0.16. We use MWPM only (blue), syndrome resampling (SR) (orange), complementary gap post-selection (CGPS)(green) and both SR and CGPS (red). For SR we set α = 2. The complementary gap confi- dence parameter is set to c = 2/5, 2/3, 1/2, 2/3 [73] for code distances d = 5, 6, 8, 9 respectively. The lowest logical error rate is always obtained by decoding using both strategies. The grey dashed line denotes the standard optimal threshold p ∼0.109.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.06101_figures/2605.06101_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Logical error rate (top panels) and acceptance rate (bottom panels) of the resampling method under increasing number of samples N for p = 0.12 (left panels) and p = 0.14 (right panels). Post-selected (circles) and α = 2 (stars) resam- pled data are considered. The acceptance rate is calculated as the number of samples kept in independently sampled batches of size N. The post-selected error rate for d = 9 (not shown) is too low to be reliably computed with the present number of samples. Error bars are computed via bootstrapping and represent the 67% confidence interval.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.06101_figures/2605.06101_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Logical error rate in the ˆZL1 ˆZL2 expectation value of the repetition code lattice surgery experiment of Ref. [5]. A surface-17 superconducting processor is initialized in a surface-code state, after which the central column of data qubits is measured out, yielding two entangled three-qubit repetition codes encoding a logical Bell pair. Here α = 1, 2, 3 denotes sampling over the probability distribution bQα (α = 1 denotes no-resampling) and decoding using MWPM. PS de- notes the expectation value discarding all non-trivial syn- dromes. The total number of samples used was 44268.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new method called syndrome resampling that improves quantum error correction performance by reweighting syndrome data. It works for any decoder and can significantly reduce logical error rates in both simulations and real experimental data without needing new hardware. The method effectively pushes the error correction threshold higher by focusing on the most probable error patterns.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of increasing quantum error correction (QEC) thresholds and reducing logical error rates without requiring additional hardware or complex, code-specific decoding modifications.

**Main result.** The introduction of syndrome resampling, which increases QEC thresholds and reduces logical error rates by up to four orders of magnitude in simulations and two orders of magnitude in experimental data.

**Method.** A decoder-agnostic method called syndrome resampling that biases syndrome averages towards more likely syndromes by resampling according to powers of the syndrome probability distribution.

**Model / system.** Quantum error correction codes, specifically surface codes (unrotated and rotated) and the toric code, subject to Pauli bit-flip noise.

**Key observables.** Logical error rate, syndrome probability distribution (SPD), Renyi Coherent Information (RCI), and threshold (p_th).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Resampling parameter alpha, physical error rate p, code distance d, and critical exponent nu.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The noise model is assumed to be Pauli noise, and the method's efficiency is limited by the sampling cost required to estimate the syndrome probability distribution.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the reconstruction of the modified syndrome distribution; Figure 2 displays logical error rates vs. error rates for different alpha values and shows data collapse; Figure 3 compares various decoding strategies; Figure 4 shows the evolution of error rates and acceptance rates; Figure 6 shows finite-size scaling collapse for MWPM.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the syndrome resampling method, establishes its theoretical connection to Renyi Coherent Information and phase transitions, presents numerical simulations on surface codes, demonstrates implementation with finite data and combination with post-selection, and validates the method using existing experimental QEC data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum error correction (QEC) enables fault-tolerant quantum computation but requires operating quantum hardware at physical error rates below code-dependent thresholds, which remains challenging for current devices. We introduce syndrome resampling, a general method that increases QEC thresholds of any decoder and suppresses logical errors without additional hardware, decoding modifications, or code-specific assumptions beyond syndrome statistics. The method exploits the fact that syndromes with low probability are likely to lead to logical failure, therefore biasing syndrome averages towards most likely syndromes effectively increases logical fidelities. We establish a direct connection between the Rényi coherent information (RCI) and powers of the syndrome probability distribution, showing that resampling syndromes according to these powers combined with maximum likelihood decoding (MLD) realizes a family of optimal thresholds associated with phase transitions in the RCI. Numerical simulations of surface codes demonstrate that syndrome resampling substantially increases thresholds for both optimal and suboptimal decoders and reduces logical error rates by up to four orders of magnitude in experimentally relevant regimes. We further show that syndrome resampling can be effectively implemented from finite data and combined with decoding-based post-selection to achieve additional gains. Finally, applying the method to existing experimental QEC data yields up to two orders of magnitude reduction in logical error rates without requiring additional measurements. Our results provide a practical and decoder-agnostic route to improved logical fidelities in near-term QEC experiments.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05518"></a>
### [Classical shadows over symmetric spaces](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05518v1)

**Authors:** Rebecca Chang, Maureen Krumtünger, Martin Larocca, Maxwell West  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05518v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05518_figures/2605.05518_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. The probability distributions on the Bloch sphere re- sulting from acting on the state |0⟩with unitaries drawn from the uniform ensembles on SU(2) and the AIII symmetric space SU(2)/S(U(1) × U(1)). Here and more generally, the invariant measure on the symmetric space G/K lifts to a non-invariant measure on the ambient group G.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05518_figures/2605.05518_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Variances for random (symmetric) observables of unit 2-norm on a d = 128-dimensional system, averaged over 100 instances. Here c = (p −q)/d is the (normalized) signature of Ip,q; each choice of c identifies distinct AIII and BDI protocols. When the random observables are strongly concentrated on the diagonal (with respect to the measurement basis W), AIII and BDI shadows narrowly outperform the standard unitary and orthogonal protocols, respectively. The orthogonal protocols having a variance lower than their unitary counterparts by a constant factor (of order 2) is expected for symmetric observables [6].</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper generalizes the classical shadows technique by investigating measurement protocols where unitaries are sampled from compact symmetric spaces rather than just groups. It reveals that certain symmetric space protocols can outperform standard methods when estimating observables that are concentrated on the diagonal of the measurement basis.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Extending the classical shadows framework beyond standard group-based sampling to include protocols based on compact symmetric spaces (G/K).

**Main result.** The authors establish a unifying theory for symmetric space shadows, showing they are invertible and can provide improved sample complexity for observables concentrated on the diagonal in certain regimes.

**Method.** The study utilizes representation theory, Weingarten calculus, and the study of measurement channels to evaluate integrals over symmetric spaces and analyze the variance of estimators.

**Model / system.** The model involves an unknown quantum state in a d-dimensional Hilbert space being reconstructed via random measurements sampled from the seven infinite families of type I compact symmetric spaces.

**Key observables.** Expectation values of quantum observables, specifically focusing on the variance of estimators for diagonal and off-diagonal components.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The dimension of the Hilbert space (d), the signature of the involution (s), and the scaling regime of the signature (s in O(1) vs s = cd).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes sampling is performed uniformly from the compact symmetric spaces and that the measurement basis is chosen such that involutions take standard forms.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares probability distributions on the Bloch sphere for different ensembles; Figure 2 compares the variance of random symmetric observables across different shadow protocols.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of symmetric space shadows, provides a mathematical framework using group theory and Weingarten calculus, classifies the resulting measurement channels, analyzes the variance in different scaling regimes, and concludes with performance comparisons.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Efficiently learning expectation values of unknown quantum states via classical shadows has become an important primitive in both theoretical and experimental aspects of quantum computation. Typically, classical shadow protocols involve randomised measurements induced by sampling uniformly randomly from a compact group, a situation which is now quite well understood. In this work we go beyond this standard assumption, studying the classical shadow protocols occasioned by sampling uniformly randomly from the so-called compact symmetric spaces. We uncover a unifying theory of such protocols, extending the extent to which the general theory of classical shadows is understood at a mathematical level. Interestingly, for the estimation of observables sampled from certain distributions we further find that some of these protocols allow for slight improvements in sample-complexity over existing shadow schemes.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05294"></a>
### [Competing nonlinearities, criticality, and order-to-chaos transition in deep networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05294v1)

**Authors:** Omri Lesser, Debanjan Chowdhury  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05294v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05294_figures/2605.05294_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the dynamics of variance propagation in a fully connected network. The preactivations z(l) at each layer l are Gaussian distributed with zero mean and variance K(l). The variance evolves according to the kernel recursion Eq. (1), which depends on the activation function σ through the kernel function g(K). (b) The activation function is chosen randomly for each neuron. (c) Schematic phase diagram: the mixing fraction p controls the relative weight of two competing activations with opposing variance stability characters, with an expected phase transition at a critical pc where the network becomes statistically scale invariant.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05294_figures/2605.05294_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Inverse variance 1/K(l) vs. depth l for a Tanh/Swish activation mixture, for several values of the mixing fraction p. Two distinct regimes appear. For p &lt; pc the Tanh-dominated network drives variance to zero (K(l) decays), while for p &gt; pc the Swish-dominated network explodes (K(l) grows), both with a power-law behavior. At the empirical critical point pc ≈0.83 the inverse variance is depth-independent: this is a transition between universality classes, with emergent statis- tical scale invariance.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05294_figures/2605.05294_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Finite-size scaling near the critical point. (a) Variance at layer L (normalized by the input variance K0) vs. mixing fraction p for several values of depth L. The variance transition sharpens with increasing depth, and the curves cross near the critical point pc. (b) Data collapse of the variance curves using the scaling variable (p −pc) L1/ν. The extracted critical exponent is ν = 1, in agreement with a continuous mean-field-like phase transition.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05294_figures/2605.05294_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Signatures of criticality. (a) Parallel susceptibility χ∥and (b) perpendicular susceptibility χ⊥vs. layer depth l for Tanh/Swish mixtures at several values of p. χ∥= 1 means the overall signal scale is preserved layer-to-layer; χ⊥= 1 means transverse perturbations neither grow nor shrink. Away from pc, both quantities drift monotonically with depth. Near pc ≈0.83, both susceptibilities remain pinned to unity across all layers, providing a signature of criticality. (c) Maximal Lyapunov exponent λ vs. mixing fraction p for Tanh/Swish networks. λ &gt; 0 signals exponential divergence of perturbations (chaotic regime, Swish-dominated), while λ &lt; 0 signals contraction (ordered regime,...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05294_figures/2605.05294_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Performance of an MLP with a mixture of Tanh and Swish activations on (a) the MNIST digit classification task [30] and (b) the Fashion-MNIST fashion items classification task [31]. Both test loss (blue) and test accuracy (red) vary non- monotonically with the mixing fraction p, exhibiting an optimum at an intermediate pc. Pure Tanh (p = 0) and pure Swish (p = 1) both underperform. The shaded regions indicate the standard deviation across 10 random seeds.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper shows that by randomly mixing two different smooth activation functions, one can create a continuous phase transition that achieves the benefits of scale-invariant signal propagation (like ReLU) without losing smoothness. This approach provides a controlled way to navigate neural network universality classes and improves model robustness to label noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of analytically controlling the transition between different universality classes of activation functions in deep neural networks, specifically resolving the tension between achieving scale-invariant signal propagation and maintaining function smoothness.

**Main result.** The discovery of a continuous phase transition in a statistical mixture of smooth activations (Tanh and Swish) that enables emergent scale-invariance and acts as a structural regularizer during training.

**Method.** Effective field theory and mean-field dynamics in the infinite-width limit, utilizing kernel recursion, Taylor expansions, and finite-size scaling analysis.

**Model / system.** Deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) where each neuron's activation function is drawn independently from a Bernoulli distribution (a mixture of Tanh and Swish) at initialization, creating quenched activation disorder.

**Key observables.** Preactivation variance propagation, parallel and perpendicular susceptibilities, Lyapunov exponents, and the stability coefficient.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mixing fraction (p), network depth (L), network width (N), and initial input variance (K0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The infinite-width limit (N to infinity) allows for self-averaging and Gaussian preactivations; the transition is analyzed in the context of quenched disorder.

**Figures summary.** Figures depict variance propagation through layers, the transition from variance-collapsing to variance-inflating regimes, the smoothness of the transition, and the non-monotonic relationship between the mixing fraction and neural network test performance/robustness.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of activation universality, proposes a statistical mixture model, develops a mean-field theory to predict the critical point, verifies the transition via Lyapunov exponents and susceptibilities, and demonstrates the practical learning advantages and regularization effects through MNIST/Fashion-MNIST simulations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Deep neural networks owe their expressive power to nonlinear activation functions. The effective field theory of signal propagation at initialization reveals a few distinct universality classes of activations that exhibit different depth scaling. Tuning across these, especially with analytical control, is an open problem. We show that a statistical mixture of activations, where each neuron independently and randomly draws its activation from a two-component distribution with mixing fraction $p$, provides a new mechanism for a continuous phase transition. Applied to a mixture of Tanh and Swish, the transition is sharp in the depth scaling of the preactivation variance, separating a variance-collapsing from a variance-inflating phase; at $p_c$, the network acquires statistical scale invariance, with depth-independent variance, without sacrificing smoothness. This resolves a longstanding tension, where scale-invariant propagation has previously required the non-smooth ReLU family, rendering such networks ill-suited to curvature-based optimizers, physics-informed architectures, and neural-network quantum states. We corroborate the transition through variance propagation, parallel and perpendicular susceptibilities, and Lyapunov exponents. Training multilayer perceptrons on real datasets reveals non-monotonic test performance as a function of $p$, with an optimum near the theoretically predicted $p_c$, confirming that the initialization-level transition has direct consequences for learned representations. The quenched activation disorder acts as a structural regularizer, suppressing memorization of corrupted labels while preserving generalization. Our framework establishes statistical activation mixtures as a controlled tool for navigating the phase diagram of deep network universality classes.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.06249"></a>
### [Finite-size general security for differential phase shift keying via variable-length quantum key distribution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06249v1)

**Authors:** Carlos Pascual-García  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06249v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06249_figures/2605.06249_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06249_figures/2605.06249_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Secret key generation rates for DPSK under different dark count rates pd, fEC = 1.10, ξ = 0.005 and n = 108. All other parameters were optimized for each point of the plot.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.06249_figures/2605.06249_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Secret key generation rates for DPSK under values for the R´enyi parameter α, pd = 0, fEC = 1.10, χ = 10 dB, ξ = 0.005, pK = 0.96 and diverse block sizes n. The amplitude of the coherent states was set to β = 0.45.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.06249_figures/2605.06249_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Secret key generation rates for DPSK under different asymmetry factors γ, coherent state amplitude β = 0.45, pd = 10−5, fEC = 1.10, ξ = 0.005 and n = 107. The R´enyi parameter α and pK were optimized for each point of the plot.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a new security proof for DPSK quantum key distribution that works with much smaller data blocks than previously possible. By using advanced entropy accumulation and conic optimization, it proves that industrial-grade, practical QKD is achievable even with significant channel loss and experimental noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum communications as it provides a rigorous security foundation for practical, high-rate, and low-complexity photonic QKD implementations.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving finite-size general security for Differential Phase Shift Keying (DPSK) QKD without requiring extremely large block sizes or low repetition rates.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that DPSK can achieve secure key rates with block sizes as small as 10^5 signals at losses beyond 12 dB, significantly outperforming previous benchmarks.

**Method.** The study uses the Marginal-Constrained Entropy Accumulation Theorem (MEAT) and Rényi leftover hashing, formulated as a conic optimization problem using the Julia programming language.

**Model / system.** A DPSK QKD protocol where Alice sends coherent states and Bob performs threshold detection using a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, incorporating channel loss, dark counts, and modulation asymmetry.

**Key observables.** Secret key rate (bits per pulse), detection outcomes (clicks, double-clicks, no-clicks), and channel loss (dB).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Block size (n) ranging from 10^5 to 10^9, channel loss (up to 30 dB), dark count probability (pd), and asymmetry factor (gamma).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The security relies on a no-signaling condition between quantum signals and assumes Eve is a general adversary.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows secret key rates vs. channel loss for various block sizes; Figure 2 shows the impact of dark count rates; Figure 3 shows the dependence on the Rényi parameter; Figure 4 shows the impact of modulation asymmetry.

**Paper structure.** The paper defines the DPSK protocol, establishes an operational equivalence to a simpler relativistic protocol, applies the MEAT framework with dimension reduction via squashing maps, and performs numerical optimization to derive key rates.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Differential phase shift keying (DPSK) constitutes a pathway towards practical quantum key distribution by using affordable commercial technologies, and robust theoretical foundations. Recent advances in the security of DPSK have proven its security against general adversaries, albeit requiring limitations, including strong repetition rate constraints at the security proof and costly statistical estimators. In this work, we overcome said limitations by leveraging recent techniques in variable-length general security by using entropy accumulation techniques based on Rényi leftover hashing, together with conic optimization methods. Our approach achieves secret key rates with $10^5$ signals beyond 12 dB, constituting a robust proof of the experimental implementability of industrial-grade DPSK.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Frustrated Dynamics of Distance Matrices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05376v1)

**Authors:** Igor Halperin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05376v1>

The leading eigenvalue is essentially time-independent at λ1 ≈N⟨d⟩= Nπ/2 ≈628 (the rank-1 component of M(t)) and is well separated from λ2, λ3 ∼O(1). (b) the three most negative eigenvalues. The most-negative eigenvalue λN drops from ≈−200 at t = 0 to ≈−400 during the ring-formation transient on the same timescale as the energy drop in Fig. 1, providing a sharp spectral signature of ring formation. Bottom row, log time: same trajectories on a logarithmic time axis using the dense early-time recording. (c) the top three eigenvalues remain visibly constant across the...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a framework to detect geometric transitions in a system by analyzing the time-varying spectrum of a distance matrix. It demonstrates that a collapse from a 2D sphere to a 1D ring can be identified through specific shifts in the eigenvalue distribution and rank-decay exponents. This provides a method for solving dynamical inverse problems in complex systems where only pairwise distances are observable.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining if structural changes in an underlying particle configuration, such as dimensional collapse from a 2D sphere to a 1D ring, can be detected using only the time series of a distance matrix without direct access to particle positions.

**Main result.** The study finds that the static Bogomolny, Bohigas, and Schmit (BBS) spectral template is preserved during dynamics, but structural changes are identifiable through a redistribution of spectral mass, specifically via rank reduction in the l=1 multiplet and shifts in the rank-decay exponent.

**Method.** Numerical simulations of Langevin SDEs for Brownian particles on a sphere, combined with spectral analysis of the distance matrix, including MLE for power-law exponents and comparison against i.i.d. resampled ensembles.

**Model / system.** The Frustrated Distance Matrix (FDM) model, consisting of N Brownian particles on a 2-sphere (S2) evolving under quenched random pairwise couplings that are linear in the geodesic distances.

**Key observables.** Eigenvalue spectrum (top, bottom, and bulk), rank-decay exponent (beta), ring-quality diagnostic (eta), and the l=1 multiplet rank reduction.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size (N), temperature (T), disorder strength (sigma), and the fast relaxation timescale (tau_fast).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The FDM is assumed to be a valid dynamic extension of the Euclidean Random Matrix (ERM) class, and the dynamics are modeled as overdamped Langevin processes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the evolution of energy and ring-quality over time; Figure 2 tracks the evolution of the largest and most negative eigenvalues.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the FDM model, describes the particle dynamics and matrix construction, presents the numerical simulation protocol, details the spectral diagnostics for detecting ring formation, and concludes with potential applications to other inverse-problem settings.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce the Frustrated Distance Matrix (FDM) model, a dynamic extension of the static distance-matrix ensemble on S^2 analyzed by Bogomolny, Bohigas, and Schmit (BBS). Its entries are pairwise geodesic distances between N Brownian particles on the sphere evolving under quenched random pairwise couplings linear in those distances. Where the static BBS theory recovers geometric information about the underlying manifold from spectra of distance matrices on i.i.d.\ samples, the time-resolved FDM spectrum carries information about structural changes of the underlying point process. The particle dynamics realize one such change: a fast collapse from a uniform configuration onto a one-dimensional ring, followed by slow rotational drift of the ring orientation; the particle-level picture provides the ground truth against which spectral diagnostics are calibrated. We find that the static BBS template is preserved at every time, with the dynamics entering as a redistribution of spectral mass within that template, sharp enough to flag ring formation. We propose self-averaging of the bulk density as the mechanism behind this preservation, verified by an i.i.d.-resample comparison, and extract a small set of spectral diagnostics of the structural change computable from the distance matrix alone. We suggest that our diagnostics can be applied in other similar inverse-problem settings: financial correlation matrices, graph and network adjacency spectra, similarity matrices in molecular dynamics, and dynamics on parameter manifolds.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05335"></a>
### [Geometric and Topological Obstructions to Hermitianization in Quasi-Hermitian Quantum Systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05335v1)

**Authors:** Ming-Zhang Wang, Xu-Yang Hou, Hao Guo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05335v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05335_figures/2605.05335_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1. Geometric obstruction: Left, quasi-Hermitian parameter space with loops at r0 = 2 √</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05335_figures/2605.05335_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2. Topological obstruction: Left, quasi-Hermitian parameter space with a hole; right, Hermitian parameter space also with a hole. The red loops enclose the holes, leading to non-trivial Wilson loops and the absence of a global proper S. The green loops avoid the holes, giving trivial Wilson loops and preserving the local equivalence.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper identifies the geometric and topological reasons why a non-Hermitian system with a real spectrum cannot always be globally mapped to a Hermitian one. It demonstrates that even if a local mapping exists, curvature and non-trivial loops in parameter space can prevent a consistent global description. This establishes a foundation for understanding the persistence of intrinsic non-Hermitian features.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and non-Hermitian physics, as it provides a fundamental mathematical framework for understanding when non-Hermitian dynamics can be truly treated as equivalent to standard Hermitian dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the conditions under which a quasi-Hermitian quantum system can be globally and dynamically mapped to an equivalent Hermitian system via a similarity transformation.

**Main result.** The authors identify two distinct obstructions to global Hermitianization: geometric obstructions arising from non-zero gauge curvature and topological obstructions arising from non-trivial holonomies (Wilson loops) in the parameter space.

**Method.** The study employs a gauge theory approach, using differential geometry to analyze the metric-induced gauge connection, curvature, and Wilson loops to determine the integrability of the similarity transformation.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on finite-dimensional quasi-Hermitian quantum systems (including PT-symmetric ones) with a real energy spectrum, characterized by a parameter-dependent Hamiltonian H(R) and a positive-definite metric operator eta(R).

**Key observables.** The Berry phase (and its mismatch between quasi-Hermitian and Hermitian descriptions), gauge curvature (F_G), and the Wilson loop (W(C)).

**Important parameters / regimes.** External parameters R defining the system; the regime of positive-definite metric operators and entirely real energy spectra.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The energy spectrum of the Hamiltonian is assumed to be entirely real, and the metric operator is assumed to be positive-definite.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 illustrates a topological obstruction, showing how loops enclosing a hole in parameter space result in different Berry phases compared to loops that avoid the hole.

**Paper structure.** The paper defines the framework of quasi-Hermitian mechanics, establishes the criteria for dynamical compatibility, classifies geometric and topological obstructions using gauge theory, and provides three illustrative examples (integrable, geometric obstruction, and topological obstruction).

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quasi-Hermitian quantum systems, including $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric ones, can be mapped to equivalent Hermitian systems via a similarity transformation that redefines the inner product with a positive-definite metric operator. Although an instantaneous algebraic Hermitianization can be obtained locally from a positive metric operator, a stronger requirement is needed for dynamical equivalence: the similarity transformation must be proper, globally single-valued, and compatible with the modified quasi-Hermitian Schrodinger equation. We identify two distinct obstructions: geometric obstructions arising from the curvature of a metric-induced connection, and topological obstructions originating from non-trivial holonomies around non-contractible loops in parameter space. We derive explicit criteria for these obstructions and illustrate them with concrete examples. Our results establish a geometric and topological foundation for the Hermitianization of quasi-Hermitian systems, clarifying when they can be globally reduced to Hermitian ones and when intrinsic non-Hermitian features persist.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.06251"></a>
### [Meromorphic Quantum Computing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06251v1)

**Authors:** Simon Burton, Hussain Anwar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06251v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06251_figures/2605.06251_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Two ways to label qubit states.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06251_figures/2605.06251_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: We show an octahedral “design” on the Riemann sphere, and its image under E7 on the right. Each of the twenty-four tiles on the left is mapped to a single region on the right which covers the sphere.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces 'Meromorphic Quantum Computing,' a framework that replaces standard Hilbert space vectors with projective points to eliminate global phase. By treating quantum decoders as meromorphic functions, the authors provide a new algebraic method to calculate how effectively quantum codes can suppress coherent errors and distill magic states.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to reformulate the kinematic axioms of quantum mechanics and quantum error correction using projective geometry and meromorphic functions instead of standard Hilbert space vectors.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that quantum decoders for stabilizer and CSS codes can be expressed as multivariate meromorphic functions, which allows for characterizing the suppression of coherent errors and the distillation of magic states via the properties of stationary points.

**Method.** The framework uses projectivization, the Segre embedding, and the ZX-calculus to map linear operators to rational functions, applying algebraic geometry tools like the Riemann-Hurwitz theorem and weight enumerators.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on finite-dimensional complex vector spaces, specifically qubits modeled as the Riemann sphere, and quantum error-correcting codes such as the Shor, Steane, and [[5, 1, 3]] codes.

**Key observables.** The degree of stationary points in the meromorphic decoder, the r-polynomial roots, and the order of coherent error suppression.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The code parameters [n, k, d], the degree of the meromorphic decoder, and the error scales (e.g., O(epsilon^2) or O(epsilon^3)).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is primarily restricted to finite-dimensional spaces, pure states, and the univariate case (k=1), and it focuses on coherent operations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows two ways to label qubit states (Riemann sphere vs. Bloch octahedron); Figure 2 illustrates Klein's octahedral function mapping tiles on the Riemann sphere.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing the projective kinematic axioms, moves to the mathematical framework of projectivization and the ZX-calculus, then applies these tools to analyze the properties and error-suppression capabilities of specific quantum error-correcting codes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider the kinematic axioms of quantum mechanics projectively. Instead of normalized (pure) states up to global phase, states become one-dimensional subspaces of vector spaces. This process of projectivization is functorial and lax monoidal. For qubits it identifies the Bloch sphere with the Riemann sphere. We interpret a fragment of the ZXW-calculus projectively and thereby provide an alternate derivation of the arithmetic GHZ/W-calculus of Coecke et al. We find meromorphic functions that characterize the coherent behaviour of circuits for logical state preparation of quantum codes and magic state distillation.

</details>

**Authors:** Tomer Berghaus, Touvia Miloh, Gregory Ya. Slepyan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05785v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides a new theory for the optical forces acting on carbon nanotubes, specifically highlighting how nonlocal conductivity enables an 'optical pulling' effect. By accounting for edge effects and spatial dispersion, the authors show that the force can become negative, offering new insights into the optomechanical control of nanotubes.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of an optical pulling force arising from spatial dispersion is highly relevant to quantum optics and optomechanics, as it provides a mechanism for light-induced manipulation of low-dimensional nanostructures.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a theoretical framework to describe the optical force on finite-length carbon nanotubes (CNTs) by accounting for nonlocal conductivity and edge effects.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that nonlocality in conductivity leads to an 'optical pulling' effect (negative axial force) in certain frequency regimes, a phenomenon that vanishes in the local conductivity limit.

**Method.** The authors employ an integral-equation-based approach using the Maxwell Stress Tensor and a Fredholm integral equation of the second kind to solve for surface current density and electric fields.

**Model / system.** A finite-length, cylindrical, zigzag carbon nanotube (m, 0) modeled as a 1D conductor using a pseudo-spin electron liquid model and the Kubo formalism.

**Key observables.** Axial component of the optical force (pushing vs. pulling), surface current density, and axial electric field.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Frequency (THz to visible), electrochemical potential (mu), CNT length (L), radius (R), and the nonlocality parameter (xi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Low temperature (collision-free electron motion), zero-temperature approximation, and a Taylor series expansion of spatial dispersion truncated at the first-order term.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Schematic of CNT under oblique wave incidence; Fig 2: Nonlocal parameter and conductivity vs frequency; Fig 3: Optical force comparison (numerical, analytical, local, non-local); Fig 4: Force dependence on electrochemical potential; Fig 5: Force dependence on CNT length in different frequency regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the derivation of nonlocal conductivity from a Hamiltonian/Liouville approach, moves to the construction of the electromagnetic integral equations for the Hertz vector, presents numerical and analytical solutions for the optical force, and concludes with a comparison of local vs. non-local regimes.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a new theory of an optical force exerted on a carbon nanotube (CNT) with a nonlocal conductivity. The optical force is expressed in terms of the surface current density and the axial electric field on the CNT surface. To determine these quantities, we employ an integral-equation-based approach in terms of the current density. The analysis is constructed for a finite-length cylindrical CNT by rigorously accounting for edge effects. In addition to numerical solutions of the integral equation, we obtain an approximate analytical expression for the optical force acting on the CNT, which shows good agreement with numerical simulations. We also demonstrate the existence of some frequency ranges in which the optical force becomes negative, corresponding to the optical pulling effect. Such a pulling behavior is shown to originate from the nonlocality of the conductivity and to vanish in the local limit. This work advances theoretical understanding of optomechanical interactions in finite-length low-dimensional conductors and clarifies the role of spatial dispersion in the emergence of optical pulling forces.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.05307"></a>
### [Perturbative, Nonperturbative and Exact Aspects of Crystalline Phases in the Gross-Neveu Model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05307v1)

**Authors:** Francesco Benini, Ohad Mamroud, Tomas Reis, Marco Serone  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05307v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5**

At some radius around zero momentum for the neutral fermions and around the Fermi surface for the charged fermions, the coupling explodes and perturbation theory breaks down.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

</details>

The gray and black solid lines are σ = 0 and σ = Λ, respectively, whereas the red and blue lines correspond to σ+ and σ−, respectively. The vertical dashed black line marks hy as defined in (3.17). For the plot on the right, h∗is the point where the black and blue lines cross and is marked by the dashed gray line. Meanwhile, the dotted line marks hpt = 2 πy sin ␀ πy</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a complete description of the crystalline phase in the O(2N) Gross-Neveu model at finite density. By reconciling perturbative, semiclassical, and exact integrable methods, it identifies the specific dynamical scales that govern the physics of the inhomogeneous vacuum. This resolves previous discrepancies in the scaling of nonperturbative effects known as the renormalon puzzle.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study of inhomogeneous phases and the emergence of new dynamical scales in a relativistic model provides deep insights into the stability of vacuum configurations and the complex phase structures found in many-body systems.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to characterize the phase diagram and crystalline (inhomogeneous) phases of the O(2N) Gross-Neveu model at finite density, specifically resolving the 'renormalon puzzle' regarding fractional powers in the free energy expansion.

**Main result.** The authors identify two new dynamically generated scales, Lambda_n and Lambda_c, which govern the mass gaps of neutral and charged excitations in the inhomogeneous phase, thereby providing a consistent unified picture across perturbative, semiclassical, and integrable approaches.

**Method.** The study employs a combination of perturbative QFT (1-loop RG flow), semiclassical large N analysis (solving saddle-point equations), and integrability techniques (Bethe Ansatz and transseries expansion).

**Model / system.** The O(2N) Gross-Neveu model in two spacetime dimensions, consisting of N massless Dirac fermions with four-fermion interactions and a chemical potential applied to a subset of the fermions.

**Key observables.** Free energy, chiral condensate profile, mass gaps of neutral and charged excitations, and the spectrum of asymptotic states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of fermion flavors N, number of charged fermions a, chemical potential h, coupling constant g^2, and the dynamically generated scales Lambda, Lambda_n, and Lambda_c.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis focuses on zero temperature (T=0), assumes a specific range for the number of charged fermions (1 <= a <= N-2), and utilizes 1-loop approximations for the perturbative RG flow.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the RG flow at finite density illustrating the breakdown of perturbation theory; Figure 2 displays the five 1-loop Feynman diagrams contributing to the four-point function.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of the crystalline phase, presents a perturbative analysis of the beta function and scale emergence, follows with a semiclassical large N analysis of the condensate profile, and concludes with an exact treatment using integrability and the Bethe Ansatz.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the crystalline phase of the $O(2N)$ Gross--Neveu model with a chemical potential for $a \leq N-2$ of the fermions. We analyze the problem in three independent ways: using perturbative QFT methods, a semiclassical large $N$ analysis, and integrability techniques (both at finite and large $N$). The resulting picture is consistent across all three approaches: at sufficiently large chemical potential $h$, an inhomogeneous phase emerges in which $a$-particle bound states condense and which, at large $N$, corresponds to a periodically oscillating chiral condensate. In this phase, the usual dynamically generated scale $Λ$ is replaced by two new dynamically generated scales $Λ_{\rm n}$ and $Λ_{\rm c}$. These two scales govern the multiple nonperturbative effects in the theory, corresponding in particular to the mass gaps of neutral and charged excitations on top of the inhomogeneous vacuum, respectively. They also control the nonperturbative corrections to observables such as the free energy and provide the parameters characterizing the oscillatory profile of the mean field at large $N$. In this paper, we provide the necessary details of each of the three methods, thereby complementing the results announced in a previous, shorter publication.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Quantum Electron Quasicrystal](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06302v1)

**Authors:** Pierre-Antoine Graham, Filippo Gaggioli, Liang Fu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06302v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Frenkel-Kontorova` **2/5**

For two decou- pled layers, ZPE = 0.483821 EH · r−3/2 s [28]. (b) First order phase transition line r∗ s separating the honeycomb and qua- sicrystal states, as obtained by setting ϵ(θ) = ϵHC in Eq. <sub>FIG. 4. Small-rs phase diagram showing the Lindemann- criterion melting lines for the quasicrystal (circles) and hon- eycomb (squares) states, as a function of rs and ts/rs. The vertical dotted line represents the melting line for a single Wigner crystal layer. The gray region represents the Fermi liquid. We indicate regions where the classical ground state is a staggered rhombic stacking (R) and staggered square stack- ing (SQ) [24] with yellow and green colors, respectively.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper provides the theoretical foundation for the existence of an electronic quasicrystal in twisted bilayer electron systems. It shows that while classical energy favors a honeycomb lattice, quantum zero-point fluctuations stabilize a 30-degree twisted quasicrystalline state. This establishes a new regime of many-body physics where quantum motion drives structural order.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of a quantum-stabilized, non-periodic phase driven by zero-point motion provides a new mechanism for spontaneous moir\2019e physics and could be extended to bosonic systems like dipolar gases.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the physical origin and stability of the previously discovered electronic quasicrystal phase in twisted bilayer electron systems.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that quantum fluctuations (zero-point energy) stabilize the 30-degree twisted quasicrystalline ground state over the classically preferred honeycomb configuration.

**Method.** The study uses an analytical framework to calculate zero-point energy corrections by expanding the Hamiltonian to quadratic order and employs commensurate approximants to study incommensurate twist angles.

**Model / system.** A bilayer system of spin-polarized electrons in wide semiconductor quantum wells, modeled by a 2D Hamiltonian with Coulomb interactions between two layers separated by distance ts.

**Key observables.** Zero-point energy (ZPE), density of states (DOS) of collective modes, normal mode spectrum (acoustic/optical modes), and interlayer structure factor.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electron density (rs), layer separation ratio (ts/rs), twist angle (theta), and the Moir\2019e modulation scale.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of commensurate approximants to represent incommensurate states, harmonic approximation for calculating normal mode frequencies, and neglecting exponentially suppressed reciprocal lattice shells.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the bilayer setup and the 30-degree twist; Figure 2 compares the normal mode spectrum and DOS for honeycomb vs. quasicrystal; Figure 3 shows ZPE differences and energy landscapes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the electronic quasicrystal, develops an analytical framework for zero-point energy, compares classical vs. quantum energy landscapes, analyzes the role of lattice relaxation, and discusses the stability and phase transitions of the system.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The strongly correlated phases of the homogeneous electron gas constitute the vocabulary of many-body condensed matter physics and find a natural realization in semiconductors. In this setting, recent neural-network variational Monte Carlo calculations discovered an unexpected quantum phase of matter in wide quantum wells: an electronic quasicrystal formed by a bilayer Wigner crystals with a 30-degrees twist. This state defies classical expectations and emerges in a regime dominated by quantum fluctuations. Here, we develop an analytical framework to reveal its origin. By computing zero-point energy corrections to bilayer Wigner crystal configurations, we show that quantum fluctuations qualitatively reshape the energetic landscape, destabilizing the classical honeycomb state and selecting the 30-degrees quasicrystalline ground state over a broad parameter range. Our results identify zero-point motion as the mechanism stabilizing the electronic quasicrystal and establish a route to spontaneous moiré physics driven by many-body quantum effects.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [The true cost of factoring: Linking magic and number-theoretic complexity in Shor's algorithm](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05347v1)

**Authors:** Alessio Paviglianiti, Matteo Seclì, Emanuele Tirrito, Vincenzo Savona  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05347v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

(b) Scaling of the final magic M2(τ = t) as a function of r. The asymptotic curves are evaluated using Eq. (6). For each r, 10 different random choices of a are shown (if available). For all plots, numerical results are averaged over 150 random realizations of the algorithm.</sub>

The main contribution to the global algorithmic success rate comes from large periods, which require close-to-maximal magic to be simulated. We present data for six choices of products of primes N, covering the range L ∈[15, 17]. The success rate is normalized by its maximum to account for dif- ferent number of periods for different N. For each r, M2 and psucc are averaged over 100 different coprimes a (if available). psucc is further averaged over 100 independent realizations of the algorithm for each...</sub>

</details>

(a-b) Graphical visualization of the time intervals, using a random coprime x with r = 1036. (c) Agreement between the time intervals. The success probability is averaged over 5000 random realizations of the algorithm. ∆τpsucc is estimated by looking for the value of t where psucc becomes zero within the sampling precision.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper connects the classical difficulty of factoring to the quantum resource requirements of Shor's algorithm. It proves that the amount of 'magic' or non-stabilizerness generated is directly tied to the size of the period being found. This provides a new, resource-based metric for understanding the complexity of quantum algorithms beyond simple gate counts.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to quantify the 'true cost' of Shor's algorithm by linking the generation of non-stabilizerness (magic) to the number-theoretic complexity of the factoring task.

**Main result.** The authors establish a direct correlation between the period $r$ and the amount of magic generated, showing that $M_2$ scales with $\log r$ and that the algorithm's success depends on preserving this quantum resource.

**Method.** The study uses an analytic theory based on the Stabilizer Renyi Entropy (SRE) and the stabilizer formalism, complemented by numerical simulations of the quantum circuit and stochastic sampling of Pauli strings.

**Model / system.** The system is the Shor's factoring algorithm, specifically the order-finding routine involving a $t$-step semi-classical Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) and modular exponentiation on an $n$-qubit register.

**Key observables.** Magic (non-stabilizerness) quantified via Stabilizer Renyi Entropy ($M_2$), algorithmic success rate ($S$), and the period ($r$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The period $r$ (factored into $2^k$ and $	ilde{r}_{odd}$), circuit depth $	au$, and the number of qubits $L$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The phases in the quantum superposition are treated as pseudo-random variables to apply the Central Limit Theorem, and the bitstring distribution is assumed to be uniform for the analytic derivation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a conceptual link between magic, number-theoretic complexity, and success; Figure 2 shows the Shor's circuit structure; Figure 3 illustrates the temporal evolution of magic (ramp, plateau, growth) and its scaling; Figure 5 shows the correlation between the decay of success probability and the magic plateau length.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of quantifying quantum resources, develops an analytic theory for magic evolution in Shor's algorithm, identifies three temporal regimes of SRE growth, demonstrates the link between number-theoretic difficulty and magic, and concludes by positioning magic as a diagnostic tool for algorithmic complexity.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The execution cost of quantum algorithms is typically quantified through asymptotic gate counts and qubit register sizes, yet these metrics do not directly capture which genuinely quantum resources, and in what amount, must be created and maintained for the computation to succeed. The systematic quantification of such information-theoretic requirements in quantum computing protocols remains an extremely challenging open problem, despite their direct role in establishing quantum advantage. To address this gap, we investigate the generation of non-stabilizerness (or magic), one of the key resources, in the paradigmatic Shor's factoring algorithm, revealing a deep connection between intrinsic quantum complexity and the computational hardness of the underlying number-theoretic problem. By developing an explicit analytic theory, we demonstrate the fundamental role of magic in the successful execution of the algorithm, and show that Shor's routine maximally exploits the quantum resource in practically relevant regimes. Our findings create a concise conceptual link between the classical algorithmic difficulty of a task and the non-stabilizer price to solve it on quantum hardware, complementing standard circuit-cost analyses with a resource-based metric that is naturally aligned with the real bottlenecks of fault-tolerant quantum computing.

</details>

### [Toward Hop-Independent Fidelity in Quantum Data Centers: Resource Requirements for Entanglement Purification](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05369v1)

**Authors:** Mohadeseh Azari, Anoosha Fayyaz, Amy Babay, David Tipper, Prashant Krishnamurthy, Kaushik Seshadreesan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05369v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<sub>Fig. 1. Schematic of the black-box setting studied in this work. First, elementary entanglement is generated across neighboring nodes. Second, routing and entanglement swapping create one or more raw end-to-end entangled pairs between the requested terminal QPUs. Third, entanglement purification may be applied to the available raw end-to-end copies to recover the quality of the final shared state. This paper focuses on the third stage: given a path length and a supply of raw end-to-end copies, we ask how many copies are required to recover elementary-link-level quality with a prescribed success probability.</sub>
