<a id="paper-2605.05589"></a>
### [Galois Solvability of Finite-Size Bethe Solutions in the Heisenberg Chain](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05589v1)

**Authors:** Oliver R. Bellwood, William J. Munro  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05589v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05589_figures/2605.05589_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Exact symbolic ground states of the two-, four-, six-, and eight-site Heisenberg chains in the non-crossing valence- bond basis. Square brackets denote a linear combination over VB configurations reached via symmetry transforms, and the number below the square brackets is the multiplicity of such configurations. At eight sites (d), the coefficients α, β, and δ are algebraic numbers and are solvable in radicals [12], which contrasts with the underlying degree-six Bethe-polynomial which is unsolvable.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05589_figures/2605.05589_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Algebraic complexity of the finite-size Bethe ansatz ground-state solutions versus chain length. The N = 6 Bethe- root and ground state is governed by a quadratic polynomial and is solvable in radicals. At N = 8, the Bethe-polynomial has degree six with unsolvable Galois group S6, yet the exact ground state remains expressible in the radicals. The N = 10 case is the first case to exhibit unsolvable Galois structure in both the Bethe-roots and the exact ground state.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores whether the exact solutions of the integrable Heisenberg chain can be written in terms of simple radicals. It reveals that the underlying Bethe-roots become algebraically unsolvable at $N=8$, even though the physical energy and wavefunction remain solvable until $N=10$. This highlights a fundamental gap between the existence of an exact solution and the ability to express it analytically.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the distinction between formal integrability and analytic tractability by determining if the exact solutions of the Heisenberg chain can be expressed in closed-form radicals.

**Main result.** The authors find that while the Bethe-roots become Galois unsolvable at $N=8$, the ground state energy and wavefunction coefficients remain solvable in radicals until $N=10$, demonstrating that algebraic complexity grows at different rates.

**Method.** The study uses the coordinate Bethe ansatz to derive symbolic ground states and employs Galois theory (Stauduhar's method and Fieker-Klueners algorithm via MAGMA) to analyze the algebraic structure of the solutions.

**Model / system.** The spin-1/2 Heisenberg antiferromagnetic chain with periodic boundary conditions, specifically focusing on finite-size systems from $N=2$ to $N=10$ sites.

**Key observables.** Bethe-roots (magnon momenta), ground state energy, and wavefunction coefficients.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size $N$ (up to 10 sites), exchange coupling $J$, and the algebraic degree of the solutions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is limited to small finite-size chains ($N \le 10$) and assumes the use of the coordinate Bethe ansatz for symbolic derivation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows exact symbolic ground states in the non-crossing valence-bond basis for $N=2, 4, 6, 8$. Figure 2 summarizes the transition of algebraic complexity from radical solvability to unsolvable structures as chain length increases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of analytic tractability, describes the Heisenberg model and Bethe ansatz methods, presents the algebraic analysis of roots and wavefunctions, discusses the crossover point at $N=8$, and concludes with scaling conjectures and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The spin-1/2 Heisenberg antiferromagnetic chain is the canonical example of an integrable quantum many-body model. Despite its exact solvability, explicit finite-size solutions are typically only accessible via numerical evaluation of the Bethe ansatz equations. Here, we analyse the algebraic structure of the exact, symbolic ground states for chains up to ten sites using the coordinate Bethe ansatz. We show that both the ground state wavefunction and the Bethe-roots rapidly develop algebraic complexity with respect to system size, but at different rates. The Bethe-roots appear to become Galois unsolvable for chains of eight or more sites, whereas the ground state wavefunction coefficients and energy appear to become unsolvable for ten or more sites. This demonstrates a lack of explicit analytic tractability in a quantum integrable model due to algebraic complexity.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.06301"></a>
### [Higher-order interactions in ecology can be hidden in plain sight](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06301v1)

**Authors:** Violeta Calleja-Solanas, Santiago Lamata-Otín, Carlos Gómez-Ambrosi, Jesús Gómez-Gardeñes, Sandro Meloni  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06301v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06301_figures/2605.06301_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1: Workflow to assess whether higher-order interac- tions are detectable from abundance time series. From the time series of abundances obtained by integrating the orig- inal higher-order system, we evaluate the per capita growth rates, fit them to a hyperplane to infer interaction coefficients and intrinsic growth rates, and integrate the resulting pairwise system. The final comparison between the inferred and orig- inal dynamics reveals whether higher-order interactions are detectable.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06301_figures/2605.06301_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2: Examples of detectable and undetectable higher-order interactions. Panels a–d correspond to System 1 (detectable), and panels e–h to System 2 (undetectable). Panels a and e show the original interaction structure, including higher-order interactions (join arrows), while panels b and f display the inferred pairwise interaction networks obtained from the fitting procedure. In these network representations, green (red) arrows indicate positive (negative) interaction coefficients. Panels c and g compare the original and the inferred attractors, and panels d and h compare the corresponding time series of species 3. In System 1, higher-order interactions leave dynamical signatures that...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.06301_figures/2605.06301_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3: Undetectable higher-order interactions in a multispecies system. Panels a and b show the original pairwise and higher-order interaction structure. The inferred pairwise interaction network (panels c and d) present two misidentified interac- tions (between species 2 and 4, and species 3 and 6), as well as new spurious interactions. Green (red) arrows indicate positive (negative) interaction coefficients. Panel e shows the comparison between the original and inferred time series of species abun- dances. The pairwise approximation closely reproduces the higher-order dynamics, with nearly identical trajectories for all species, but introduces new interactions that alter the ecological...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper shows that higher-order interactions in complex systems can be 'hidden' because simpler pairwise models can accurately predict population dynamics while providing a false description of the underlying interaction structure. It identifies a fundamental limit to inferring complex interaction networks from time-series data alone.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the 'mechanistic identifiability' problem: whether higher-order interactions (HOIs) in ecological communities can be uniquely detected from species abundance time-series data or if they can be hidden by effective pairwise models.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that higher-order Lotka-Volterra dynamics can be accurately mimicked by pairwise models, leading to high predictive accuracy but fundamentally incorrect ecological interpretations (misidentifying species roles).

**Method.** The study uses a computational pipeline involving numerical integration of higher-order Lotka-Volterra systems, linear least-squares regression to fit growth rates to pairwise hyperplanes, and the use of Hessian matrices to detect curvature in growth rate functions.

**Model / system.** The system consists of multi-species ecological communities modeled using higher-order Lotka-Volterra (hoLV) equations, which include intrinsic growth rates, pairwise interactions, and triadic (three-species) interactions.

**Key observables.** Species abundance trajectories, per capita growth rates, system attractors (equilibrium vs. limit cycles), and the Hessian matrix of growth rates.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Intrinsic growth rates (ri), pairwise interaction coefficients (aij), higher-order interaction coefficients (bijk), and the relative weight of HOIs (theta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis uses idealized in silico conditions with high-resolution trajectories and no observation error to test fundamental limits of identifiability.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the methodology flowchart; Figure 2 compares detectable vs. undetectable systems using 3-species models; Figure 3 shows a 7-species multi-trophic system where pairwise approximations remain accurate despite HOIs; Figure 4 illustrates how Jacobian entries can fail to show sign changes even when HOIs are present.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of mechanistic identifiability, defines the higher-order Lotka-Volterra model, presents a computational pipeline for inference, categorizes failure modes of detection (detectable vs. hidden regimes), proposes the Hessian matrix as a diagnostic tool, and demonstrates the scalability of the phenomenon in complex communities.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Higher-order interactions are increasingly recognized as a key component of ecological dynamics. However, we show that higher-order Lotka-Volterra dynamics can, in some scenarios, be accurately reproduced by effective pairwise models fitted to the same abundance time series. Consequently, higher-order interactions cannot, in general, be inferred from time-series data alone. We further identify a fundamental problem of mechanistic identifiability, whereby distinct interaction mechanisms generate nearly indistinguishable dynamics, potentially leading to accurate yet misleading ecological interpretations. Our results highlight the need to complement time-series data with additional ecological information to infer interaction structure reliably.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.05915"></a>
### [Lack of self-averaging of the critical internal energy in a weakly-disordered Baxter model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05915v1)

**Authors:** Ramgopal Agrawal, Victor Dotsenko, Maxym Dudka, Marco Picco, Enzo Marinari, Gleb Oshanin  
**Type:** both · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05915v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05915_figures/2605.05915_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Baxter’s eight-vertex model: The model is represented as two square-lattice Ising models coupled via four-spin interactions [33, 34]. The first lattice (depicted by dotted lines) contains spins σ(1) (green) numbered by indices i and j. The second (depicted by dashed lines) contains spins σ(2) (blue) numbered by indices k and l. Additional four-spins interaction term involves the sum of products of values of the spins appearing within the dashed circle, its center being translated over all the crossings of the dashed and dotted lines.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05915_figures/2605.05915_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2: Plot of the relative variance RE as function of linear system size L on a log-linear scale for different values of parameters g0 and u0 (see the keys).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05915_figures/2605.05915_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Plot of the Binder cumulant U4 vs. temperature T in disordered Baxter model with four-spin coupling parameter (a) g0 = 0.2, and (b) g0 = −0.2 for various systems sizes (see the keys) and disorder strength √u0 = 0.2. The vertical dashed line in main frames denotes the value U4(T →0, L →∞) = 2/3. The inset in both panels magnifies the intersection region, where the estimated values of critical temperature Tc and U∗= U4(Tc) are indicated by vertical and horizontal dashed lines, respectively.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05915_figures/2605.05915_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Relative variance Re of total internal energy vs. system size L (on log-log scale) in disordered Baxter model with four-spin coupling strength (a) g0 = 0.2, and (b) g0 = −0.2 for different fixed temperatures (see the keys) and disorder strength √u0 = 0.2. The dashed line in main panels denotes the self-averaging law: Re ∝L−2. In the insets of both panels the power-law exponent v obtained from a fit of form Re ∝L−v is plotted against the temperature T. See main text for more details.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05915_figures/2605.05915_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: Relative variance Res of the critical internal energy vs. system size L in disordered Baxter model with four-spin coupling (a) g0 = 0.2, and (b) g0 = −0.2 for different estimates of regular part e0 (see the key) and fixed disorder strength √u0 = 0.2. Insets depict e vs. L at Tc. The dashed line denotes the fit of form (64).</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proves that the critical internal energy in the weakly-disordered Baxter model does not self-average, meaning fluctuations remain significant even in the thermodynamic limit. This implies that single large samples cannot represent the ensemble, necessitating averaging over many disorder realizations. The findings also provide numerical confirmation of the lack of self-averaging in the disordered Ising model.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the presence or absence of self-averaging for the critical internal energy in a weakly-disordered two-dimensional Baxter eight-vertex model.

**Main result.** The relative variance of the critical internal energy increases with system size and approaches a finite constant as L goes to infinity, demonstrating a lack of self-averaging for both the internal energy and free energy.

**Method.** The researchers used a combination of analytical replica and renormalization-group methods alongside extensive numerical Monte Carlo simulations and parallel tempering.

**Model / system.** A weakly-disordered two-dimensional Baxter eight-vertex model, represented as two ferromagnetic Ising models coupled by a four-spin interaction of strength g0, with Gaussian spatial disorder in the Ising couplings.

**Key observables.** Critical internal energy (E), relative variance (Re), Binder cumulant (U4), and specific heat (Cp).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling parameter g0 (determining the sign of the specific heat exponent), disorder strength (u0), and system size (L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on the weak disorder limit and small positive g0 for the analytical RG framework.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the lattice structure and four-spin interactions; Figure 2 depicts the relative variance Re increasing with system size L; additional figures show the scaling of energy and specific heat in the disordered Ising model.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of self-averaging, defines the disordered Baxter model Hamiltonian, presents analytical results using replica and RG methods, provides numerical verification via Monte Carlo simulations, and concludes with comparisons to the disordered Ising model.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the first two moments of the critical internal energy $E$ in a weakly disordered two-dimensional Baxter eight-vertex model as a function of the system size $L$, evaluated at the pseudo-critical point. Disorder is introduced via an equivalent representation of the pure eight-vertex model in terms of two ferromagnetic Ising models coupled by a four-spin interaction of strength $g_0$, where the Ising couplings consist of a uniform ferromagnetic part $J>0$ supplemented by weak Gaussian spatial disorder. In the critical regime, the model is formulated in terms of interacting Grassmann-Majorana spinor fields with quartic interactions and analyzed, for small positive $g_0$, using a combination of replica and renormalization-group methods. We also run extensive numerical simulations measuring the critical internal energy. Our results show that its relative variance increases with $L$ and approaches a finite constant as $L \to \infty$ for both $\pm g_0$. Hence, fluctuations remain relevant independently of the sign of $g_0$ (and thus of the specific-heat exponent), implying a lack of self-averaging of both the critical internal energy and the free energy. Consequently, reliable estimates of these quantities require averaging over many disorder realizations. In addition, we numerically confirm earlier predictions concerning the absence of self-averaging of the critical internal energy in the disordered Ising model.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** These lecture notes provide a mathematical tutorial connecting the physics of phase transitions and renormalization to the mechanics of neural networks. It highlights how concepts like energy landscapes and marginalization explain the behavior of models ranging from spin glasses to Large Language Models.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Establishing a pedagogical and mathematical bridge between classical statistical physics (phase transitions, renormalization group) and the framework of modern deep learning and large language models.

**Main result.** The notes demonstrate that the energy functions of spin-glass models are mathematically identical to Hopfield networks and that the process of integrating out hidden neurons in RBMs is analogous to the Renologization Group process.

**Method.** The author uses probability theory, the transfer matrix method, mean-field approximations, and Renormalization Group (RG) transformations to connect microscopic spin configurations to macroscopic thermodynamic properties and neural network architectures.

**Model / system.** The lecture notes cover the Ising model, spin-glass models (e.g., Edwards-Anderson), Hopfield networks, Boltzmann machines, and Transformer architectures used in Large Language Models.

**Key observables.** Partition function, free energy density, magnetization, two-point correlation functions, temperature, and scaling exponents.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (beta), coupling constants (J), external magnetic field (B), and scaling parameters for LLMs (number of parameters N, data size D, and compute C).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Initial treatments assume a finite configuration space; the 2D RG analysis uses a truncated subspace of interactions; and the LLM scaling analysis assumes power-law behavior.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 illustrates the RG flow of the 1D Ising model; Figure 4 shows a 2D lattice structure; and Figure 7 displays the power-law scaling of loss relative to parameters, data, and compute.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from basic probability and the Ising model to the Renormalization Group, then to Boltzmann machines and Hopfield networks, and finally to modern deep learning architectures and LLM scaling laws.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

These lecture notes introduce some topics of classical statistical physics, particularly those that are relevant for neural networks and deep learning. Statistical physics is treated as a branch of probability theory or statistics, with the goal of making concepts such as phase transitions and the renormalization group accessible to readers without prior knowledge of physics. We introduce the Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution and the thermodynamic potentials on a finite configuration space, notably for Ising spins and spin-glass models on a lattice, and then define phase transitions as discontinuities that arise in the limit that the number of lattice points goes to infinity. We further introduce Hopfield networks and Boltzmann machines, which are governed by the same energy function as spin-glass models, and discuss the learning algorithm for restricted Boltzmann machines. In this algorithm hidden neurons are integrated out as in the renormalization group. Finally, modern deep learning is introduced, whose early developments were in part motivated by restricted Boltzmann machines in that they carry many layers of hidden neurons. A description of large language models is given.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a mathematical framework for arranging quantum error-correcting code components into local 2D and 3D architectures. By 'unfolding' high-dimensional Reed-Muller codes, it enables more efficient magic state distillation with significantly reduced logical error rates.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to generalize the 'unfolding' of Reed-Muller distillation factories to achieve local 2D and 3D layouts for Z stabilizers, providing an algebraic basis for these structures.

**Main result.** The author derives 2D and 3D local layouts for Z stabilizers of distance 4 and distance 7 Reed-Muller codes and demonstrates significant improvements in magic state distillation fidelity.

**Method.** The work uses an algebraic framework involving polynomials over finite fields (F2) and Gray code ordering to transform high-dimensional hypercube descriptions into lower-dimensional local layouts.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on Quantum Reed-Muller (QRM) codes, specifically analyzing the stabilizer groups and logical operations of codes like QRM(4,1), QRM(6,1), and QRM(7,2) within a noise-biased model (e.g., cat qubits).

**Key observables.** Logical error probability, stabilizer group dimension, minimum distance (dZ), and output infidelity of distilled T and CCZ states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Input infidelity (p=10^-3), code distance, number of logical qubits, and the prefactor of the logical error probability.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The efficiency of the local layouts assumes a noise-biased model where the X stabilizer group does not necessarily require locality.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the geometric unfolding of stabilizers, showing 2D planar layouts, 3D 'Rubik's Cube' layouts, and the progression of building stabilizer bases using subcubes and Gray code ordering.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the unfolding problem, provides an algebraic derivation of the stabilizer structure, details the construction of 2D and 3D layouts, analyzes the logical action of Clifford hierarchy operators, and concludes with error performance analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We generalize the unfolding of a Reed Muller distillation factory of Ruiz et. al. by exhibiting the algebraic structure that the unfolding is based on. We describe a 2D local layout for the Z stabilizers of a distance 4 Reed Muller distillation factory and a 3D local layout for the Z stabilizer of a distance 4 and a distance 7 Reed Muller distillation factory. Given input T states with infidelities $p=10^{-3}$, the 2D local distillation factory with distance 4 outputs a CCZ state with infidelity $p=8.256 \times 10^{-9}$ and the 3D local distillation factory with distance 7 outputs a T state with infidelity $p=1.1811 \times 10^{-17}$.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a method to use interpretable machine learning to automate the discovery of efficient quantum circuit architectures. By analyzing the weights of a linear model, the authors derive a simplified, scalable algorithm for diagonal matrices that outperforms standard numerical decomposition techniques.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of decomposing large unitary operators into efficient, minimal sequences of elementary quantum gates and the lack of interpretability in standard black-box machine learning models.

**Main result.** The authors developed an interpretable machine learning workflow that discovers a universal, shortest analytic quantum algorithm for arbitrary diagonal matrices with a complexity of O(2^n), significantly more efficient than standard Qiskit numerical methods.

**Method.** A two-stage interpretable machine learning approach using a linear model (PyTorch) and Model-Weight Analysis (MWA) to extract mathematical hypotheses from raw circuit data, combined with PCA and clustering to filter redundant gate configurations.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on n-qubit quantum computing architectures, specifically targeting the decomposition of diagonal unitary operators within the U(2^n) group using gate sets like CNOT and single-qubit rotations.

**Key observables.** Gate counts (CNOT and rotations), circuit depth, Mean Squared Error (MSE), and R-squared score.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (n), rotation angles (within a small neighborhood epsilon around zero to ensure linearity), and learning rate hyperparameters (alpha and beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The mapping between circuit parameters and matrix elements is assumed to be linear; the training data is assumed to be noise-free; and the study focuses primarily on diagonal matrices rather than general unitaries.

**Figures summary.** Plots comparing gate count scaling (log-scale), workflow diagrams for raw and pretty data generation, PCA visualizations of parameter clusters, and convergence plots for loss and R-squared scores.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of gate decomposition, presents a two-stage ML workflow (Raw Data to Math Hypothesis), details the data generation and clustering techniques, demonstrates the discovery of optimized diagonal circuit schemes, and provides mathematical proofs for SGD convergence and gate bounds.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Machine learning nowadays becomes a useful instrument in many subjects. In this paper we use interpretable machine learning to build quantum algorithm. By studying the parameters of the machine learning algorithm we were able to construct universal shortest analytic quantum algorithm for arbitrary diagonal matrix of any size.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new variational quantum algorithm for computing eigenvalues and generalized eigenvalues of any complex matrix. By encoding matrix elements directly into quantum amplitudes and using ancilla measurements to clean the state, it avoids the need for complex Hermitian embedding or Trotterization. The method provides a scalable way to handle non-unitary problems in quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a variational quantum algorithm to calculate both standard eigenvalues and generalized eigenvalues for arbitrary N x N complex matrices, specifically addressing the non-unitarity obstacle.

**Main result.** The proposed algorithm achieves a loss function minimum of zero when matrices are transformed into upper triangular forms, with a circuit depth of O(N^2 log N) and a proven quadratic relationship between loss function accuracy and eigenvalue accuracy.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical variational approach using matrix encoding into probability amplitudes, ancilla measurement for garbage removal, and classical gradient optimization via parameter shifts.

**Model / system.** A quantum computing architecture utilizing n-qubit subsystems (R, C, L, chi, psi, etc.) to encode matrix elements and perform unitary transformations.

**Key observables.** The loss function (L) representing the deviation from upper triangular form, the normalization factor (G), and the success probability (p) of the measurement step.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Matrix dimension N, circuit depth O(N^2 log N), circuit size O(log N), and the relationship between eigenvalue accuracy (epsilon) and parameter encoding accuracy (sigma).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The matrices are assumed to be non-degenerate and complex; numerical simulations assume perfect encoding of optimization parameters to establish baseline performance.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the quantum circuit architecture and specific gate implementations; Figure 2 demonstrates the linear scaling of the iteration number with log(epsilon) and the quadratic scaling of the loss function accuracy.

**Paper structure.** The paper proposes the GEV algorithm, discusses the EV algorithm, analyzes algorithm characteristics and accuracy, and concludes with numerical simulations for 4x4 matrices.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a variational method for constructing the eigenvalues and generalized eigenvalues for an arbitrary $N\times N$ complex matrix. The quantum part of our algorithm is based on encoding the matrix elements into the pure state of a quantum system and expressing the loss function with optimization parameters in terms of certain probability amplitudes in the superposition state. The principal step of this algorithm is the measurement of the ancilla state   that removes all extra terms from the above superposition and allows to probabilistically construct the required loss function along with its derivatives with respect to the optimization parameters. These output data are used to find the new values of optimization parameters for the next iteration of the loss function in the gradient optimization method. The depth and size of the circuit for this algorithm are, respectively, $O(N^2 \log N)$ and $O(\log N)$.

</details>

### [Microsopic Theory of Spin Polarons in Chern Ferromagnets](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05292v1)

**Authors:** Qiang Gao, Eslam Khalaf  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05292v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The system size for (a,b) is 19×19. (c) Diagrammatics for the n = 2 normalization (and related) integrals in the geminal representation. </details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a microscopic foundation for understanding charged excitations in Chern ferromagnets, such as those found in moiré materials. It demonstrates how the interplay between interaction-induced dispersion and Berry curvature distribution determines whether spin polarons remain stable or unbind.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a microscopic theory for spin polarons in Chern ferromagnets and understanding how their stability and energy are affected by quantum geometry and interaction-induced dispersion.

**Main result.** The authors derive a variational family of wavefunctions that accurately capture spin polaron states, showing that interaction-generated dispersion is the primary cause of polaron unbinding.

**Method.** A microscopic variational approach using an antisymmetrized geminal product (AGP) of particle-hole wavefunctions, benchmarked against Exact Diagonalization in the Lowest Landau Level.

**Model / system.** A system of electrons in $C=1$ Chern bands (typical of moiré heterostructures) with $SU(2)$ spin symmetry, governed by a projected density-density interaction Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Binding energy, wavefunction overlap with exact results, total spin, and single-particle bandwidth.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of spin flips ($n$), screening length ($d$), quantum geometry non-uniformity ($\gamma$), and the variational size parameter ($L_c$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory assumes the existence of an ideal Chern band limit and utilizes a variational ansatz that may be limited by the choice of the single-particle dressing function.

**Figures summary.** Figures show binding energy and wavefunction overlap as functions of screening distance and size parameter, as well as the impact of Berry curvature non-uniformity on polaron stability.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the microscopic problem, develops the exact wavefunctions in the ideal limit, extends these to a variational framework for realistic bands, performs numerical benchmarking against ED, and analyzes the effects of quantum geometry and dispersion.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a microscopic theory of charged excitations in an SU(2) Chern ferromagnet and obtain closed-form wavefunctions for a hierarchy of charge-$e$ spin polaron states binding an arbitrary number of spin flips. In an ideal Chern-$1$ band with a normal-ordered contact interaction, we show that these polarons are exact eigenstates of the Hamiltonian with the same energy as single-hole excitations. Away from this ideal limit, we promote these states to a variational family by introducing a single size parameter and a geometry-informed single-particle dressing. Our momentum-space wavefunctions admit two equivalent representations: a ratio of Jastrow factors of Weierstrass functions of relative momenta or an antisymmetrized geminal product of particle-hole wavefunctions. The latter enables efficient evaluation of overlaps and expectation values for large system sizes and many spin flips. Benchmarking in the lowest Landau level, the single-spin-flip ansatz achieves $\gtrsim 99\%$ overlap with exact diagonalization and accurately captures binding energies, while the multi-spin-flip energies interpolate smoothly toward the large-texture (skyrmion) regime. For Chern bands with tunable quantum geometry, we find that interaction-generated single particle dispersion quickly destabilizes the spin polarons once quantum geometry becomes sufficiently non-uniform. When such dispersion is suppressed, however, the bound states persist deeper into the non-uniform regime, with the binding energy slowly decreasing and the bound state becoming larger as the quantum geometry becomes more concentrated. Our results provide a microscopic foundation for analyzing doped Chern ferromagnets in moiré platforms and lay the groundwork for variational wavefunctions of multi-polaron excitations and phases.

</details>

### [Parameter estimation for kappa distributions using the EM algorithm in the superstatistical framework](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05428v1)

**Authors:** Leonardo Sebastian Herrera, Sergio Davis  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05428v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new way to estimate parameters in kappa distributions, which are common in non-equilibrium plasma physics. By treating temperature fluctuations as a hidden variable, the authors turn a difficult optimization problem into a simple, efficient algorithm. This provides a more robust and mathematically clear alternative to traditional fitting methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Parameter estimation for kappa distributions is difficult because they do not belong to the exponential family, leading to transcendental equations that are numerically unstable and lack closed-form solutions.

**Main result.** The authors developed an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm that uses data augmentation to recover an exponential family structure, providing a computationally efficient and analytically closed-form method for estimating the spectral index.

**Method.** The method uses a data augmentation approach where the inverse temperature is treated as a latent gamma-distributed variable, allowing for an EM algorithm with an analytically tractable E-step and M-step.

**Model / system.** The model is based on the Beck-Cohen superstatistics framework, specifically applied to collisionless plasmas where particle velocities follow a kappa distribution derived from a hierarchical model with fluctuating inverse temperature.

**Key observables.** Particle velocities (or kinetic energies) and the fourth-to-second moment ratio (R).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spectral index (kappa), thermal velocity (v_th), shape parameter (alpha), scale parameter (theta), and the Maxwellian limit (kappa approaching infinity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The inverse temperature follows a gamma distribution, observations are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.), and the fourth velocity moment must exist for the specific moment-based initialization used.

**Figures summary.** The figures show velocity histograms compared to optimal distributions, the monotonic increase of log-likelihood, and the convergence of alpha and theta parameters across different kappa regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the failure of direct MLE, introduces the superstatistical hierarchical model, derives the EM algorithm via data augmentation, provides a moment-based initialization strategy, and validates the method using Monte Carlo simulations on synthetic data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Kappa distributions are widely used in space plasma physics to model velocity distribution functions with extended tails. However, parameter estimation in these distributions presents a fundamental challenge: the kappa distribution does not belong to the exponential family, which prevents the direct application of analytically tractable maximum likelihood methods. In this work we propose a solution to this problem based on data augmentation: we introduce the inverse temperature $β$ as a gamma-distributed latent variable, thereby recovering the exponential family structure in the complete-data likelihood. This enables an implementation of the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm in analytically closed form, with E-step and M-step derived from sufficient statistics. Our approach is agnostic with respect to the underlying physical mechanism generating fluctuations of $β$, which is a central aspect of the Beck-Cohen superstatistics framework, allowing a statistically rigorous treatment without compromising physical interpretability. We demonstrate that the method converges to the usual maximum likelihood estimators by applying it to synthetic data. Our results suggest that EM offers a computationally efficient and conceptually clear alternative for inference in superstatistical systems with temperature fluctuations.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.05625"></a>
### [Quantum Kernels for Parity-Structured Classification: A Hybrid Pipeline](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05625v1)

**Authors:** Tushar Pandey  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05625v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05625_figures/2605.05625_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 1. Test accuracy (mean ± std over 10 seeds, n = 11, flip_y= 0.22). The quantum ZZ kernel substantially outperforms all classical baselines; all five continuous methods collapse to near-random, confirming that the advantage is structural rather than incidental.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05625_figures/2605.05625_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Fig. 2. Kernel–target alignment comparison (n = 11). The quantum ZZ kernel shows ∼7× higher alignment with labels compared to RBF (0.094 vs. 0.013), consistent with the accuracy gap.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that quantum kernels can outperform classical methods specifically when tackling high-order parity-structured classification tasks. It identifies a clear complexity threshold where the quantum ZZ feature map's ability to capture multi-feature correlations provides a genuine advantage over classical RBF kernels.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether quantum kernels provide a genuine advantage over classical kernels for high-order parity (XOR) classification tasks, and determining if this advantage stems from the quantum circuit's expressivity or merely the feature encoding.

**Main result.** A phase transition in performance was identified: for low parity complexity (n <= 8), classical binary encoding dominates, but for high complexity (n >= 9), the quantum ZZ kernel achieves a significant advantage, outperforming classical methods by up to 12 percentage points.

**Method.** The study uses exact statevector simulations of a hybrid pipeline, comparing a ZZ quantum feature map against classical RBF SVMs and a 'binary encoding ablation' study to isolate circuit effects from encoding effects.

**Model / system.** A quantum feature map using n qubits with a ZZ structure (comprising single-qucept rotations and entangling Z_i Z_j gates) applied to synthetic 500-dimensional data with n-way XOR parity labels.

**Key observables.** Test accuracy, Kernel-Target Alignment (KTA), and parity complexity (n).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Parity complexity (n = 5 to 11), number of ZZ repetitions (r = 3), and label noise (22%).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes exact statevector simulation without hardware noise (shot noise, decoherence) and uses an 'oracle' feature selection method which may not reflect real-world feature discovery.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows the comparison of Kernel-Target Alignment, highlighting the 7x superiority of the quantum ZZ kernel over RBF at n=11; Table I illustrates the performance transition/complexity sweep.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the parity classification problem, describes the quantum and classical models/encoding, details the simulation methodology, presents the complexity sweep results and ablation study, and concludes with a discussion on the source of quantum advantage and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Parity (XOR) classification requires detecting discrete, high-order feature interactions that smooth classical kernels cannot efficiently capture. We study how quantum kernel advantage depends on parity complexity, the number of features entering the XOR rule, and find a clear threshold behavior. We pair a ZZ quantum feature map with binary {0, pi} encoding (features median thresholded before circuit input) to expose parity structure. A binary encoding ablation, RBF SVM trained on the identical {0, pi} features, separates encoding from circuit effects: at low complexity (n = 5 features), binary RBF achieves 83.4% +/- 1.7% and the quantum kernel 81.2% +/- 1.9%, showing encoding drives performance there. At high complexity (n = 11 features, 11 qubits, r = 3 ZZ repetitions), all classical methods collapse to near-random (approx. 50%), binary RBF reaches only 54.3% +/- 1.1%, and the quantum ZZ kernel achieves 66.3% +/- 3.2% (mean +/- std, 10 seeds), a +12.0 percentage-point margin over the binary ablation and approx. 7x higher kernel-target alignment (0.094 +/- 0.020 vs. 0.013 +/- 0.001). These results identify parity complexity as a concrete axis along which genuine quantum kernel advantage, not attributable to encoding alone, emerges.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.06093"></a>
### [Singularity Resolution in Quantum Cosmology via Page-Wootters Formalism](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06093v1)

**Authors:** Vishal, Malay K. Nandy  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06093v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06093_figures/2605.06093_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 1: Conditional probability density p(x; λ, µ) given by (36) for different values of the parameters λ and µ. Graphs in the left (right) column correspond to µ &lt; µmin (µ &gt; µmin) (the lower bounds µmin for different values of λ are shown in Table 1). The insets illustrate the presence (absence) of negative probability regions for µ &lt; µmin (µ &gt; µmin).</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper uses the Page-Wootters relational formalism to show how time and dynamics can emerge from a stationary quantum state in a Bianchi Type-I universe. It demonstrates that quantum effects effectively resolve the classical Big Bang singularity by making the probability of zero volume vanish. The study also establishes necessary constraints on the internal clock to maintain physically valid, positive probability densities.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the problem of time in canonical quantum gravity and the resolution of the classical Big Bang singularity in a timeless Wheeler-DeWitt framework.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that the conditional probability density of the universe's volume vanishes at the zero-volume limit, indicating quantum resolution of the singularity, while also identifying constraints on the clock variable to ensure positive probability density.

**Method.** The Page-Wootters relational formalism is used to derive dynamics by conditioning a global stationary state on a clock subsystem, employing a Gaussian superposition of momentum eigenstates and a Klein-Gordon type inner product.

**Model / system.** A plane-symmetric Bianchi Type-I universe within the Wheeler-DeWitt framework, modeled using Misner variables where the anisotropy variable beta acts as an internal clock and alpha represents the volume.

**Key observables.** The conditional probability density of the universe's volume (alpha) relative to the clock reading (beta).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The mean momentum (k0), the Gaussian wavepacket width (sigma), and the clock reading (beta_0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The universe is treated as a closed system without an external observer; the clock Hamiltonian is quadratic in momentum; and the clock does not interact with the remaining degrees of freedom.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 displays plots of the conditional probability density for the volume parameter.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of time and singularity, implements the Page-Wootters framework on a Bianchi Type-I model, constructs the wavefunctions and inner products, derives the probability densities, and analyzes the resulting constraints and singularity resolution.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the problem of classical big bang singularity in a plane-symmetric Bianchi type-I universe within the Wheeler-DeWitt (WDW) framework of quantum gravity. To address the problem of time, we employ the Page-Wootters formalism, which provides a relational notion of dynamics by conditioning the global state on a clock subsystem. Using Misner variables, the WDW equation assumes a Klein-Gordon (KG) type form. Its general solution is constructed as a Gaussian superposition of momentum eigenstates, resulting in an entangled global state between the clock and the remaining subsystem. Within this relational framework, we construct conditional states and obtain the corresponding probability density consistent with the KG-type inner product. The resulting conditional probability density vanishes in the limit of zero volume for all clock values, indicating quantum resolution of the classical singularity. We further show that positivity of the probability density imposes constraints on the admissible clock values, which depend on the parameters of the Gaussian wavepacket. These results highlight the essential role of quantum correlations in the emergence of relational dynamics, and demonstrate that the Page-Wootters formalism provides a consistent and nonsingular probabilistic description of quantum cosmology.

</details>

**Authors:** Theja N. De Silva  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06422v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

As temperature (kBT) decreases from 0.2t to 0.1t (Top to bottom: 0.2t, 0.18t, 0.15t, 0.1t), the secondary local minimum in the free energy becomes the global minimum indicating a first order phase transition from normal phase to superconducting phase.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores the complex phase diagram of a 3D Hubbard model on a BCC lattice, which serves as a model for alkali-doped fullerides. It reveals that the transition to superconductivity is first-order and identifies a multicritical region where Mott insulating, antiferromagnetic, and Fermi-liquid phases coexist. The work provides a unified perspective on how lattice geometry and electron-phonon-mediated interactions drive competing electronic orders.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the competition and interplay between superconducting, magnetic, and Mott insulating phases in a 3D BCC lattice, specifically motivated by alkali-doped fulleride molecular solids.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates a first-order normal-to-superconducting phase transition and identifies a temperature-interaction phase diagram in the strong-coupling regime featuring a narrow region where Fermi-liquid, antiferromagnetic, and Mott insulating phases compete.

**Method.** The authors employ two approaches: the Hatsugai-Kohmoto model supplemented by a BCS-type pairing term for the intermediate-coupling regime, and a spin-rotationally invariant slave-boson formalism for the strong-coupling regime.

**Model / system.** An effective Hubbard model on a body-centered-cubic (BCC) lattice, incorporating renormalized on-site Coulomb interactions (U_eff) and an effective inverted Hund's coupling (J_eff) originating from electron-phonon interactions.

**Key observables.** Superconducting order parameter (delta), critical temperature (T_C), quasiparticle weight, and free energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Effective on-site interaction (U_eff), effective Hund's coupling (J_eff), hopping amplitude (t), and the super-exchange interaction scale (J_ex).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Neglects off-site Coulomb interactions, spin-flip interactions, and orbital degrees of freedom; assumes diagonal electron hopping between molecular orbitals; uses the anti-adiabatic limit for electron-phonon interactions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows effective on-site interactions as a function of C60 volume; Figure 6 shows a three-phase structure for the BCC lattice similar to a 2D square lattice.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical motivation (fullerides), defines the effective Hubbard model, presents the intermediate-coupling analysis using the HK-BCS model, details the strong-coupling analysis using the slave-boson approach, and concludes with a discussion of the resulting phase diagram and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the electronic phases of an effective Hubbard model on the body-centered-cubic lattice, motivated by alkali-doped fulleride molecular solids. The model incorporates renormalized on-site interactions and an effective inverted Hund's coupling originating from electron-phonon interactions. To access complementary interaction regimes, we employ two theoretical approaches. In the intermediate-coupling regime, the on-site repulsive interaction is approximated by a long-range interaction in momentum space, yielding an exactly solvable Hatsugai-Kohmoto model supplemented by a BCS-type pairing term. Within this framework, we analyze the superconducting instability and demonstrate a first-order normal-superconducting phase transition, characterized by a discontinuous jump of the order parameter. In the strong-coupling regime, where pairing fluctuations are suppressed, we apply the spin rotationally invariant slave-boson formalism to map out the temperature-interaction phase diagram. This analysis reveals first-order transitions between a Fermi-liquid phase, an antiferromagnetic phase, and a Mott insulating phase, with a narrow intermediate region where all three phases compete. The resulting phase diagram captures the interplay of itinerancy, magnetic order, and Mott localization in three dimensions and provides a unified perspective on superconducting and correlation-driven phenomena in fulleride-inspired lattice systems.

</details>

### [Surface-Code Thresholds and Qubit Footprints in Shuttling-Based Spin-Qubit Railways](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05881v1)

**Authors:** Arun John Moncy, Reza Dastbasteh, Josu Etxezarreta Martinez, Ryo Nagai, Pedro M. Crespo, Normann Mertig, Charles Smith, Ruben M. Otxoa  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05881v1>

The different circles represent different quantum dot components: white circles are empty quantum dots, black circles represent data qubits, and blue and red circles are check qubits for detecting Z or X error.</sub>

The check qubit is initialized in |+⟩, accomplished by a Hadamard gate applied to |0⟩. It is sequentially entangled with the data qubits i, j, k, l using CNOT gates (for X operator of the sta- bilizer) and CZ gates (for Z operator of the stabilizer), before being measured in the X-basis. The red ‘X’ illustrates a single physical fault occurring on the check qubit mid-cycle, specif- ically between the second and third entangling operations. During the remainder of the circuit, this single fault propa- gates to the data qubits k and l, manifesting as a correlated weight-2 data error...</sub>

</details>

</details>

<img src="2605.05881_figures/2605.05881_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Three single qubit errors: two hook errors and a single qubit error on a data qubit (shown as red X’s), forming an undetectable logical error on a d = 5 CSS rotated surface code. This choice syndrome extraction order aligns the hook error along the direction of the logical X operator.</sub>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

The dark gray colored plaquettes and the light gray colored plaquettes have to have different set of extraction orders. The extraction orders in orange are suitable for all plaquettes.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how to implement fault-tolerant surface codes on a 1D-like 'railway' of silicon spin qubits that use electron shuttling to solve wiring constraints. It demonstrates that by moving check qubits instead of data qubits and using the XZZX code to match the inherent dephasing noise, the physical hardware required for large-scale quantum computing can be significantly reduced.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper provides deep insights into how tailoring topological error correction to the specific, asymmetric noise profiles of a physical platform (dephasing-biased transport) can drastically reduce the resource overhead for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Addressing the wiring fan-out bottleneck in large-scale 2D spin-qubit arrays by evaluating fault-tolerant surface codes on a restricted 2xN 'railway' architecture using electron shuttling.

**Main result.** Shuttling check qubits instead of data qubits improves thresholds, and using the XZZX surface code under dephasing-biased noise allows a distance-7 code to reach the 'Megaquop' regime with a 72% reduction in qubit footprint.

**Method.** Circuit-level noise modeling using the STIM package and Minimum-Weight Perfect Matching (MWPM) decoding to evaluate error thresholds and logical error rates.

**Model / system.** A 2xN silicon spin-qubit 'railway' architecture utilizing coherent electron shuttling, modeled with a time-dependent Hamiltonian including external magnetic fields, micromagnet gradients, and Spin-Orbit Coupling (Rashba and Dresselhaus).

**Key observables.** Error thresholds (physical error rate p), logical error rates, noise bias (ratio of Z to X/Y errors), and qubit footprint (hardware overhead).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code distance (d=3 to 17), noise bias (eta), idling factor, and physical error rate (p = 10^-3 to 3*10^-3).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Quasi-static noise limit, Pauli-twirled approximation for uncorrelated errors, and a unidirectional 'Train Schedule' for syndrome extraction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the 2xN railway architecture schematic; Figure 2 shows a d=5 XZZX lattice; Figure 3 illustrates stabilizer extraction and hook errors; Figure 5/6 demonstrate the optimized extraction order and 'Train Schedule'.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the wiring bottleneck and railway architecture, defines the physical Hamiltonian and noise model, presents the syndrome extraction protocol (Train Schedule), compares CSS vs. XZZX codes under various noise biases, and concludes with hardware footprint analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a fault-tolerant mapping of rotated surface codes onto a $2\times N$ silicon spin-qubit railway architecture, utilizing electron shuttling to resolve the wiring fan-out bottleneck. Employing circuit-level noise modeling, we evaluate threshold performances across various noise biases. We demonstrate that shuttling check qubits instead of data qubits fundamentally improves system thresholds. Crucially, under a noise model biased towards dephasing for spin-qubit shuttling, the non-CSS XZZX surface code outperforms standard CSS variants. By tailoring the topological code to this specific inherent bias, we show that the Megaquop footprint is achievable with a distance 7 code requiring a $p = 10^{-3}$ physical error rate, highlighting a pathway for substantial hardware reductions in early fault-tolerant quantum processors.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new algebraic method to systematically find exact solutions to the non-linear Yang-Mills equations. By using tensor ring decomposition, the authors derive stable configurations like color waves and flux tubes that provide analytical insights into non-perturbative phenomena like mass gap generation and confinement.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of extracting exact classical solutions from the non-linear partial differential equations of Yang-Mills theory and understanding the stability of these solutions against tachyonic vacuum instabilities.

**Main result.** The authors successfully extracted exact solutions for SU(2) and SU(3) gauge theories, including relativistic color waves and dynamical flux tubes, and proved their structural stability against the Savvidy instability.

**Method.** An algebraic tensor ring decomposition framework that maps non-linear PDEs into tractable differential-algebraic systems using differential ideals and quotient rings (elliptic and Artinian).

**Model / system.** Non-Abelian Yang-Mills theory (specifically SU(2) and SU(3) sectors) in Minkowski spacetime, featuring configurations such as relativistic color waves, helical dyonic flux tubes, and chaotic SU(3) resonances.

**Key observables.** Energy-momentum tensor (energy density, momentum flux), field strength components, effective mass, and isospin charge.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temporal frequency (omega), longitudinal wavenumber (k), azimuthal winding number (n), geometric coupling constant (gamma), and the temporal dominance condition (M^2 > 0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the validity of evaluating the system over specific algebraic rings and that certain field components can be constrained to proportional rays.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the workflow from PDEs to exact solutions; Figure 3 displays radial profiles of SU(2) helical flux tubes; Figure 4 shows 3D orbital trajectories of SU(3) chaotic attractors and the kinetic cancellation mechanism.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the algebraic tensor ring decomposition framework, applies it to SU(2) color waves and helical flux tubes to demonstrate mass gap and screening, extends the analysis to SU(3) dynamics and chaotic oscillators, and concludes with a semiclassical stability analysis against the Savvidy instability.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The non-linear nature of Yang-Mills theory presents a challenge for extracting exact classical solutions, which are useful for understanding non-perturbative vacuum structures. In this paper, an algebraic tensor ring decomposition framework is introduced to systematically map the non-linear partial differential equations (PDEs) of Yang-Mills theory into tractable differential-algebraic systems. By promoting static pure-gauge backgrounds to dynamical variables, the reference state acts as a geometric template whose Maurer-Cartan forms generate the algebraic cross-terms necessary to stabilize non-linear self-interactions. To analytically resolve the resulting differential ideals, specific differential-algebraic quotient rings are employed as evaluation tools, and the solution space is organized by an algebraic bifurcation analysis. Applying this framework, three distinct classes of exact solutions are extracted: (i) relativistic $SU(2)$ color waves evaluated over an elliptic quotient ring, where the differential ideal bifurcates into a Decoupled Branch and two Coupled Branches, the latter exhibiting mass gap generation; (ii) dynamical dyonic flux tubes obtained from a time-dependent helical template, where the Gauss law ideal bifurcates the system into Coulomb, Dyonic, and symmetric Meissner branches. In the Meissner branch, an Artinian asymptotic truncation yields Bessel-type exponential screening, stabilized by a temporal dominance condition; and (iii) dynamical $SU(3)$ configurations where the Gauss law ideal bifurcates the solution space into four distinct phases. The non-trivial branches enforce a kinetic cancellation mechanism that maps the amplitude dynamics onto a generalized $x^2y^2$ chaotic oscillator. Across these settings, the framework provides a methodical approach to characterize the classical solution space of strongly coupled gauge theories.

</details>

<img src="2605.06367_figures/2605.06367_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4: Imbalance can reverse the order of learning for classes. In both subfigures, solid lines represent the- oretical predictions derived in Appendix G, while crosses represent empirical average gaps from gradient descent simulations. Parameters: χp = 60.0, χm = 30.0, t = 0.01, N = 100, Nepoch = 2 × 106, learning rate η = 5 × 10−5, N train run = 50.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical and empirical framework to explain how data heterogeneity and imbalance cause diffusion models to learn different classes at different rates. It reveals that class variance is the dominant factor in determining whether a model generalizes or memorizes a specific sub-population first.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding how class-specific data structures (variance, geometry) and sampling imbalance shape the training dynamics, specifically the timing of generalization and memorization in diffusion models.

**Main result.** Established a hierarchy where class variance is the primary driver of learning order, while sampling imbalance acts as a modulator that can reverse this order and delay the learning of minority classes.

**Method.** A high-dimensional analytical framework using Random Matrix Theory and Gaussian Equivalence, validated against numerical simulations of Random Feature models and U-Net diffusion models on Fashion MNIST and CIFAR.

**Model / system.** The study analyzes score-based diffusion models (DDPM) and Random Feature models trained on heterogeneous Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) representing imbalanced datasets.

**Key observables.** Feature-covariance spectrum, class-specific speciation times, generalization and memorization windows, and memorization fraction based on nearest-neighbor distance ratios.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Class variance, centroid geometry (norms), sampling imbalance (mixing proportions), diffusion time (t), and training time (tau).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Data follows a Gaussian Mixture Model; the analysis relies on high-dimensional asymptotic limits; the model uses a random feature architecture; classes are assumed to be well-separated.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate how imbalance induces separation in speciation times, show the alignment between theoretical error curves and simulations, and demonstrate the correlation between memorization gaps and class variance/geometry.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of class-dependent dynamics, develops a high-dimensional analytical framework using RMT, provides theoretical derivations for spectral edges and error dynamics, and validates these findings through large-scale experiments on Fashion MNIST and CIFAR datasets.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Real-world datasets are inherently heterogeneous, yet how per-class structural differences and sampling imbalance shape the training dynamics of diffusion models-and potentially exacerbate disparities-remains poorly understood. While models typically transition from an initial phase of generalization to memorizing the training set, existing theory assumes homogeneous data, leaving open how class imbalance and heterogeneity reshape these dynamics. In this work, we develop a high-dimensional analytical framework to study class-dependent learning in score-based diffusion models. Analyzing a random-features model trained on Gaussian mixtures, we derive the feature-covariance spectrum to characterize per-class generalization and memorization times. We reveal the explicit hierarchy governing these dynamics: class variance is the primary determinant of learning order-consistently favoring higher-variance classes-while centroid geometry plays a secondary role. Sampling imbalance acts as a modulator that can reverse this ordering and, under strong imbalance, forces minority classes to acquire distinct, delayed speciation times during backward diffusion. Together, these results suggest that diffusion models can memorize some classes while others remain insufficiently learned. We validate our theoretical predictions empirically using U-Net models trained on Fashion MNIST.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.05533"></a>
### [Thermodynamics and emergent thermomechanical response of a quantum ring with nonminimal spin--orbit coupling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05533v1)

**Authors:** João A. A. S. Reis, L. Lisboa-Santos, Edilberto O. Silva  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05533v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05533_figures/2605.05533_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Single-particle canonical partition function Z1 as a function of temperature. The dashed curve represents the di- rect numerical evaluation obtained by summing over a large number of energy levels, while the solid curves show succes- sive truncations of the Euler–Maclaurin expansion at zeroth, first, and second order. Already at first order, the analytic approximation is virtually indistinguishable from the exact result over the entire temperature range, confirming that the method captures both the low-temperature discreteness and the high-temperature continuum limit with remarkable preci- sion.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05533_figures/2605.05533_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Entropy per quantum ring as a function of tem- perature for representative values of ξ. Finite values of the effective coupling unlock the first excited states at lower tem- peratures, producing an earlier and steeper onset of entropy compared with the uncoupled case.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05533_figures/2605.05533_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Helmholtz free energy per quantum ring as a function of temperature for representative values of the effective cou- pling ξ. The monotonic decrease reflects the growth of ther- mally accessible microstates as temperature increases. Larger values of ξ lower the free energy throughout the plotted range, with the most pronounced separation occurring at low tem- perature, where only the first few mesoscopic branches con- tribute.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05533_figures/2605.05533_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Internal energy per quantum ring versus tempera- ture for several representative values of ξ. The vertical off- set between curves originates from the ξ-dependent shift of the ground-state energy, while the common positive slope at higher temperatures reflects the progressive thermal occupa- tion of excited angular-momentum states.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05533_figures/2605.05533_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 6. Canonical magnetization analog M = −∂F/∂ξ as a function of temperature for representative couplings. The quantity measures the thermodynamic response of the free energy to variations of the effective Rashba parameter rather than to a physical magnetic flux.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how spin-orbit coupling in a quantum ring alters its thermodynamic and mechanical properties. It shows that these interactions can induce measurable effects like mechanical instabilities and anomalous thermal contraction, providing a way to probe spin-orbit coupling through macroscopic measurements.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how nonminimal spin-orbit coupling deforms the energy spectrum of a 1D quantum ring and how this deformation manifests in macroscopic thermodynamic and thermomechanical response functions.

**Main result.** The coupling parameter xi reorganizes the energy spectrum, leading to mesoscopic de Haas-van Alphen-like oscillations in the grand-canonical ensemble and even anomalous thermal contraction in an interacting model.

**Method.** The authors use the Euler-Maclaurin summation formula to derive analytical expressions for partition functions in both canonical and grand-canonical ensembles, and introduce a phenomenological exponential resummation for interacting effects.

**Model / system.** A 1D quantum ring of radius r0 containing fermions, subject to effective spin-orbit interactions induced by nonminimal couplings to antisymmetric tensor fields.

**Key observables.** Internal energy, entropy, heat capacity, magnetization analog, susceptibility analog, persistent spin current, effective pressure, isothermal compressibility, and thermal expansion coefficient.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin-orbit coupling strength (xi), temperature (T), chemical potential (mu), and ring circumference (L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The electrons are initially treated as non-interacting; the interacting model is phenomenological; the ring radius is treated as a quasi-static thermodynamic variable for mechanical analysis.

**Figures summary.** Figures demonstrate the accuracy of the Euler-Maclaurin expansion, the monotonic decrease of free energy, the sigmoid growth of entropy, the nonmonotonic behavior of magnetization and spin current, and the presence of mechanical instabilities and peaks in compressibility and expansion coefficients.

**Paper structure.** The paper moves from the derivation of the energy spectrum to the construction of thermodynamic partition functions, followed by the development of the thermomechanical framework and an extension into interacting systems.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the thermodynamic and emergent thermomechanical properties of fermions confined to a one-dimensional quantum ring with effective spin--orbit interactions induced by nonminimal couplings to antisymmetric tensor fields. Using the exact spectrum obtained in the companion work, we develop canonical and grand-canonical descriptions and show that the coupling parameter~$ξ$ deforms the angular-momentum branches, reorganizing the low-energy spectrum and leaving clear signatures in the internal energy, entropy, heat capacity, and spin--orbit response functions. We also formulate an effective thermomechanical description by treating the ring circumference as a quasi-static thermodynamic variable. This leads to a pressure-like quantity and associated response coefficients, directly linked to the microscopic spectrum. In the grand-canonical ensemble, Fermi statistics strongly enhance the response, producing coupling-dependent instabilities and sign changes reminiscent of mesoscopic de~Haas--van Alphen oscillations. Finally, we introduce a phenomenological interacting extension based on an exponential resummation of the free energy, showing that collective effects can sharpen the thermomechanical response and induce anomalous thermal contraction. Our results connect spectral deformation, finite-size thermodynamics, and emergent mechanical behavior in spin--orbit-active quantum rings.

</details>

**Authors:** David T. S. Perkins, Joseph J. Betouras  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06551v1>

Shaded regions indicate the high-symmetry point of interest in panel (e). (c): Band structure for θ =...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a theoretical framework for understanding twisted kagome bilayers, identifying specific 'higher-order magic angles' where electronic bands become extremely flat. It reveals that twisting these layers can induce non-trivial topological phases and higher-order Van Hove singularities, offering a new platform for studying correlated physics.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the emergence of higher-order magic angles, topological flat bands, and the role of sublattice interference in twisted kagome bilayers (TBK).

**Main result.** The study demonstrates the emergence of higher-order magic angles where significant band flattening and higher-order Van Hove singularities occur, and shows that twisting can induce non-trivial topology.

**Method.** A generalized Bistritzer-MacDonald continuum model is developed by projecting the kagome monolayer Hamiltonian onto the Dirac subspace and truncating the tunneling Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** The system is a twisted kagome bilayer (TBK) heterostructure near 1/3 filling, modeled using a low-energy continuum Hamiltonian that incorporates interlayer tunneling and $p_z$-orbital-based parameters.

**Key observables.** Fermi velocity (vF), band flattening, Van Hove singularities (VHS), higher-order Van Hove singularities (HOVHS), and valley Chern numbers.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Twist angle (theta), interlayer tunneling energy scale (omega_0), layer separation (d_perp), and 1/3 filling regime.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the physics is dominated by the Dirac cones (neglecting higher-energy bands), uses a truncated tunneling Hamiltonian, and assumes rapid decay of interlayer tunneling with momentum.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows kagome geometry and MBZ construction; Fig 2 displays band structures, contour plots, and velocity renormalization; Fig 3 shows layer-resolved sublattice projections.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the TBK system and geometry, develops a generalized continuum model, analyzes the emergence of magic angles and higher-order singularities, investigates topological properties and symmetry breaking, and discusses the role of sublattice interference.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a low-energy continuum model to describe the moiré physics of heterostructures, which is a generalization of the celebrated Bistritzer-MacDonald (BM) method [R. Bistritzer and A. H. MacDonald, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 108, 12233 (2011)]. We take as an example the moiré physics of electrons in twisted bilayer kagomé (TBK) metals near $1/3$ filling where monolayer Dirac cones lie. We demonstrate the emergence of higher-order magic angles where significant local band flattening occurs as a high-order Van Hove singularity emerges and show how twisting alone can induce non-trivial topology. We, furthermore, show that while sublattice interference effects are present, their role is not as prominent as in monolayer kagome.

</details>

The vertical dotted line marks the analytical percolation threshold ⟨k⟩c.
