**Summary.** This paper presents a new renormalization method for bipartite networks that preserves their two-mode structure during coarse-graining. It shows that traditional one-mode projections fail to capture the true multiscale hierarchy of complex systems, especially when interactions depend on long-range alternating paths.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Standard one-mode projections of bipartite networks collapse the two-mode architecture, leading to a loss of multiscale hierarchical information and a distortion of the underlying diffusion geometry.

**Main result.** The authors introduce a bipartite-preserving Laplacian renormalization framework (b-LRG) that reveals hidden hierarchies and demonstrates that while structural imbalance reshapes network geometry, it does not alter the universality class of the percolation transition.

**Method.** A Laplacian-based renormalization group (b-LRG) approach using diffusion dynamics on the graph Laplacian, combined with information-theoretic metrics (AMI/NMI) and finite-size scaling analysis.

**Model / system.** Bipartite networks with two distinct node classes, including controlled random bipartite ensembles at criticality and empirical networks such as host-virus, plant-pollinator, and social affiliation networks.

**Key observables.** Giant component size (P_inf), susceptibility (chi), spectral gap function (Psi), spectral specific heat (C), and effective diffusion-mediated distance (D_ij).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Diffusion timescale (tau), partition asymmetry (alpha = N_A/N_B), and average degree (k).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The renormalization process assumes that clustering can be restricted to nodes within the same class to prevent the creation of artificial intra-class links.

**Figures summary.** Comparison of standard vs. bipartite renormalization; finite-size scaling of the percolation transition; dendrograms showing hierarchical organization; and information-theoretic comparisons (AMI/NMI) between bipartite and projected networks.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of projection-induced distortion, presents the b-LRG framework, analyzes universality and geometry in controlled bipartite ensembles, and validates the framework using various empirical bipartite networks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Many complex systems are organized around complementary roles and naturally described as bipartite networks. Unveiling their multiscale structure presents a fundamental challenge because coarse-graining procedures must preserve role separation, whereas standard approaches collapse it via one-mode projections. Here we introduce a Laplacian-based renormalization framework that operates directly on the bipartite architecture, enabling scale transformations while retaining role differentiation. Using controlled bipartite ensembles at criticality, we show that structural imbalance systematically reshapes organization across scales while leaving scaling properties invariant, revealing a separation between universality and geometry. Applying the coarse-graining framework to empirical bipartite networks, we uncover nontrivial multiscale hierarchies for both roles. In contrast, renormalization performed after one-mode projection -- which truncates diffusion paths to nearest neighbors -- yields qualitatively different structures. Our results identify two-mode geometry as a fundamental constraint for revealing multiscale organization in systems with role separation.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

</details>

**Authors:** L. Hackner, A. R. Myatt, W. Wustmann, N. J. Lambert  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05576v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **5/5** · 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5** · `interference shaping light` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5**

(b) |S21| of uncoupled cavity mode spectrum under single tone frequency modulation as function of drive frequency Ω/2π, with ωc/2π = 2.615 GHz, showing sidebands with min- ima coincident with Bessel function zeros (top axis, dotted lines.) (c) |S21| as a function of applied magnetic field, with ωc/2π =...</sub>

Rows from top to bottom correspond to a2/2π = 10 MHz, a2/2π = 25 MHz, a2/2π = 40 MHz. Left column shows experimental data; right column – input-output theory (see main text).</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.05576_figures/2605.05576_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>

Rows from top to bottom correspond to ϕ = 0, ϕ = π/2, ϕ = π, ϕ = 3π/2. Left column shows experimental data; right column – input- output theory.</sub>

**Summary.** The paper presents a method for Floquet engineering of magnon-polaritons by modulating the microwave cavity frequency instead of the magnetic field. This approach enables larger modulation depths and allows for complex spectrum manipulation through multi-frequency drives. The study reveals new features like additional anticrossings and the formation of dark modes.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a new way to control quasi-energy levels and sideband dynamics in hybrid systems using multi-tone driving, specifically highlighting interference-induced dark modes.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Traditional Floquet engineering of magnon-polaritons via magnetic field modulation is experimentally difficult due to the requirement for large-amplitude, rapid frequency tuning. The authors seek an alternative method using cavity frequency modulation to achieve larger modulation depths and bandwidths.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that modulating the microwave cavity frequency allows for effective Floquet engineering, including the creation of new anticrossings between previously uncoupled sidebands using multi-frequency commensurate drives. They also observe the emergence of 'dark' modes due to destructive interference between sidebands.

**Method.** The researchers use an arbitrary waveform generator and an IQ demodulator to apply sinusoidal frequency modulation to a microwave cavity frequency. The transmission spectrum is measured using a vector network analyzer (VNA) and analyzed using Floquet matrix formalism and cavity input-output theory.

**Model / system.** A hybrid cavity electromagnonic system consisting of a microwave resonator (coplanar waveguide loop) coupled to a Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) sphere in the strong coupling regime. The system is described by a time-periodic Hamiltonian involving a time-varying cavity frequency.

**Key observables.** Transmission coefficient (|S21|), sideband appearance, and anticrossing gaps (mode splitting).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling rate (g/2pi approx 20.7 MHz), cavity/magnon frequencies (approx 2.17 GHz), modulation frequency (5 to 180 MHz), and modulation amplitude (10 to 50 MHz).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Floquet matrix is truncated at sideband orders n = +/- 8; the system is modeled using cavity input-output theory.

**Figures summary.** The figures likely show the transmission spectrum (S21) under single-frequency modulation, the appearance of sidebands, and the complex spectrum resulting from multi-frequency (m=2, 3) modulation including new anticrossings.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of magnetic modulation, describes the experimental setup and cavity modulation technique, presents results for single-tone Floquet engineering, extends the study to multi-frequency commensurate drives, and concludes with the implications for hybrid system manipulation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Floquet engineering of cavity magnon-polaritons by periodically modulating the magnon frequency has recently attracted much interest as a way to manipulate the energy spectrum of magnon-photon hybrid systems. However, modulating the frequency of magnons by a time-varying bias magnetic field can be challenging. We demonstrate cavity magnon-polariton Floquet engineering by modulating the microwave cavity frequency, allowing large modulation depth and bandwidth. We apply commensurate two-frequency Floquet modulations with the higher frequency at twice and three times the lower frequency, and demonstrate that the resulting spectrum depends on the relative amplitude and phase of the two drive tones. In comparison with single-frequency Floquet modulations, the spectrum has qualitatively different features; in particular, new anticrossings appear between previously uncoupled sidebands. Our platform offers an alternative way to manipulate Floquet quasi-energy levels in hybrid systems.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Electrical Spin Pumping in Exchange-coupled Molecules](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06450v1)

**Authors:** Paul Greule, Wantong Huang, Kwan Ho Au-Yeung, Máté Stark, Johannes Schwenk, Christoph Sürgers, Wolfgang Wernsdorfer, Philip Willke  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06450v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **2/5**

For ESR measurements an external magnetic field B is applied and a magnetic tip apex is used. A DC bias voltage VDC and a radio-frequency voltage VRF are applied to the tip which is positioned above the sensing spin SA to probe and drive its spin state. SA is exchange coupled to a second, remote spin SB. (B) STM topography of a molecule pair [Image conditions: 3 nm x 3 nm, I = 10 pA, VDC = −100 mV]. The cross marks the measurement position used in (D). (C) Energy level diagram of the spin system, showing the configurations with the remote spin SB in the ground...</sub>

**Summary.** The researchers demonstrated a way to remotely initialize the spin state of a molecule using an electrical current from an STM tip. By using exchange coupling between two molecules, they showed that angular momentum can be transferred to a 'remote' spin, allowing for all-electrical control. This approach provides a scalable method for initializing qubits in molecular-based quantum architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates a practical mechanism for non-equilibrium state preparation in a coupled spin system, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and the control of many-body spin dynamics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for reliable, efficient, and all-electrical initialization of electron spin states in single molecules for quantum information processing, overcoming thermal equilibrium limits.

**Main result.** Demonstrated remote, all-electrical initialization of a single electron spin using spin-polarized tunneling current, where exchange coupling enables angular momentum transfer to a remote spin.

**Method.** Electron Spin Resonance Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (ESR-STM) was used to probe spin transitions, combined with rate equation modeling and second-order tunneling simulations.

**Model / system.** A pair of S=1/2 molecules (Fe-FePc) on an MgO/Ag(100) surface, modeled by an effective spin Hamiltonian including Zeeman terms and anisotropic exchange coupling (J_parallel and J_perp).

**Key observables.** Resonance frequencies, peak amplitudes, remote spin magnetization (M_B), and change in tunneling current (delta I).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange coupling strength (|J_perp| approx 220-621 MHz), tip spin polarization (eta approx 0.2-0.5), and spin lifetime (T1 approx 1-10 microseconds).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The sensing spin is assumed to fully adapt to the tip's spin polarization; dipole-dipole interactions are neglected and absorbed into effective exchange parameters.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows voltage dependence of ESR spectra; Figure B shows ESR spectra at avoided level crossing; Figure C shows remote spin magnetization vs. tunneling current and the relationship between I0 and exchange coupling; Figure S8/S9 show temperature-dependent pumping and spin chain transfer simulations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of spin initialization, describes the ESR-STM experimental setup and molecular system, presents the experimental demonstration of remote spin pumping, provides a theoretical model for the spin dynamics and exchange-mediated transfer, and concludes with discussions on scalability and temperature effects.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electron spins in single molecules are a promising platform for quantum information processing. However, their practical implementation as qubits requires reliable control at the single-entity level, including an efficient state initialization. Here, we demonstrate the remote, all-electrical initialization of the electron spin in single molecules: Using electron spin resonance scanning tunneling microscopy, we investigate coupled pairs of S=1/2 molecules (Fe-FePc), where one molecule serves as a readout and pumping unit for the neighboring one. We show that the exchange interaction between them enables angular momentum transfer, which allows for the control of the remote spin state via the direction and magnitude of the spin-polarized tunneling current and the exchange coupling strength. These results establish a general, all-electrical approach for remote spin initialization that is readily transferable to a wide range of spin-based quantum architectures.

</details>

FSS value obtained by the fitting: 69 ± 9 µeV. e) Time-resolved photoluminescence for the X and XX lines with decay times τX 1 = 1.06 ns, τX 2 = 2.16 ns and τXX 1 = 0.48 ns, τXX 2 = 2.21 ns. The ratio of around 1.8 is close to 2, expected in the...</sub>

**Summary.** The researchers developed a local droplet etching method to grow highly symmetric InGaAs quantum dots for telecom-wavelength light emission. These dots show excellent single-photon purity and low fine-structure splitting, making them promising candidates for scalable quantum communication technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a scalable fabrication route for high-purity, symmetric single-photon sources at the critical telecom C-band wavelength.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for reproducible, symmetric, and low-density single-photon emitters at telecom C-band wavelengths for quantum communication, overcoming the asymmetry and high fine-structure splitting found in standard Stranski-Krastanov grown quantum dots.

**Main result.** Demonstrated successful fabrication of high-quality InGaAs/InAlAs quantum dots using local droplet etching (LDE) that exhibit high single-photon purity (g2(0) < 0.09) and high in-plane symmetry in the telecom C-band.

**Method.** The study combines experimental MOVPE growth via local droplet etching, structural characterization (AFM, TEM, STEM, EDS), optical spectroscopy (micro-PL, TRPL, HBT), and numerical modeling (8-band k.p and configuration-interaction methods).

**Model / system.** Epitaxial InGaAs quantum dots embedded in InAlAs nanoholes on an InP substrate, characterized by a symmetric cone-like structure and a dome-shaped cap.

**Key observables.** Second-order autocorrelation g2(0), fine-structure splitting (FSS), degree of linear polarization (DOLP), photoluminescence linewidth, radiative decay time, and activation energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Telecom C-band (1500-1620 nm), QD density of 10^9/cm^2, in-plane aspect ratio of 1.14, and FSS values around 40-70 microeV.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The numerical modeling assumes a continuum elasticity theory for strain and uses a finite single-particle basis for the configuration-interaction approach.

**Figures summary.** Figures show nanohole and QD morphology via AFM/STEM, elemental composition via EDS, micro-PL spectra, polarization-resolved spectroscopy, and time-resolved photoluminescence decay.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the LDE growth approach, details the structural and compositional characterization of the resulting nanostructures, presents the optical performance and single-photon statistics, and concludes with theoretical modeling of carrier confinement and stability.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Significant progress in quantum light sources for quantum communication applications requires reproducible and symmetric quantum emitters acting as single-photon sources capable of generating entangled photons on demand at specific telecom wavelengths. Here, we propose telecom-emitting epitaxial quantum dots (QDs) fabricated using the local droplet etching (LDE) approach. The resulting well-defined, low-density ($10^9$/cm$^2$) QDs based on In$_{x}$Ga$_{1-x}$As are formed in symmetric LDE nanoholes (in-plane aspect ratio of 1.14) in In$_{0.52}$Al$_{0.48}$As. Detailed transmission electron microscopy provides comprehensive insight into the structural integrity, interface quality, and compositional profiles of the QDs, which underpin their promising optical properties. Photoluminescence spectroscopy reveals narrow emission lines (0.2 meV) and high optical quality, while second-order autocorrelation measurements confirm clear single-photon emission, with $g^{(2)}(0)=0.07\pm0.02$ under above-band continuous-wave excitation and $g^{(2)}(0)=0.16 \pm 0.18$ under pulsed excitation. Precise numerical modeling, combining multiband $\boldsymbol{k} \cdot \boldsymbol{p}$ and configuration-interaction methods, supports the optical characterization and identifies thermal excitation pathways that explain the persistence of emission up to liquid-nitrogen temperatures. These results highlight the versatility of the LDE approach for integrating new material systems and pave the way toward scalable fabrication of quantum light sources with tailored emission properties.

</details>

### [Soliton gas resolution and statistics of random wave fields in semiclassical integrable turbulence](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06306v1)

**Authors:** T. Congy, G. A. El  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06306v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

For reference the dashed green line represents the initial condition in (b) and (c); (d) comparison between the numerically evaluated PDF for several values of a (color markers) and the corresponding PDF evaluated using Eqs. (22), (25) (solid lines). The dashed black line represents the exponential PDF e−ρ (Gaussian single-point statistics for the fNLSE field amplitude); (e) comparison between the numerically evaluated moment ratio q2α(23) (red markers) and Γ(α + 1) (solid...</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.06306_figures/2605.06306_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

**Summary.** This paper provides a new analytical framework to predict the statistical distribution of intensities in nonlinear wave fields dominated by solitons. By using a stochastic version of the inverse scattering transform, the authors successfully map initial random states to long-time asymptotic distributions. This result is applicable to a wide range of nonlinear physical phenomena, including optics and fluid dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The development of a statistical framework for integrable turbulence and the derivation of exact PDFs for extreme events (rogue waves) is highly relevant to understanding non-equilibrium dynamics and wave-particle interactions in many-body systems and nonlinear optics.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the probability density function (PDF) and statistical properties of random nonlinear wave field intensities in the long-time, semiclassical integrable turbulence regime.

**Main result.** The authors develop an analytical framework using a stochastic inverse scattering transform to derive an explicit integral representation for the asymptotic PDF, showing excellent agreement with numerical simulations.

**Method.** The study employs a stochastic analogue of the inverse scattering transform (SST) and compares analytical predictions with direct numerical simulations using a pseudo-spectral, fourth-order Runge-Kutta method.

**Model / system.** The focusing Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (fNLSE) in the semiclassical regime, representing physical systems such as water waves, nonlinear optics, and superfluids.

**Key observables.** Probability density function (PDF) of wave field intensity, spectral density of states (DOS), and statistical moments such as skewness and kurtosis.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Semiclassical limit (slowly varying initial states), spectral support (Lambda), initial intensity PDF, and the amplitude of initial periodic modulation (a).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is assumed to be integrable, the initial state is slowly varying (semiclassical limit), and the long-time state is approximated by a bound-state soliton gas.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the numerical evolution of wave intensity and compares numerical PDFs to analytical predictions; Figure 2 illustrates the temporal evolution of spatial averages and the transition to a homogeneous regime.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear wave statistics, proposes the soliton gas resolution conjecture, develops the stochastic inverse scattering transform framework, validates the theory with specific cases (modulational instability and partially coherent waves), and extends the analysis to composite soliton gases.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a general analytical framework for determining the probability distribution of random nonlinear wave fields governed by the focusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation (fNLSE) in regimes where typical realizations are dominated by solitons. We formulate the soliton gas resolution conjecture for the long-time evolution of slowly varying ("semiclassical") random initial states and implement a stochastic analogue of the inverse scattering transform by establishing a relationship between the spectral density of states of the underlying bound-state soliton gas and the probability density function (PDF) of the intensity of the resulting turbulent wave field. The derived explicit integral representation for the PDF is shown to be in excellent agreement with direct numerical simulations across several representative regimes of fNLSE integrable turbulence. The results have broad applicability to physical systems including water waves, nonlinear optics, and superfluids.

</details>

### [Band Unfolding via the Quadratic Pseudospectrum](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05423v1)

**Authors:** Christopher A. Bairnsfather, Ralph M. Kaufmann, Terry A. Loring, Alexander Cerjan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05423v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **3/5**

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05423_figures/2605.05423_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

(a) Schematic of the trimerized lattice. (b) Exact folded band structure of this model. (c) Heatmap of the quadratic gap µ(E,k) in units of t with k chosen in the range of the primitive Brillouin zone k ∈[−π, a, π/a). The analytic solution [42] for the unfolded band structure is superimposed in magenta.</sub>

</details>

<img src="2605.05423_figures/2605.05423_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

The finite lattice has 72 unit cells of 3 sites each, with v = 0.8t and w = 1.2t, and κT = 0.3t. (b) Approximate unfolded band structure using µ(E,k) of the same system, but with open boundary conditions (OBC). (c) Approximate boundary-independent unfolded band structure using µ(E,k,x) with κX = 0.1t with OBC. For x chosen in the lattice’s center, the smoothing function is zero on sites 72 through 144, 1 on sites 1 through 36 and 180 through 216, and smoothly interpolates between 0 and 1 in between, see Supplemental Information [43].</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05423_figures/2605.05423_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4: (a)-(c) Approximate unfolded band structures found using µ(E,k) of the finite breathing honeycomb lattice (shown in the Supplement [43]), over a path in k-space through high-symmetry points of the Brillouin zone. The finite system has 546 total sites and the calculations use κT = 0.1t. Three different realizations of the model are shown, with intra-hexagon couplings t1 = 0.8t and inter-hexagon couplings t2 = 1.2t (a), t1 = t2 = t (b), and t1 = 1.2t, t2 = 0.8t (c).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05423_figures/2605.05423_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3: Approximate dispersion for an open Fibonacci lattice calculated using the bulk-resolved quadratic gap µ(E,k,x). The chain was formed using 12 recursive substitutions yielding a total system length of 987 sites, in which the coupling coefficients are v = 0.7t and w = 1.0t with t setting the energy scale. Pseudospectral calculations used κT = 0.05t and κX = 0.1(t/a), where a is the site-to-site spacing. Using x in the system’s center and 14 substitutions to obtain a longer chain, the smoothing function selected for the sites 862 through 1722. The predicted mini-gap locations labeled by {p, q} are marked on the right-side of the approximate unfolded band structure [40, 44].</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new numerical framework for 'band unfolding' that works for systems without exact periodicity, such as quasicrystals and disordered materials. By using a quadratic pseudospectrum, the method can reconstruct dispersion relations and effectively separate bulk electronic properties from edge states in finite systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** The method provides a robust way to extract momentum-resolved spectral features from complex, non-periodic Hamiltonians, which is highly relevant for analyzing the dynamics and spectra of many-body systems or cold atom lattices with disorder or moiré potentials.

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Traditional band theory fails in systems lacking exact translational symmetry, such as disordered, aperiodic, or finite systems, and existing unfolding methods do not guarantee states are simultaneously localized in energy and momentum.

**Main result.** The authors introduce a quadratic pseudospectrum framework that generalizes band unfolding to aperiodic and finite systems, enabling the reconstruction of dispersion relations and the isolation of bulk properties from boundary effects.

**Method.** A quadratic pseudospectrum approach using a composite operator Q(E, k) to identify approximate joint eigenvectors of the Hamiltonian and translation operators, supplemented by a position-dependent operator to suppress boundary states.

**Model / system.** The framework is benchmarked on 1D trimerized lattices, 1D Fibonacci chains (quasiperiodic), and 2D breathing honeycomb lattices (topological/moiré-like).

**Key observables.** Quadratic gap mu(E, k), quadratic epsilon-pseudospectrum, and momentum-resolved uncertainty (variance and deviation).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Kappa (balance between energy and momentum resolution), kappa_X (spatial localization scale), and hopping amplitudes (v, w, t1, t2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the existence of almost-commuting operators and is subject to finite-size effects (beading) in real-space implementations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a trimerized lattice schematic and folded/unfolded band heatmaps; Figure 2 compares unfolded spectra with and without boundary suppression; Figure 3 displays the Fibonacci chain dispersion and gap hierarchy; Figure 4 shows reconstructed Dirac points in the breathing honeycomb lattice.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the problem (limitations of band theory) -> Mathematical framework (quadratic pseudospectrum and composite operator) -> Implementation of boundary suppression -> Benchmarking on periodic/trimerized systems -> Application to aperiodic (Fibonacci) and 2D (breathing honeycomb) systems -> Error decomposition and uncertainty analysis.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Band theory provides the foundation for understanding electronic structure in crystalline materials, but its reliance on exact translational symmetry limits its applicability to systems with defects, disorder, incommensurate modulations, or large unit cells. Here, we introduce a band unfolding framework that directly generalizes traditional band theory to systems where exact periodicity is absent, and which remains well-defined for both aperiodic and finite systems. To do so, we employ a pseudospectral approach to identify approximate joint eigenvectors of a system's Hamiltonian and translation operators, thereby yielding an unfolded band structure whose features are directly connected to the manifestation of approximate extended states simultaneously localized in energy and crystalline momentum. To reveal bulk-only spectral phenomena in finite systems, we further show that this pseudospectral framework naturally accommodates additional operators that suppress contributions from boundary-localized states, enabling the systematic isolation of intrinsic bulk behavior. We benchmark the scheme on several representative systems in one and two dimensions, including a Fibonacci chain, where our approach is able to both reveal a dispersive envelope while preserving the underlying hierarchy of spectral gaps. Looking forward, this pseudospectral approach may yield a broad framework for predicting momentum-resolved material responses in aperiodic, disordered, and finite systems where conventional band-theoretic methods are not applicable.

</details>

### [Light-Induced Even-Wave Spin Splittings in Nonmagnetic Centrosymmetric Systems with Spin-Orbit Coupling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05316v1)

**Authors:** Xiao-Jiao Wang, Dongling Liu, Di Zhu, Zheng-Yang Zhuang, Zhongbo Yan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05316v1>

<sub>FIG. 1. (a) Conventional picture of spin splitting induced by SOC and MEC. Starting from a static Hamiltonian H0 that preserves spin- SU(2), T and P, the inclusion of T -invariant and P-broken SOC (HSO) leads to odd-parity spin splitting, whereas the inclusion of T - broken and P-invariant MEC (HM) leads to even-parity spin split- ting. (b) A counterpart to the conventional picture. Starting from a CPL-driven Hamiltonian HF that preserves spin-SU(2) and P but lacks T , the inclusion of T -invariant and P-invariant SOC leads to even-parity spin splitting, whereas the inclusion of T -broken and P- broken MEC (preserving the combination of a π spin rotation and a spatial inversion) leads to...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05316_figures/2605.05316_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. Light-induced extended s-wave spin splitting. Energy bands along high-symmetry lines for the conduction-valence-band symmet- ric [(a), t1 = 0] and asymmetric [(c), t1 = 0.1] case, respectively. Orange and blue lines represent spin-up and spin-down bands, re- spectively. Panels (b) and (d) show the isoenergy contours at E = 1, with (b) [(d)] corresponding to (a) [(c)]. In (d), the dashed rings cor- responds to a (π, π) shift of the solid rings centering round the M point. Shared parameters are: t2 = 1, t3 = 0.5, λ = 0.5, ω = 5, η = 1, and A0 = 1.0.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.05316_figures/2605.05316_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. (a) Topological phase diagram. (b) Open-boundary spectrum for the Chern insulator phase labeled by the black dot illustrated in panel (a). Each edge contains two spin-up and two spin-down chiral edge states, consistent with the Chern number C = 4. Shared pa- rameters are: t1 = 0.1, t2 = 1, t3 = 0.5, ω = 5, and η = 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05316_figures/2605.05316_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Light-induced d-wave spin splitting. (a) Energy bands along high-symmetry lines. Orange and blue curves denote spin-up and spin-down bands, respectively; grey curves indicate spin-degenerate bands. (b) Isoenergy contours at E = 0.8. The alternating orange and blue lobes reveal a dxy-wave symmetry. Such a spin texture is a hallmark of d-wave altermagnetism, here induced without intrinsic magnetism. Parameters are: t+ = 0.5, t−= 1, t3 = 0.5, λ = 0.5, ω = 5, η = 1, and A0 = 1.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05316_figures/2605.05316_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. Evolution of spin and orbital magnetizations as functions of the driving amplitude. The electron filling fraction is fixed at 1. (a) Evolution in the s-wave spin-split phase. (b) Evolution in the d-wave spin-split phase. The unit of M (s) z is µB, and the unit of M (o) z is µ∗ B = ℏe/2m∗, where m∗is the effective mass defined at the band edge of the lower band before driving. Parameters for (a): {t1, t2, t3, λ, ω, η, A0} = {0.5, 1, 0.5, 0.5, 5, 1, 1}. Parameters for (b): {t+, t−, t3, λ, ω, η, A0} = {0.5, 1, 0.5, 0.5, 5, 1, 1}.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper shows that circularly polarized light can induce even-parity spin splitting in nonmagnetic, centrosymmetric materials, effectively mimicking the spin physics of ferromagnets and altermagnets. By controlling the orbital character, one can realize various spin-split symmetries and even topological Chern insulator phases. This provides a new pathway for light-induced control of spin and topological properties without intrinsic magnetism.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates breaking the traditional dichotomy between spin-orbit coupling (odd-parity) and magnetic exchange coupling (even-parity) by generating even-parity spin splitting in nonmagnetic, centrosymmetric systems using light.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that circularly polarized light can dynamically generate even-parity spin splitting with s-, d-, and g-wave symmetries, and that these driven states can host Chern insulator phases.

**Method.** The study employs Floquet-Bloch theory and a high-frequency, off-resonant expansion to derive an effective Hamiltonian for the time-periodic system.

**Model / system.** The model consists of 2D, nonmagnetic, centrosymmetric systems with spin-orbit coupling, including square lattice models with p-orbitals (s-wave) and d-orbitals (d- and g-wave).

**Key observables.** Spin-splitting parity, spin-split energy spectra, Chern number, spin magnetization, and orbital magnetization.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Driving frequency (high-frequency regime), light amplitude, light helicity, spin-orbit coupling strength, and orbital angular character.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is performed in the high-frequency, off-resonant regime where the driving frequency is much larger than the bandwidth.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the symmetry dichotomy and the new light-induced mechanism; Figure 2 shows energy bands and isoenergy contours for s-wave splitting; Figure E1 shows g-wave spin texture isoenergy contours.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing the symmetry dichotomy, introduces the Floquet-driven model, analyzes specific orbital-dependent splitting symmetries (s, d, and g-wave), discusses topological phases and magnetization, and concludes with material candidates and experimental signatures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Spin splitting underpins a vast range of spin-dependent phenomena. Traditionally, two primary mechanisms generate such splitting: relativistic spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and nonrelativistic magnetic exchange coupling (MEC). Governed by distinct symmetry constraints, they produce splittings of opposite parity -- odd for SOC and even for MEC -- a dichotomy that underpins the distinct spin physics of nonmagnetic and magnetic systems. In this work, we break this dichotomy by demonstrating the dynamic generation of even-parity spin splitting in centrosymmetric, nonmagnetic systems driven by circularly polarized light. We show that the symmetry of the induced splitting is controlled by the angular character of the underlying orbitals, enabling the realization of s-wave, d-wave, and g-wave spin-split band structures identical to those of ferromagnets and altermagnets. Furthermore, we find that these spin-split bands can naturally host a Chern insulator phase. We also discuss the associated spin and orbital magnetization. Our results establish a direct and previously unrecognized conceptual link between the two fundamental mechanisms of spin splitting.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a method to separate magnetic and optical signals in the ultrafast dynamics of non-collinear antiferromagnets. By studying Mn3NiN and Mn3GaN, the authors reveal how magnetic field and temperature influence domain redistribution and quenching processes. This provides deeper insight into the complex spin dynamics of antiperovskite materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of disentangling magnetic (MOKE-like and Voigt) from non-magnetic (refractive index changes) contributions to the ultrafast dynamics in antiperovskite non-collinear antiferromagnets.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully separated magnetic quenching from optical heating and discovered that Mn3NiN exhibits a temperature-dependent transition from single-step to two-step quenching dynamics, unlike the temperature-invariant dynamics in Mn3Sn.

**Method.** Time-resolved pump-probe spectroscopy combined with full optical modeling using Yeh's formalism and probe-polarization-resolved measurements.

**Model / system.** Thin films of antiperovskite non-collinear antiferromagnetic metals Mn3NiN (Gamma4g phase) and Mn3GaN (Gamma5g phase). The model incorporates domain redistribution driven by piezomagnetic moments and uses a four-parameter fit for the permittivity tensor.

**Key observables.** Change in polarization rotation (delta beta), transient transmission (delta T/T), and magnetic-field-dependent (MFD) components.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Neele temperature (TN approx 246 K), quenching time (tauQ approx 0.4 ps), and sample tilt angle (Theta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Neglecting domain pinning and defects, and assuming equal initial domain populations in zero-field Mn3NiN.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the pump-probe setup and polarization angles; Fig 5 displays temperature-dependent rotation dynamics (single-step vs two-step); Fig 8 and 9 illustrate the domain variants and computed MOKE/Voigt signals for different tilt angles.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of signal disentanglement, describes the experimental setup and materials, presents the optical modeling strategy, details the results for Mn3NiN and Mn3GaN (including field and tilt dependence), and concludes with a discussion on temperature-dependent quenching dynamics.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Non-collinear antiferromagnets are a class of spin-polarized antiferromagnets in which chiral spin textures give rise to Berry-curvature-driven phenomena, such as the anomalous Hall effect (AHE), without net magnetization. We investigate the properties of thin films of antiperovskite non-collinear antiferromagnetic metals Mn3NiN and Mn3GaN using pump-probe experiments. In both materials, we observe a strong dependence of pump-polarization-independent dynamics, induced by femtosecond laser pulses, on the angle between the sample normal and the direction of probe propagation. In Mn3NiN, where the presence of a sizable AHE indicates the Γ4g phase, the measured magnetooptical (MO) signals acquire an additional, strong dependence on the external magnetic field when the probe pulses are incident at nonzero angles. In contrast, in Mn3GaN, where the absence of AHE indicates the Γ5g phase, the measured signals do not depend on the magnetic field. Using probe-polarization-resolved measurements combined with full optical modeling based on Yeh's formalism, we quantitatively separate magnetic and non-magnetic contributions to the measured signals. We show that in Mn3NiN, the observed magnetic field dependence results from field-controlled redistribution of magnetic domain populations, enabled by their piezomagnetic moments and detected by a Kerr-like MO effect, while this effect is absent in Mn3GaN. Temperature-dependent measurements reveal a change from single-step to two-step quenching dynamics with increasing temperature in Mn3NiN. This behavior contrasts with the nearly temperature-independent quenching dynamics reported for the non-collinear antiferromagnetic Heusler compound Mn3Sn, but resembles the crossover from type-I to type-II demagnetization dynamics in metallic ferromagnets.

</details>

### [MLM: Multi-Layer Moire -- A Python Package for Generating Commensurate Supercells of Twisted Multilayer Two-Dimensional Materials](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05393v1)

**Authors:** Anikeya Aditya, Sampad Mohanty  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05393v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Frenkel-Kontorova` **2/5**

**Summary.** The MLM Python package provides an efficient way to generate periodic supercells for twisted multilayer 2D materials. By replacing brute-force searches with a linear-algebra-based approach, it significantly accelerates the discovery of commensurate structures, even for very small twist angles and complex multi-layer systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Constructing periodic, commensurate supercells for twisted multilayer 2D materials is computationally expensive because conventional brute-force enumeration scales as O(N^4).

**Main result.** The authors developed MLM, a Python package that uses a 'solve-and-round' algorithm to reduce complexity to O(N^2), achieving speedup ratios of up to 216x and enabling the simulation of supercells with millions of atoms.

**Method.** The 'solve-and-round' algorithm solves a 2x2 linear system to determine top-layer indices from bottom-layer vectors, combined with a sequential extension for multiple layers and a fractional-coordinate folding algorithm for atom selection.

**Model / system.** The package is applied to twisted multilayer 2D materials with various Bravais lattices, including hexagonal (graphene, MoS2), square (SrTiO3), and mismatched heterostructures (PbTiO3/SrTiO3).

**Key observables.** Twist angle, mismatch residual (delta_vec), relative residual (rho), and supercell vector norm.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Twist angles (0 to 90 degrees), lattice constants, fractional tolerance (delta), and the integer index search window [n_min, n_max].

**Assumptions / limitations.** The matching process is inherently asymmetric, treating the bottom layer as an exact reference while the top layer matches within a prescribed tolerance.

**Figures summary.** Visualizations of graphene and MoS2 supercells showing AA and AB stacking, a trilayer MoS2 supercell, and top-view images of SrTiO3 structures.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to the commensurability challenge, description of the MLM package and its solve-and-round algorithm, demonstration of the package on various 2D materials (graphene, MoS2, oxides), performance benchmarking against brute-force methods, and discussion of limitations and future work.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Moire superlattices formed by stacking atomically thin two-dimensional materials with a relative twist angle have emerged as a versatile platform for engineering quantum electronic, optical, and ferroic properties. Computational modelling of such systems with periodic boundary conditions requires the identification of commensurate supercells in which the moire periodicity is reproduced exactly, or within a prescribed tolerance. While several codes exist for bilayer systems, extension to three or more layers with independently chosen twist angles remains a significant challenge. Here we present MLM (Multi-Layer Moire), an open-source Python package that constructs periodic, PBC-compatible moire supercells for an arbitrary number of twisted layers with any Bravais lattice type. The package employs a solve-and-round algorithm that reduces the coincidence-site search to an $O(N^2)$ linear-algebra problem per twist angle, compared to the O(N^4) brute-force enumeration required by conventional approaches. We demonstrate the package on bilayer graphene, bilayer and trilayer MoS$_2$, bilayer SrTiO$_3$, and a PbTiO$_3$/SrTiO$_3$ oxide heterostructure, producing simulation-ready structure files for both VASP and LAMMPS. The fractional-coordinate atom-selection algorithm scales to supercells containing millions of atoms and is robust across all twist angles including very small angles below 1 degree.

</details>

**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06146v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides experimental measurements of the thermal conductivity of various substrates at sub-Kelvin temperatures. It identifies high-resistivity silicon as the best candidate for heat removal and highlights the need for 3D integration to manage heat in large-scale quantum processors.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Thermal management is a critical bottleneck for scaling large-scale quantum integrated systems, as heat from control electronics can cause qubit decoherence.

**Main result.** High-resistivity silicon exhibits the highest thermal conductivity among studied substrates, while Nb routing lines increase in-plane conductance but the substrate remains the dominant heat path.

**Method.** Thermal conductivity was measured using a heater and RuO2 thermometer in the sub-Kelvin regime, with analysis using a finite-element non-equilibrium Green's function approach to extract phonon mean free paths.

**Model / system.** The study investigates various substrates (high-resistivity and low-resistivity silicon, sapphire, and borosilicate glass) and an on-chip routing test vehicle featuring superconducting Niobium lines on silicon.

**Key observables.** Thermal conductivity (kappa), phonon mean free path (MFP), and in-plane thermal conductance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sub-Kelvin temperature regime (50 mK to 1.2 K), power dissipation in the nanowatt to microwatt range, and phonon mean free path ranging from 30 to 4000 micrometers.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The thermal resistance of NbTi measurement wires and the volume of Stycast 1266 are assumed to be negligible; self-heating of the thermometer is assumed to be negligible at 1 nW.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the measurement setup and thermal model; Fig 5 and 6 illustrate the routing test vehicle setup and temperature gradients as a function of power; Supplementary figures detail the measurement validation and packaging workflow.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the thermal management challenge, presents experimental characterization of substrate thermal conductivity, evaluates the impact of on-chip Nb routing, and concludes with implications for 3D quantum architectures.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The development of large-scale quantum systems increasingly relies on the close integration of heterogeneous components such as qubits, control electronics, and readout circuits, making thermal management at cryogenic temperatures a central challenge in such architectures. In this work, we present an experimental thermal study of two building blocks of such systems: the substrate and the on-chip routing. We first investigate the sub-kelvin thermal conductivity of four substrate materials: high-resistivity silicon, low-resistivity silicon, borosilicate, and sapphire. We report that high-resistivity silicon exhibits the highest thermal conductivity among the substrates studied ($5\cdot10^{-2}$~W/m$\cdot$K at 300~mK), while low-resistivity silicon, borosilicate, and sapphire show lower values ($8\cdot10^{-4}$~W/m$\cdot$K, 2$\cdot10^{-3}$~W/m$\cdot$K, and 2$\cdot10^{-3}$~W/m$\cdot$K at 300~mK, respectively). Ballistic conductance evaluation using a finite-element non-equilibrium Green's function approach further allows us to extract the phonon mean free path in each substrate and gives insights into the involved scattering mechanisms. Additionally, we employ a dedicated test vehicle to evaluate the impact of on-chip routing on the thermal conductance of the system. Our measurements with superconducting Nb routing lines reveal that the routing increases the in-plane thermal conductance of the system, but the substrate remains the dominant heat path. These results highlight the critical role of the substrate choice within quantum systems and underscore the importance of function partitioning through 3D integration approaches for more efficient thermal management in quantum architectures.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores how nematic fluctuations change the nature of collective modes in superconductors. It demonstrates that the resulting highly anisotropic gap leads to a unique dispersion of phase and amplitude modes that differs significantly from standard BCS theory.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the dispersion and spectral structure of collective modes (transverse phase and longitudinal amplitude modes) in a superconductor where pairing is mediated by long-range nematic fluctuations.

**Main result.** The authors find that the analytic structure of the pair susceptibility is qualitatively different from BCS theory, exhibiting highly unconventional dispersion, including split peaks and logarithmic singularities in the longitudinal channel.

**Method.** The study uses Gorkov's diagrammatic technique and the Random Phase Approximation (RPA) to calculate the pair-pair susceptibility and its spectral function.

**Model / system.** A 2D one-band model of electrons with an anisotropic s-wave gap, specifically motivated by nematic-fluctuation-mediated superconductors like FeSe and its doped derivatives.

**Key observables.** Transverse (phase) susceptibility, longitudinal (amplitude) susceptibility, and their respective real and imaginary spectral functions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fermi velocity (vF), superconducting gap amplitude (delta_0), momentum transfer (q), frequency (Omega), and coupling strength (g).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a zero-temperature limit, a 2D electron system, and neglects long-range Coulomb interactions in certain parts of the derivation.

**Figures summary.** The notes mention a polar plot (Fig 1) and a frequency dependence plot (Fig 9).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nematic-mediated pairing, defines the Hamiltonian and anisotropic gap model, derives the pair susceptibility using diagrammatic techniques, analyzes the pole structure and dispersion in both transverse and longitudinal channels, and provides mathematical proofs for the analytic properties of the susceptibility in an appendix.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We analyze the spectrum of collective modes in a superconductor in which pairing is mediated by long-range nematic fluctuations. Previous experimental and theoretical studies have found that the superconducting gap in such a system is highly anisotropic and, at any finite $T<T_c$, vanishes on four arcs of the Fermi surface, even when the pairing symmetry is $s$ wave ($s^{+-}$ between hole and electron pockets). We derive the expression for the pair susceptibility $χ(\mathbf{q},Ω)$ at finite momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $Ω$ deep in the superconducting phase. We analyze the spectral function, $\operatorname{Im}χ(\mathbf{q},Ω)$, and its pole structure in the transverse (phase) and longitudinal (amplitude) channels, and compare the results with those of a conventional $s$-wave superconductor. We find that the analytic structure of the pair susceptibility in both channels is qualitatively distinct from that in a BCS superconductor. This gives rise to a highly unconventional dispersion of phase and amplitude collective modes.

</details>

<sub>Figure 4: The energy evolution over time for GSPM. The final time is 1 ns. For all α, the energy level without projection is lower than that of projection method.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.05858_figures/2605.05858_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 5: The energy evolution over time for BDF1. The final time is 1 ns.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper compares different numerical schemes for simulating micromagnetics, specifically investigating if the magnetization magnitude constraint can be maintained without an explicit projection step. It finds that the BDF1 method is robust and consistent even without projection, potentially simplifying the numerical implementation of magnetic simulations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study evaluates the necessity and impact of using nonlinear projection steps to enforce constant magnetization magnitude in micromagnetic simulations.

**Main result.** The BDF1 method is found to be highly consistent whether or not a projection step is used, whereas the Gauss-Seidel method shows significant discrepancies at high dissipation coefficients.

**Method.** A comparative analysis of two first-order semi-implicit time-discretization strategies: the implicit Gauss-Seidel method (GSPM) and the Backward Differentiation Formula (BDF1), both tested with and without a spherical projection step.

**Model / system.** The study uses the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation to model ferromagnetic materials, specifically focusing on ferromagnetic thin films and nanostrips.

**Key observables.** Energy evolution (decay and stability), magnetization profiles, and domain wall motion.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Gilbert damping coefficient (alpha) ranging from 0.01 to 0.1, external magnetic field (5 mT), and grid discretization scales.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is limited to first-order numerical schemes and assumes the validity of the LLG equation for the modeled ferromagnetic systems.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare magnetization profiles and energy evolution for GSPM and BDF1 at different damping levels, alongside time-series snapshots of domain wall motion and various magnetic configurations (C, Diamond, Flower, etc.).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the micromagnetic problem, defines the numerical schemes (GSPM and BDF1) with and without projection, presents stability analysis, compares static and dynamic simulation results across different damping regimes, and concludes with suggestions for future higher-order extensions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In micromagnetic simulations, the constant magnitude of the magnetization can be derived from the continuity equation. Since the time evolution of the magnetization in the continuity equation is perpendicular to the plane determined by the magnetization and the effective field, taking the inner product of both sides of the model with the magnetization shows that the evolution rate of the magnitude of the magnetization is zero, thus keeping the magnitude constant. From this perspective, the equation itself can maintain the constraint of constant magnetization magnitude. We discretized the continuity equation and compared two first-order semi-implicit strategies in time: one is the implicit Gauss-Seidel method, and the other is the semi-implicit Backward Differentiation Formula (BDF) method. We considered the comparison between these two schemes with and without the projection step. The results of micromagnetic simulations show that when the dissipation coefficient is large, the implicit Gauss-Seidel method without the projection step has significant differences from the method with the projection step in both the achieved steady state and domain wall motion. When an appropriate dissipation coefficient is selected, the difference between the two narrows, and both the steady state and domain wall motion can be simulated. For the other method, BDF1, whether the dissipation coefficient is large or small, the results with and without the projection step are quite consistent, and it can effectively simulate the domain wall motion.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.05675"></a>
### [A Scalable Translationally Invariant Variational Theory of Ab Initio Polarons](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05675v1)

**Authors:** Moritz K. A. Baumgarten, Hamlin Wu, Tong Jiang, Joonho Lee  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05675v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Summary.** The authors present a new, scalable variational method for calculating the properties of polarons in real materials. By avoiding supercells and using a momentum-projected approach, the method works efficiently across both weak and strong electron-phonon coupling regimes. This provides a systematically improvable way to study polaron physics in the thermodynamic limit.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a scalable and translationally invariant method to study ab initio polarons across different coupling regimes without the computational cost of supercells.

**Main result.** The introduction of a scalable variational theory that achieves near-linear scaling with k-points and accurately captures both delocalized and self-trapped carriers.

**Method.** A momentum-projected Toyozawa-type wavefunction combined with a low-rank factorization of the electron-phonon kernel.

**Model / system.** The study applies the method to the Fröhlich model and real materials including LiF, anatase TiO2, and rutile TiO2.

**Key observables.** Polaron binding energies, thermodynamic-limit band structures, and real-space measures of polaron extent.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Weak-coupling and strong-coupling regimes, and the number of k-points.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes a momentum-projected variational ansatz and utilizes low-rank factorization of the electron-phonon kernel.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a new variational theory, demonstrates its scalability via k-point scaling, and validates it through benchmarks against the Fröhlich model and first-principles data (LiF, TiO2), including a comparison with DiagMC.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a scalable, translationally invariant variational theory for ab initio polarons that remains applicable across coupling regimes without resorting to supercells. Our approach combines a momentum-projected Toyozawa-type wavefunction with a low-rank factorization of the electron-phonon kernel, enabling near-linear scaling with the number of $\mathbf{k}$-points while capturing both delocalized and self-trapped carriers. Benchmarks for the Fröhlich model, LiF, and anatase and rutile TiO$_2$ yield accurate polaron binding energies, thermodynamic-limit band structures, and transparent real-space measures of polaron extent. For LiF, comparison with first-principles diagrammatic Monte Carlo (DiagMC) reveals close agreement for the weak-coupling electron-polaron ground state and band structure. However, in the hole-polaron of LiF, which is in the strong-coupling regime, we found a significant bias in DiagMC results. These results establish momentum-projected variational wavefunctions as a systematically improvable route to thermodynamic limit studies of polarons in real materials.

</details>

### [Anomalous Thomson Effect](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06299v1)

**Authors:** Ying-Fei Zhang, Zhi-Fan Zhang, Zhen-Gang Zhu, Gang Su  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06299v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(a) Temperature dependence of the anomalous Nernst coefficient SA xy (line-symbol type, taken from experiments) and the calculated anomalous Thomson coefficient τATE (line type) for CoS2, Co3Sn2S2, and CeCrGe3, where τATE is eval- uated from the experimental SA xy using Eq.(4). (b) The ratio η = τATE/SA xy vs T.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper proposes the Anomalous Thomson Effect, a new transverse thermoelectric phenomenon driven by Berry curvature. It demonstrates that this effect can be significantly stronger than the Anomalous Nernst Effect, offering a promising mechanism for efficient solid-state cooling in magnetic materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper proposes and investigates a new thermoelectric phenomenon called the Anomalous Thomson Effect (ATE) and evaluates its potential for solid-state refrigeration.

**Main result.** The authors derive that the Anomalous Thomson Coefficient (ATC) is generally enhanced relative to the Anomalous Nernst Coefficient (ANC), reaching a ratio of 3 in the low-temperature limit and up to 15 in CeCrGe3 at liquid-nitrogen temperatures.

**Method.** The study uses the Boltzmann transport equation, Onsager reciprocity relations, and the Debye model approximation to derive transport coefficients and evaluate experimental data.

**Model / system.** The research focuses on magnetic materials with Berry curvature, specifically using a massive Dirac model to represent the topological layered kagome ferromagnet Fe3Sn2 and analyzing materials like CoS2, Co3Sn2S2, and CeCrGT3.

**Key observables.** Anomalous Thomson Coefficient (ATC), Anomalous Nernst Coefficient (ANC), electrical conductivity, anomalous Hall conductivity, and heat current density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fermi energy (epsilon_F), temperature (T), Berry curvature, and the low-temperature limit (T -> 0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The transport is assumed to be dominated by a narrow energy range around the Fermi energy, and the Debye model is used for the wave vector cutoff.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows schematics of various thermoelectric effects; Figure 2 displays Berry curvature contours, the temperature dependence of the ATC, and the evolution of the coefficient with Fermi energy; Figure 3 compares experimental ANC data for various materials.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the ATE concept, provides a theoretical derivation using a Dirac model, explores the relationship between ATC and ANC, applies the theory to specific magnetic materials, and concludes with the potential for refrigeration applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose an effect named the anomalous Thomson effect (ATE), analogous to the anomalous Hall effect and the anomalous Nernst effect (ANE). The anomalous Thomson coefficient (ATC) is derived as a function of the anomalous Nernst coefficient (ANC); hence, the ATC inherits the same mechanisms of the ANC. Specifically, we study a massive Dirac model for Fe3Sn2 to capture intrinsic Berry-curvature-driven transport. Our results show that the ATC is generally enhanced relative to the ANC. In the low-temperature limit, the ratio ATC/ANC approaches three. Since the relation between the ATE and the ANE is model-independent, we utilize experimental ANE data to infer experiment-related ATC for CoS2, Co3Sn2S2, and CeCrGe3. We find that the ATC for CeCrGe3 can be as large as fifteen times the ANC in the liquid-nitrogen temperature regime, making this effect highly attractive for solid-state thermoelectric refrigeration in this temperature range. It is important to emphasize that the proposed ATE can be directly verified using existing ANE data, without the need for additional equipment or measurements.

</details>

**Authors:** Marco Agozzino, Eleonora Moroni, Yifan Zhang, Valeria Russo, Carlo Spartaco Casari

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a method to electrochemically synthesize stable, amorphous carbon nanoparticles containing a high percentage of sp-hybridized carbon. By controlling the reduction kinetics, the researchers achieved tunable particle sizes and unprecedented ambient stability for these nanostructures.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a bottom-up electrochemical approach to synthesize amorphous sp-sp2 carbon nanoparticles that preserve a high sp-fraction and exhibit high ambient stability.

**Main result.** The electrochemical reduction of polyynes produces amorphous carbon nanoparticles with a high sp-fraction (>60%) and tunable diameters, which remain stable in ambient conditions for over six months.

**Method.** Electrochemical reduction of hydrogen-capped polyynes in acetonitrile using a three-electrode cell, followed by characterization via Raman spectroscopy, SEM, and UV-Vis spectroscopy.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of carbon atomic wires (CAWs) in the form of hydrogen-capped polyynes (HCnH, 8 <= n <= 18) in an acetonitrile electrolyte with TBA-TFB.

**Key observables.** sp and sp2 carbon fractions (Xsp, Xsp2), nanoparticle diameter, Raman band frequencies (D and G bands), and I_D/I_G ratio.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Applied potential (-1.8 V vs Pt), reduction duration (400 s), and precursor/electrolyte concentrations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The calculation of sp content assumes negligible sp3 content and relies on a specific Raman scattering cross-section ratio of 0.48.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows cyclic voltammetry and absorption spectra indicating polyyne consumption; Table 1 relates nanoparticle diameter to sp content; Figure 3 shows Raman fingerprints and long-term stability.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the synthesis problem, describes the electrochemical experimental setup and precursor preparation, presents results on morphology and structural ordering, discusses the mechanism of chain preservation and stability, and concludes with future outlooks.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Carbon Atomic Wires (CAWs) are finite linear chains of sp-hybridized carbon atoms. Here the electrochemical reduction of CAWs in the form of polyynes (i.e. with alternated single-triple bonds) is reported. Upon applying a reducing potential to a solution containing polydispersed hydrogen-capped polyynes, the formation of a black precipitate was observed. Electronic absorption spectroscopy confirmed the irreversible reaction of the carbon chains while excluding degradation or side reactions. Subsequent analyses revealed that the precipitate consisted of amorphous carbon nanoparticles with tunable diameters. This control over particle size is attributed to the modulation of growth kinetics through restricted mass transport toward the solid-liquid interface. Raman spectroscopy showed that the resulting material exhibits an amorphous sp-sp2 character, with a retained sp fraction exceeding 60%. Smaller nanoparticles displayed reduced disorder within the sp2 domains and a broader distribution of sp-chain lengths preserved in the amorphous matrix. Additional experiments on size-selected polyynes suggest that this synthesis method allows to better preserve the starting chain length in the final structure. Unlike previously reported amorphous sp-sp2 carbon networks, the nanoparticles produced in this study show remarkable stability under ambient conditions, retaining their sp character for times in excess of six months. These findings pave the way for future applications, particularly as further diameter tuning may enable access to the quantum-dot regime.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that silica thin films used in gravitational-wave detectors are not isotropic but exhibit significant compressive anisotropy along the growth axis. It shows that while standard annealing does not fix this, high-temperature treatment can restore isotropy, potentially reducing thermal noise in future detectors.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates whether the assumed elastic isotropy in ion-beam-sputtered silica thin films is valid, as unmodeled anisotropy may contribute to thermal noise in gravitational-wave detectors.

**Main result.** The researchers discovered that as-deposited silica films exhibit cylindrical elastic symmetry with a 6% compressive anisotropy along the film normal, which can be nearly eliminated through high-temperature (900 C) annealing.

**Method.** The study uses Brillouin Light Scattering (BLS) to probe GHz-frequency elastic constants, alongside Infrared (IR) reflectivity, X-ray Reflectivity (XRR), and Spectroscopic Ellipsometry (SE) to characterize structural and optical properties.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of ion-beam-sputtered (IBS) SiO2 thin films on silicon or sapphire substrates, modeled as transversely isotropic media with five independent elastic constants.

**Key observables.** Elastic constants (c11, c33, c44, c66, c13), acoustic wave velocities, refractive index, mass density, and oxygen structure distribution (bridging vs. non-bridging).

**Important parameters / regimes.** GHz-range acoustic frequencies, annealing temperatures (as-deposited, 500 C, and 900 C), and the ratio of film thickness to phonon wavelength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study compares GHz-range BLS measurements to kHz-range mechanical properties, assuming negligible mechanical relaxations in the frequency range between the two.

**Figures summary.** Figures include schematics of BLS backscattering geometry and acoustic wave types, IR spectroscopy mode configurations, and various spectra/plots for XRR, ellipsometry, and elastic constant evolution.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of thermal noise in GW detectors, describes the experimental setup for BLS and IR spectroscopy, presents the discovery of elastic anisotropy, analyzes the effects of thermal annealing on structural and elastic properties, and concludes with implications for coating design.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The thermal noise of mirror coatings for gravitational-wave detectors critically depends on the elastic properties of the constituent materials. Data analyses and theoretical models typically assume each material is homogeneous and isotropic, but isotropy has never been explicitly verified. Using Brillouin light scattering (BLS), we demonstrate for the first time that ion-beam-sputtered SiO2 -- a material still viable for future mirror coatings -- exhibits cylindrical elastic symmetry, with in-plane isotropy but a notable 6% compressive anisotropy along the film normal. This anisotropy remains unchanged after the post-deposition heat treatment currently used in ground-based detectors (500 $^\circ$C, 10 h) but is nearly eliminated at 900 $^\circ$C. Infrared reflectivity experiments support these findings by directly revealing heterogeneities in the distribution of bridging and non-bridging oxygen structures along the growth axis. While BLS measures the real part of the elastic constants at GHz frequencies, the data reveal negligible contributions from mechanical relaxations in the kHz-GHz range, making BLS a valid substitute for low-frequency properties obtained from standard anisotropy-insensitive techniques. Our results highlight that restoring isotropy through heat treatment -- by softening the material, enabling more than 7% out-of-plane expansion, and smoothing out structural heterogeneities -- may play a key role in reducing thermal noise. This proof-of-concept study extends beyond silica, providing critical insights for the design of future coatings.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Electrically controlled Heat Assisted Magnetic Recording in Intercalated 2D Magnets](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06645v1)

**Authors:** Josue Rodriguez, Ruishi Qi, Catherine Xu, Feng Wang, James G. Analytis, Hossein Taghinejad  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06645v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Magnetic recording trilemma and HAMR concept. a The trilemma of magnetic recording: increas- ing areal density by shrinking bit size imposes contradic- tory requirements for simultaneously maintaining reasonable SNR (depends on volume, V), thermal stability (depends on anisotropy, Ku, and V), and writing field (Bw, depends on Ku). Optimizing one metric necessarily compromises another. b HAMR circumvents this limitation: transiently heating the sample above its Curie temperature reduces the anisotropy barrier, enabling bit flipping with modest fields during the write cycle. Upon cooling, the magnetic anisotropy is re- stored to a large value, ensuring thermal stability for long- term...</sub>

Two current sources are used, a small ac component for electronic readout, and a pulsed DC current for magnetization reversal. b The anomalous Hall resistivity, showing distinct low- and high-resistance states, corresponding to the UP and DOWN magnetization states, respectively. c Anomalous Hall resistivity measured after applying current pulses of various amplitudes. The device was initially set to a low (high)...</sub>

Both readout modalities consistently yield a threshold current density of Jth ∼9.7 × 104A.cm−2. b Sequence of Kerr microscopy maps acquired after successive current pulses of increasing amplitudes, corresponding to the data points presented in panel (a). The device is initialized to the UP state under a +3 T magnetic field and then pulsed under a writing field of Bw = −2 mT. Above the critical current density (J &gt; Jth), the MOKE contrast switches sign, directly...</sub>
