<sub>FIG. 4. Direct measurement of Joule heating. a The temperature dependence of the longitudinal resistance ρxx in response to a small measuring ac current of JAC = 20A.cm−2× cos(2πf◦t) superimposed on a large DC current (JDC) of varying amplitudes as indicated on the figure. The total applied current is Jtotal = JAC + JDC, with ρxx calcu- lated from the measurement of the ac component of Vxx at f◦ frequency. b The shift of the apparent Curie temperature, Tc (JDC ̸= 0), relative to the equilibrium Curie temperature, Tc (JDC = 0), is defined as ∆T = Tc (JDC ̸=0) - Tc (JDC = 0). ∆T quadratically scales with JDC, which is the signature of Joule heating. The absolute value of ∆T is plotted. c...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a method for all-electronic heat-assisted magnetic recording using the 2D magnet Ni1/4TaSe2. By using Joule heating from electrical pulses to transiently heat the material above its Curie temperature, the authors can switch magnetic states using much smaller magnetic fields than usual. This approach offers a path toward highly scalable, energy-efficient, and integrated magnetic memory for next-generation computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The magnetic recording trilemma makes it difficult to scale high-density storage without sacrificing thermal stability or writability. Existing Heat-Assisted Magnetic Recording (HAMR) relies on bulky laser optics, which are hard to integrate into on-chip architectures.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate an all-electronic variant of HAMR using Joule heating in the 2D magnet Ni1/4TaSe2, allowing magnetization reversal with a magnetic field 100 times smaller than the coercive field.

**Method.** The researchers used transport measurements (Anomalous Hall Effect) and MOKE imaging on Hall-bar devices patterned from Ni1/4TaSe2 crystals via focused-ion beam milling to observe magnetization switching induced by current pulses.

**Model / system.** The physical system is an intercalated 2D itinerant metallic ferromagnet, Ni1/4TaSe2, which features perpendicular magnetic anisotropy and a Curie temperature of approximately 55 K.

**Key observables.** Anomalous Hall resistivity (rho_xy), longitudinal resistivity (rho_xx), coercive field (Bc), and the shift in apparent Curie temperature (Delta T).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coercive field of ~0.2 T, writing field of ~2 mT, threshold current density of ~10 x 10^4 A/cm^2, and current pulse duration of 1 ms.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The demonstration is currently limited to low-temperature regimes (cryogenic temperatures) where the material is ferromagnetic.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 introduces the magnetic recording trilemma and the HAMR concept; Figure 2 shows the device SEM image, Hall hysteresis loops, and response to current; Figure 4 illustrates the temperature dependence of resistance and the quadratic scaling of temperature shift with current density.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by motivating the need for electronic HAMR, introduces the Ni1/4TaSe2 material and device fabrication, presents transport measurements establishing the magnetic baseline, demonstrates the current-induced switching mechanism, provides evidence for Joule heating via temperature scaling, and concludes with potential applications and limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The ever-increasing demand for fast, reliable, and energy-efficient information storage continues to push magnetic memory technologies toward their fundamental limits. Conventional scaling strategies, which rely on reducing bit size, inevitably run into the "magnetic recording trilemma," where signal-to-noise ratio, thermal stability, and writability cannot all be optimized simultaneously. Heat-assisted magnetic recording (HAMR) has emerged as the leading solution, enabling high-density storage by transiently heating the medium during the write cycle. However, the reliance on laser optics and plasmonic transducers restricts HAMR primarily to hard-disk drives, limiting its integration with on-chip or embedded architectures. Here, we demonstrate an electronic variant of HAMR in which Joule heating from low-current density current pulses facilitates data writing, while the anomalous Hall effect provides electronic readout. Employing intercalated 2D magnet Ni$_{1/4}$TaSe$_2$, we show direct evidence that current pulses heat the material above its Curie temperature, during which a small magnetic field of ~2mT (100 times smaller than the coercive field) enables efficient data writing. The all-electronic approach combined with the 2D magnetic medium creates timely opportunities to revisit the energy-assisted magnetization recording, enabling new recording schemes that combine fundamental novelty with technological impact.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Emergent spin quantum Hall edge states at the boundary of two-dimensional electron gas proximitized by an $s$-wave superconductor](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05847v1)

**Authors:** M. V. Parfenov, V. S. Khrapai, I. S. Burmistrov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05847v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Inset ii) is for (µ↑+ µ↓)/2 = µS. (b): Schematic dependence of the charge and spin conductances on the B-field. We predict that, unlike the strongly fluctuat- ing charge conductance GQ, the spin...</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper shows that the topological protection in 2DEG-superconductor hybrids is preserved through spin transport even when charge transport is disrupted by disorder. It predicts an even-integer quantized spin conductance and proposes an electrical setup to detect this effect. This provides a new way to identify the underlying symmetry class C of these topological edge states.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether topological protection survives in hybrid 2D electron gas-superconductor (2DEG-S) structures where charge conductance appears non-integer and sensitive to disorder.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that while charge conductance is not quantized due to Andreev reflection and disorder, the topological protection manifests in the spin transport channel via even-integer quantized spin conductance.

**Method.** The study employs a Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) Hamiltonian, the replica trick for disorder averaging, and the derivation of a Non-Linear Sigma Model (NL$\sigma$M) action within the Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry class C.

**Model / system.** A hybrid structure consisting of a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in the quantum Hall regime proximitized by an s-wave superconductor (S) under a perpendicular magnetic field.

**Key observables.** Charge conductance (G_Q), spin conductance (G_S), and charge and spin current densities.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Symmetry class C, Andreev reflection probability, magnetic field (B), superconducting order parameter ($\Delta$), and the universal regime where distance is much larger than the coherence length.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Neglects spatial dependence of the superconducting order parameter magnitude, assumes a negative g-factor for the 2DEG, and assumes the superconducting layer is thick enough to neglect z-direction motion.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the energy spectrum of chiral Andreev edge states; Figure 2a illustrates a proposed experimental device with constrictions; Figure 2b shows the fluctuation of charge conductance versus the quantization of spin conductance under a magnetic field.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by identifying the discrepancy in experimental charge conductance, moves to a microscopic BdG Hamiltonian analysis of the edge spectrum, develops a field-theoretic NL$\sigma$M approach to study disorder effects, and concludes with an experimental proposal for detecting spin quantization.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hybrid two-dimensional electron gas-superconductor (2DEG-S) structures in a quantized magnetic field offer a promising platform for realizing new topological phases. While recent experiments reveal chiral Andreev edge states, their charge conductance is not integer quantized and is disorder sensitive, raising the question of whether topological protection survives. We argue that it does, but manifests in the spin transport channel. The 2DEG-S system belongs to symmetry class C of the Altland-Zirnbauer classification, which supports an even-integer quantized transverse spin conductivity -- the spin quantum Hall effect, so far unobserved experimentally. We demonstrate that 2DEG-S hybrids host topologically protected edge states carrying a spin current with an even-integer quantized spin conductance robust against disorder. Finally, we propose an experimental setup to probe this protection via electrical measurements, establishing a concrete route to detect the class C origin of the chiral Andreev edge states.

</details>

**Summary.** This study demonstrates that proton irradiation can drastically increase the critical current density in Hg1223 superconductors by creating effective pinning centers. The research identifies a transition in the vortex pinning mechanism toward point pinning and provides a pathway for improving the performance of high-Tc materials for practical applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Enhancing the critical current density (Jc) in HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+d (Hg1223) single crystals and understanding the resulting changes in vortex pinning mechanisms and dynamics following proton irradiation.

**Main result.** 3 MeV proton irradiation significantly enhances the self-field Jc at 2 K from 5.5 to 26 MA/cm2 at a dose of 1x10^16/cm2, while shifting the pinning mechanism from delta-kappa to point pinning.

**Method.** 3 MeV proton irradiation was used to introduce defects, followed by SQUID magnetization measurements, magnetic relaxation studies, and analysis using the extended Dew-Hughes model.

**Model / system.** HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+d (Hg1223) single crystals, a high-Tc cuprate superconductor with Tc approx 129 K, subjected to proton irradiation at NIRS-HIMAC.

**Key observables.** Critical current density (Jc), critical temperature (Tc), normalized magnetic relaxation rate (S), pinning force density (Fp), and power-law exponent (alpha).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Proton dose (up to 7.6 x 10^16/cm2), temperature (2 K to 77 K), and magnetic field dependence.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the extended Bean model for Jc estimation and the linear energy barrier approximation for flux creep.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows isothermal magnetization curves and Jc dependence; Figure 6 shows the reduced pinning force density as a function of reduced magnetic field.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Jc enhancement, describes the experimental irradiation and measurement setup, presents results on Tc suppression and Jc enhancement, analyzes vortex dynamics and pinning mechanism shifts, and concludes with a discussion on the implications for practical applications.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Critical current density is the key parameter for the practical application of superconductivity. In this study, 3 MeV proton irradiation experiments were conducted on HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_8$$_+$$_δ$ single crystals to introduce pinning centers. The critical current density is found to be strongly enhanced after the irradiation with its maximum at a dose of 1$\times$10$^{16}$/cm$^2$, where the self-field critical current density at 2 K is enhanced from 5.5 MA/cm$^2$ to 26 MA/cm$^2$. At 77 K, the self-field critical current density for all irradiated crystals is over 0.1 MA/cm$^2$. The power-law dependence of the critical current density on the magnetic field is observed after irradiation, with a large power-law exponent $α$ close to 1. A monotonic magnetic field dependence of the normalized magnetic relaxation rate is observed, which could be attributed to the low irreversibility field caused by the large anisotropy in Hg1223 single crystals. Through the analysis of the pinning force density of the crystal before and after irradiation, a clear mechanism change has been observed.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides an exact mathematical theory for how plasmons reflect and transmit at the edge of a metal gate in a 2D electron system. It corrects previous models by accounting for radiative losses and complex phase shifts, which is crucial for designing tunable terahertz plasmonic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the exact complex reflection and transmission coefficients of 2D plasmons when scattering at the boundary between gated and ungated regions of a 2D electron system.

**Main result.** The authors derived analytical expressions for complex coefficients that account for evanescent fields and radiative losses, showing that the reflection phase depends strongly on gate-2DES separation and contradicts simpler plane-wave matching models.

**Method.** The Wiener-Hopf technique was used to solve the wave equation for vector potentials, involving Fourier transforms and residue calculus.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional electron system (2DES) partially covered by a perfectly conducting gate at a distance d above the 2DES plane.

**Key observables.** Complex reflection and transmission coefficients (amplitude and phase), power reflectance, and power transmittance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Gate-2DES separation (qud), normalized conductivity (eta), non-retarded limit, and small separation limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The conductivity is assumed to be primarily imaginary (low dissipation), and the theory focuses on the non-retarded limit.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the dependence of reflection/transmission amplitudes and phases on conductivity and separation, highlighting the divergence from the plane-wave matching approximation and the behavior of power reflectance/transmittance.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scattering problem, defines the physical model and parameters, applies the Wiener-Hopf method to derive exact coefficients, compares results with the plane-wave matching approximation, and discusses implications for plasmonic crystals.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop an exact theory of plasmon scattering at the boundary between gated and ungated regions of a two-dimensional electron system (2DES). Using the Wiener-Hopf technique, we derive analytical expressions for the complex reflection and transmission coefficients of plasmons incident from both sides of the interface. The theory fully accounts for evanescent fields at the gate edge and radiative losses into free-space electromagnetic waves. In the non-retarded limit and for small gate-2DES separation, the reflected plasmon dominates the total electric field, while radiative losses are negligible when plasmon scattering. The amplitudes and phases of the reflection and transmission coefficients for plasmons incident from both sides have a complex dependence from 2DES-gate separation and conductivity of 2DES. Our results provide a rigorous foundation for modeling tunable plasmonic crystals based on 2DES for terahertz detection and modulation.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a more accurate mapping of the Weyl node landscape in AMTe4 semimetals by considering multiple electronic subbands. It successfully bridges the gap between theory and experiment by showing that Weyl nodes located very close to the Fermi energy are responsible for previously unexplained transport phenomena.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses discrepancies between theoretical predictions and experimental observations (such as quantum oscillations and magnetoresistance) in AMTe4 Weyl semimetals, specifically regarding the true topological landscape of Weyl nodes.

**Main result.** The authors reveal a 'tiered' Weyl node landscape where many nodes exist much closer to the Fermi energy than previously thought, including the discovery of Type-III Weyl nodes and a unified explanation for anomalous transport features like Hall kinks and non-saturating magnetoresistance.

**Method.** The researchers employed Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the FPLO code with LDA and GGA functionals, combined with Wannier models and a generalized search procedure that accounts for multiple subbands near the Fermi energy.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of a family of layered, noncentrosymmetric orthorhombic transition metal tellurides, specifically AMTe4 where A = Ta, Nb and M = Ir, Rh.

**Key observables.** Weyl point energy and type (I, II, III), chirality, Fermi surface topology (electron/hole pockets), magnetoresistance scaling (B^1.5 vs B^2), de Haas-van Alphen frequencies, and Hall effect kinks.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fermi energy (Ef), Weyl point energy (Ewp), magnetic field (B), Berry curvature, and chemical pressure (substitution of Ir with Rh).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the validity of DFT-based approximations (LDA/GGA) and relies on the accuracy of structural relaxation models to represent experimental reality.

**Figures summary.** Figures include Weyl node positions and energy distributions in the Brillouin zone, Fermi surface topologies (including hole and electron pockets), and the angular dependence of experimental dHvA frequencies.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by identifying the discrepancy between theory and experiment, presents a new computational approach for scanning subbands, details the resulting Weyl node landscape and types, compares different material compositions (Ta vs Nb), and concludes by linking these topological features to observed magnetotransport anomalies.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Key aspects of the quantum oscillations and magnetoresistance in Weyl semimetals $AM$Te$_4$ ($A$=Nb,Ta, $M$=Rh, Ir) persist as open questions, obscuring the link between their topological electronic structure and practical implementations. Employing a generalised search procedure, we carry out a comprehensive scan of WPs accounting for all the subbands close to the Fermi energy, and show that this dramatically alters the WP landscape in these compounds. In particular, we predict these compounds to feature WPs within a few meV of the Fermi energy which significantly influence their properties. Remarkably, most of the considered compounds host WPs of more than one type, including NbRhTe$_4$ which hosts type-I, II and III Weyl points. Our comparative analysis of structure and fidelity of computational parameters/models not only provides a detailed mapping of the complex electronic structure in these compounds, but also clarifies quantum oscillations and magnetoresistance observations in this family, bridging the gap between theory and experiments and offering a framework for precise tunability of WPs.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates how controlling the sputtering pressure of platinum thin films allows for the tuning of residual strain, which directly impacts electrocatalytic performance. It reveals that compressive strain optimizes hydrogen adsorption for the hydrogen evolution reaction, providing a way to engineer catalysts via deposition parameters.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how deposition-induced residual strain and microstructural changes in magnetron-sputtered platinum thin films influence the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) activity.

**Main result.** Low sputter pressure films exhibit the highest HER activity due to compressive strain that optimizes hydrogen adsorption energy, whereas higher pressures lead to reduced activity as tensile strain and increased surface roughness/porosity dominate.

**Method.** The researchers used RF magnetron sputtering to tune stress, Digital Holographic Microscopy and XRD for strain/stress analysis, electrochemical measurements (LSV, CV, EIS) for activity, and DFT calculations to model hydrogen adsorption energetics.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of magnetron-sputtered platinum (Pt) thin films (60-70 nm) with a Tantalum adhesion layer on Silicon substrates, where sputter pressure is used to control the transition between compressive and tensile stress states.

**Key observables.** Residual in-plane and out-of-plane strain, surface roughness (Ra,Rq), electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), hydrogen adsorption free energy (delta G_H), and HER overpotential.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sputter pressure (0.13 to 3.33 Pa), biaxial in-plane strain, and hydrogen surface coverage.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Thermal stress contribution from substrate heating is considered negligible; biaxial modulus was approximated using single-crystal elastic constants for Pt(111).

**Figures summary.** XRD patterns and out-of-plane strain vs. pressure; TEM images of morphology transition; correlation of lattice parameters with pressure; CV curves and ECSA/roughness trends; HER polarization curves and theoretical hydrogen adsorption free energy landscapes.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing the link between sputter pressure and residual stress, moves to characterizing the resulting microstructural and crystallographic changes, evaluates the electrochemical HER performance, and concludes with DFT calculations to provide a mechanistic thermodynamic explanation.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Strain has emerged as a promising approach for tuning electrocatalytic properties, yet its role in sputter-deposited thin films remains poorly understood. In this work, magnetron-sputtered platinum (Pt) thin films with different stress states were prepared by varying the sputter pressure. The resulting changes in microstructure, residual strain, and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity were investigated using complementary characterization techniques and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Structural analysis reveals a transition of (111)-textured Pt thin films from dense and smooth films at low pressures, to more porous microstructures with increased roughness at higher pressures. Electrochemical measurements show that films deposited at low sputter pressure exhibit the highest HER activity, while higher sputter pressures lead to reduced activity despite increased surface area. DFT calculations demonstrate that lattice strain alters hydrogen adsorption energetics and surface coverage on Pt(111), providing a mechanistic explanation for the observed activity trends. Overall, the results highlight that HER activity in sputtered Pt thin films is governed by the interplay of residual strain, microstructure, and hydrogen coverage.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Genus-protected higher-order topological phases](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06383v1)

**Authors:** Shahroze Shahab, Hui Liu, Daniel Varjas, Ion Cosma Fulga  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06383v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06383_figures/2605.06383_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 2. A cylinder consisting of an odd number of Kitaev chains (17 in this example). The 2D system is topologically equivalent to the Corbino disk of Fig. 1(b), having one hole (g = 1). There are two disjoint edges, each of which hosts an odd number of Majorana modes.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.06383_figures/2605.06383_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 1. Second-order states on different geometries. (a) On the disk, zero modes can be gapped out by bringing them together along the edges. (b) In a Corbino-disk geometry, the zero modes cannot be removed without closing the bulk gap, simply because they occur on two disjoint edges. (c) On the sphere, the 1D edge mode can be moved along the surface until it shrinks to a point (e.g. at the north pole) and gaps out. (d) In a torus-shaped 3D system, propagating modes on non-contractible loops cannot be removed without traversing the bulk.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<img src="2605.06383_figures/2605.06383_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Probability density and low-energy spectrum near E = 0 of the Hamiltonian Eq. (1) discretized on a Corbino-disk geometry with local edge defects, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b), for a system of size (L, l) = (9.5, 4.5). In the real-space plots, darker color indicates a higher probability density. The insets show the energy spectrum E/t and the two red points correspond to the localized Majorana zero modes. (a) In the dimerized regime (δt = −0.5t), the system hosts two Majorana zero modes. (b) In the opposite regime (δt = +0.5t), two Majorana zero modes are again present, but they localize on the opposite pair of corners.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.06383_figures/2605.06383_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Model of a 2D second-order topological superconduc- tor Eq. (1). Gray circles denote Majorana modes. (a) Real- space representation of the model Hamiltonian with hopping amplitudes t1,2 = t ± δt. Majoranas hopping along the red arrows acquire an additional π phase, resulting in a mag- netic flux threading each lattice plaquette. (b) Realization of a GPT phase by introducing local boundary defects on the square lattice with a square hole. Majorana modes located inside the red dashed rectangles are removed modifying the boundary geometry without affecting the bulk.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 5</summary>

<img src="2605.06383_figures/2605.06383_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 5. BBH model with a non-uniform dimerization pattern. (a) The system hosts two MZMs in the topological regime, due to the presence of two local boundary defects (i.e. removing two sites). (b) Introducing a non-uniform dimerization pat- tern allows the realization of a trivial phase.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new class of higher-order topological phases protected by the global topology (genus) of a system rather than crystalline symmetries. It shows that in geometries like tori or Corbino disks, boundary modes are robust against surface perturbations. The work provides a complete topological classification for these phases in 2D and 3D.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether higher-order topological phases (HOTPs) can be protected by the global topology (genus) of a system's shape, rather than relying on specific crystalline symmetries.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate the existence of 'genus-protected' topological phases (GPT phases) where boundary modes are protected by the bulk gap and the system's genus, and provide a topological classification using homology theory.

**Method.** The study uses Volterra construction to introduce topological defects (dislocations and disclinations) and employs the Kwant package for numerical simulations of energy spectra, probability densities, and scattering-matrix invariants.

**Model / system.** The research focuses on 2D and 3D systems, including the Benalcazar-Bernevig-Hughes (BBH) model on Corbino disks, Class-D Hamiltonians, and Class-DIII Hamiltonians on toroidal geometries.

**Key observables.** Majorana zero modes (MZMs), helical hinge/surface modes, energy spectra, real-space probability densities, and the reflection-matrix determinant (det(r)).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Genus (g), number of holes, bond dimerization (delta t), chemical potential (mu), and anisotropy parameter (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The protection of boundary modes assumes the preservation of the bulk gap and fundamental local symmetries (e.g., time-reversal, particle-hole, or chiral symmetry).

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the construction of higher-order topological phases, the effect of defects like disclinations, real-space probability densities of localized modes, and the topological classification for different genus surfaces.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic HOTPs, presents construction schemes for GPT phases using local and global defects, provides numerical verification via scattering invariants, and concludes with a general topological classification using homology theory.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Higher-order topological phases (HOTPs) feature protected gapless modes on boundaries of higher codimension, such as the corners or hinges of a crystal. They are understood as being protected by lattice symmetries: If the latter are broken, it becomes possible to remove the boundary modes without closing the bulk gap. In this work, we present construction schemes for HOTPs protected solely by the bulk gap, by fundamental symmetries, and by the global topology of the system shape (its genus, or number of holes), independent of any crystalline symmetries. As long as the fundamental local symmetries are preserved, the resulting boundary states cannot be removed by any purely-surface perturbation.

</details>

(2) describes the upper branch (ω = nωc + Ωn &gt; nωc), but the splitting Ωn, the curvature scaling, and the resulting n−1 ordering of peak heights are common to both. The plotted quantity is the normalized peak amplitude aν = αν(Iinc)/αν,0 = ∆Rν(Iinc)/[AνIinc] versus the scaled intensity Iinc/Ifit 0,ν, where Ifit 0,ν is fitted per trace as the one-parameter horizontal rescaling. **Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical framework to separate intrinsic electronic response from experimental artifacts in graphene's Bernstein modes. It establishes a universal scaling law for harmonic intensity ratios and predicts distinct power-dependent saturation behaviors for Bernstein modes versus cyclotron resonance.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to develop a robust protocol for decomposing Bernstein mode (BM) absorption in graphene into intrinsic dielectric properties and device-specific effects, while also establishing a link between linear absorption and nonlinear saturation.

**Main result.** The authors derive a factorization theorem for BM peak absorption and show that inter-harmonic ratios follow a parameter-free m/n baseline, which is robust against launcher/dielectric errors but sensitive to linewidth assumptions. They also demonstrate that BM and cyclotron resonance (CR) follow distinct nonlinear saturation scaling laws.

**Method.** The study uses kinetic plasma theory, a hot-electron energy-balance model, and asymptotic analysis (Debye-Olfer/Airy functions) alongside synthetic recovery tests using Monte-Carlo simulations and full q-integral benchmarks.

**Model / system.** The physical system is graphene in the quasiclassical, ballistic, and degenerate regime under a perpendicular magnetic field, specifically focusing on finite-wavevector Bernstein modes excited via near-field metallic contacts.

**Key observables.** BM peak absorption amplitudes, inter-harmonic intensity ratios (In/Im), linewidths (Gamma), and nonlinear saturation curves.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Cyclotron frequency (omega_c), cyclotron mass (m_c), wavevector (q approx omega/v_F), linewidth exponent (s_BM = 1/2), and the bolometric transfer coefficient (beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the quasiclassical regime (neglecting Landau quantization), a ballistic transport regime, and that the launcher/dielectric prefactors vary slowly across the resonance window.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental geometry; Figure 2 shows numerical agreement between the factorized formula and full q-integrals; Figure 3 illustrates kinematic weight functions; Figure 5 displays normalized nonlinear saturation master curves; Figure 6 shows a stress test for model misspecification.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the BM absorption factorization, moves to the derivation of universal inter-harmonic ratios and their robustness, introduces a protocol for extracting intrinsic residues, extends the theory to nonlinear power-dependent saturation, and concludes with validation via synthetic stress tests.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Bernstein modes (BM) in graphene are finite-wavevector magnetoplasmons excited by contact near fields, whereas ordinary cyclotron resonance (CR) probes $q\approx0$. We derive the BM peak absorption in the quasiclassical ballistic regime and show that it factorizes into a launch spectrum, Bernstein-mode splitting, turning-point enhancement, and residual dielectric-response factor. At fixed excitation frequency, BM overtones ($n\ge2$) are sampled, to leading order, at the same momentum $q\simeqω/v_F$. Smooth launch and screening factors therefore cancel in inter-harmonic peak ratios, yielding $I_n/I_m\simeq m/n$, modified by linewidth corrections and one residual response ratio for each harmonic pair. In smooth-launcher synthetic tests, noisy full-$q$ spectra recover the residual ratio within errors: moderate launcher/dielectric misspecification within this benchmark family shifts it by only $\sim\!1$--$2\%$, whereas linewidth assumptions shift it by $\sim\!10$--$30\%$. The same factorization connects low-power amplitudes to nonlinear saturation. If BM harmonics share the same cooling region and bolometric readout, the low-power slope times onset intensity is harmonic independent, while BM and CR power sweeps obey distinct normalized saturation curves with linewidth scalings $Γ^{-1/2}$ and $Γ^{-1}$.

</details>

**Authors:** Hailang Qin, Xiao-Yu Yan, Hanbin Deng, Mu-Wei Gao, Guowei Liu, Yuanyuan Zhao, Jia-Xin Yin  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06150v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** The paper explores the transition of the Josephson effect from tunneling to the deep point-contact regime in kagome superconductors. It reveals that observed conductance saturation at high coupling is an artifact of series resistance, providing a cautionary note for interpreting exotic physics. Finally, it identifies the best regime for using JSTM to study pair-density wave states.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the behavior of the Josephson current and zero-bias conductance (ZBC) in JSTM as the junction transitions from the tunneling regime to the deep point-contact regime, and identifying measurement artifacts.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that ZBC saturation at extremely low junction resistances is an experimental artifact caused by series resistance, and identifies an optimal conductance regime for probing pair-density wave states.

**Method.** Josephson scanning tunneling microscopy (JSTM) using superconducting tips on kagome superconductors, involving $dI/dV$ spectroscopy, $I-z$ spectroscopy, and calibration of series resistance.

**Model / system.** Kagome superconductors, specifically Cs(V0.86Ta0.14)3Sb5 and KV3Sb5, studied using a superconducting Nb or Pt-Ir tip in a dilution refrigerator.

**Key observables.** Zero-bias conductance (ZBC), normal-state conductance (Gn), superconducting gap (delta), and critical current (Ic).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Tunneling regime (Gn <= G0), crossover/point-contact regime (G0 <= Gn <= 2G0), and deep point-contact regime (Gn > 2G0); series resistance (Rseries).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the ZBC saturation is driven by external circuit resistance rather than intrinsic physics like Majorana zero modes.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Comparison of spectra with superconducting vs non-superconducting tips; Fig 2: I-V and dI/dV spectra across varying resistances; Fig 3: ZBC vs Gn relationship and topographic surface modification; Fig 4: Investigation of optimal regime for PDW probing.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to JSTM; experimental setup and calibration; exploration of conductance regimes via I-V and I-z spectroscopy; identification of ZBC saturation artifact; demonstration of optimal regime for PDW mapping.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Josephson scanning tunneling microscopy (JSTM) has emerged as an important technique for probing the superconducting order parameter at the atomic scale. However, the Josephson current in JSTM may behave quite differently when the coupling strength varies. Here, we push the junction to the deep point-contact regime, reaching a normal-state junction resistance of only 0.15 $h/2e^2 \simeq 2~{\rm k}Ω$. We demonstrate, using kagome superconductors, that the zero-bias conductance, a key characteristic of the Josephson current, deviates strongly from the quadratic dependence on the normal-state conductance upon entering the deep point-contact regime. Furthermore, we observe a striking saturation of the zero-bias conductance, which we show arises from the series resistance in the circuit. This also serves as a cautious reminder when interpreting zero-bias conductance saturation or quantization in studies of exotic physics such as that of Majorana zero modes if the tip-sample junction resistance is extremely small. Finally, we identify an optimum regime where JSTM can be used as an atomic-scale probe for studying pair-density wave states in materials with low superconducting transition temperature, such as AV3Sb5 kagome superconductors.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.05981"></a>
### [Massive Mitigation of Transport AC Losses in Superconducting Hybrid CORC-TSTC Cables](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05981v1)

**Authors:** Hasan N. Al-Ssalih, Antonio Badía-Majós, Harold S. Ruiz  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05981v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.05981_figures/2605.05981_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>

Pictorial representation of hybrid CORC-TSTC cables with current feeders in the so-called: a) non-insulated configuration where CORC &amp; TSTC conductors are electrically connected in the terminals; b) the insulated configuration where the CORC and TSTC are electrically decoupled and; c) Simplified view of the helical winding characteristic of CORC Tapes, enclosing the twisted tapes of the TSTC along a pitch length.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 2</summary>

<img src="2605.05981_figures/2605.05981_fig2.jpg" width="500"><br>

Simulated AC loss and transport current distribution in 6-by-6 and 6-by-2 hybrid CORC-TSTC cables subjected to an applied transport current equal to 90% of the cable’s rated capacity, i.e., Ia = 0.9 × ntapes × Ic, at a frequency of 60 Hz, and Ic = 190 A at 77 K. (a) Transport current response for the non-insulated and insulated configurations normalized by the theoretical maximum current allowed at the CORC conductor (ICORC = 1140 A) ; (b) Exemplary cumulative losses for the 6-by-6 hybrid cables in the insulated and non-insulated configurations, highlighting the individual loss contributions from each conductor. (c) Shows a zoom in of the relevant losses displayed at (b).</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 3</summary>

<sub>Figure 3. Percentual loss reduction of the 6 by 6 insulated CORC-TSTC hybrid cable under transport current with respect to a non-insulated hybrid cable with the same number of tapes.</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05981_figures/2605.05981_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 4. a) AC losses as function of the normalized rated current of the hybrid CORC-TSTC cable, ia = Ia/(ntapes× &lt; Ic &gt;), for non-insulated (red curves) and insulated (green curves) layouts (Fig. 1), each comprising six 2G-HTS tapes in the CORC conductor and within; a TSTC conductor made of up to (a) six 2G-HTS tapes -squares-, that are displayed for comparison against (b) a compelling hybrid cable with two 2G-HTS tapes -triangles-.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** The study investigates how the electrical connection between components in hybrid superconducting cables affects energy dissipation. It finds that by insulating the conductors at the feeding point to prevent current redistribution, AC losses can be reduced by up to 90%. This provides a scalable method for designing more efficient high-current power cables.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** High transport AC losses in hybrid superconducting cables (CORC-TSTC) limit their use in power transmission, specifically due to current redistribution between conductors.

**Main result.** Implementing an insulated feeding strategy that electrically decouples the CORC and TSTC conductors suppresses inter-conductor current exchange, reducing transport AC losses by up to 90% at high operating currents.

**Method.** A 3D electromagnetic finite-element model was implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics using a non-linear E-J constitutive law and validated against magnetization experiments.

**Model / system.** A hybrid superconducting cable architecture consisting of an outer Conductor-on-Round-Core (CORC) layer and an inner Twisted Stacked-Tape Conductor (TSTC) core, utilizing 2G-HTS REBCO tapes operating at 77 K.

**Key observables.** Transport AC losses, current density distributions, current waveform distortion, and phase shifts.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Operating temperature (77 K), frequency (60 Hz), transport current amplitude (1520 A to 2280 A), and the critical current threshold of the CORC layers.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on self-field transport current conditions and assumes the effectiveness of insulation is localized to the feeding point.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the non-insulated vs. insulated feeding geometries; Figure 2 shows current waveform distortion and cumulative AC loss contributions; Figure 3 shows the percentage reduction in losses; Figure 4 compares AC losses across different TSTC tape counts and configurations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of AC losses in hybrid cables, describes the 3D electromagnetic modeling approach, compares non-insulated and insulated feeding schemes, analyzes the resulting current redistribution and waveform distortion, and concludes with the benefits of electrical decoupling.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

High-current superconducting cables are emerging as key enablers for next-generation power transmission systems; however, their deployment is often limited by transport AC losses. Hybrid superconducting cables combining Conductor-on-Round-Core (CORC) and Twisted Stacked-Tape Conductor (TSTC) architectures have recently been proposed as a promising route toward cables with high current capacity and compact form factors. However, their electrodynamic response under transport current operation remains poorly understood, particularly regarding how current injection conditions govern internal current redistribution. Here, we employ a fully-3D electromagnetic model, previously validated against magnetisation experiments in equivalent cables, to investigate the influence of current injection strategy on the electrodynamics of hybrid CORC-TSTC cables under self-field conditions. By comparing configurations in which the total current is either injected through a common connection between the CORC and TSTC conductors (non-insulated feeding) or supplied independently to each conductor (insulated feeding), we show that electrical coupling in non-insulated designs leads to strong current redistribution, pronounced waveform distortion and elevated AC losses once the CORC layers approach magnetic saturation. In contrast, independent current feeding suppresses inter-conductor current exchange, stabilises the current waveforms, and exhibits an outstanding reduction in transport AC losses of up to 90% at practical operating currents, compared with conventional feeding schemes. These findings reveal the central role of the current injection strategy in governing the internal electrodynamics and energy dissipation of hybrid superconducting cables, and identify the electrical decoupling of the constituent conductors at the feeding point as a simple and scalable route toward ultra-efficient power cables.

</details>

### [Moire based strain analysis in wurtzite GaAs -- rock-salt (Pb,Sn)Te core-shell nanowires grown by molecular beam epitaxy](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06603v1)

**Authors:** Maciej Wojcik, Sania Dad, Piotr Dziawa, Slawomir Kret, Wojciech Pacuski, Janusz Sadowski  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06603v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<details open><summary>📷 Fig 1</summary>

<img src="2605.06603_figures/2605.06603_fig1.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 2 demonstrates close up view on the core-shell interface of wz-GaAs/Pb0.47Sn0.53Te NW.</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper explores the structural properties of GaAs/(Pb,Sn)Te core-shell nanowires, focusing on how lattice mismatch creates dislocations and moiré patterns. It demonstrates that analyzing moiré fringes can effectively estimate strain in these topological crystalline insulator heterostructures.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the interface structure, strain distribution, and dislocation density in wurtzite GaAs/(Pb,Sn)Te core-shell nanowire heterostructures.

**Main result.** The study validates moiré pattern analysis as a viable alternative to Geometric Phase Analysis for estimating strain and proves that the (Pb,Sn)Te shell consists of connected fragments with uneven strain distribution.

**Method.** Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) for growth, followed by High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), STEM-HAADF, and Geometric Phase Analysis (GPA).

**Model / system.** Core-shell nanowires consisting of a wurtzite (wz) GaAs core and a rock-salt (Pb,Sn)Te topological crystalline insulator (TCI) shell.

**Key observables.** Misfit dislocations, moiré fringe spacing (axial and perpendicular), lattice parameters, and dislocation density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Shell thickness (~10 nm), core diameter (~70 nm), Sn content (x), and lattice mismatch percentages (1.7% to 4% axially, higher laterally).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The shell is treated as a quasi-continuous structure composed of connected fragments.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2(b) shows FFT patterns for lattice mismatch; Figure 5(a) visualizes moiré fringes in axial and perpendicular directions, highlighting the difference in fringe spacing due to anisotropic mismatch.

**Paper structure.** The paper describes the growth process via MBE, moves to structural characterization using TEM/STEM, performs strain analysis via GPA and moiré patterns, and concludes with a comparison of theoretical vs. measured dislocation/moiré distances.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate core/shell GaAs/(Pb,Sn)Te nanowire nanoheterostructures with wurtzite (wz) GaAs cores and (Pb,Sn)Te topological crystalline insulator shells. The nanostructures have been grown by molecular beam epitaxy using two distinct MBE systems dedicated to III-V, and IV-VI semiconductors. The interface structure of wz-GaAs/(Pb,Sn)Te nanowires is investigated using high resolution transmission electron microscopy, scanning transmission electron microscopy and geometric phase analysis. Misfit dislocations and moiré fringes are observed as a direct result of the lattice mismatch between the core and the shell materials, and used to estimate strain in crystalline topological insulator shells. Our results point to a possibility of using moiré patterns analysis as an alternative, for estimating strain in the core-shell nanowire structures.

</details>

</details>

**Summary.** This study uses molecular dynamics to quantify how extended dislocation networks soften the mechanical response of 3C-SiC. It demonstrates that increasing defect density reduces the material's elastic moduli and Debye temperature by up to 6%. The research highlights the importance of using accurate potentials like ABOP to capture the intrinsic anisotropy of the material.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how extended structural defects, such as Shockley partial dislocations and stacking faults, influence the elastic and thermal properties of cubic silicon carbide (3C-SiC).

**Main result.** Increasing the density of extended defects leads to a reduction in the material's stiffness (up to 4-6%) and a decrease in the Debye temperature, as the defects distort sp3 bonds into more compliant hexagonal-like arrangements.

**Method.** Large-scale molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the LAMMPS software package, employing both Vashishta and Analytic Bond-Order Potentials (ABOP).

**Model / system.** The physical system is cubic silicon carbide (3C-SiC) containing various extended defect configurations, including Shockley partial dislocations, stacking faults, and dislocation complexes (double and triple).

**Key observables.** Elastic stiffness constants (C11, C12, C44), effective elastic moduli (Bulk, Shear, Young's), Universal Elastic Anisotropy Index, Debye temperature, and phonon energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Defect concentrations, crystallographic directions ([111], [110], [112]), and interatomic potential types (Vashishta vs. ABOP).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of Voigt-Reuss-Hill averaging is assumed applicable to single crystals with defects; the Vashishta potential is noted to be less accurate for shear modulus predictions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the influence of defect concentration on elastic constants; Figure 2 displays directional Young's and shear moduli; Figure 5 and 6 show VRH-averaged moduli; Figure 7 illustrates the reduction in Debye temperature.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of defect-induced mechanical changes, details the MD simulation setup and interatomic potentials, presents the mathematical framework for calculating elastic and acoustic properties, and concludes with a comparative analysis of results against experimental data.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this study, large-scale molecular dynamics simulations with the Vashishta potential and the analytic bond-order potential (ABOP) were performed to investigate the effect of extended defects on the elastic properties of cubic silicon carbide (3C-SiC). Specifically, we focused on systems containing Shockley partial dislocations terminating stacking faults, along with double and triple dislocation complexes. The changes in the independent elastic stiffness constants C11, C12 and C44 upon varying the mentioned extended defects concentrations were quantified. Using the values of these constants, the effective bulk, shear, and Young's moduli were calculated for different defect types and concentrations. The moduli were calculated along particular crystallographic directions aligned with the mentioned defect configurations as well as evaluated using Voigt-Reuss-Hill averaging to provide overall orientation-independent characterization of the defect-altered lattice. The obtained results reveal a general trend of diminishing the material's stiffness with increasing densities of Shockley partial dislocations and dislocation complexes. Depending on the defect configuration, the average elastic moduli decrease by up to approximately 6 % with the Vashishta potential and up to about 4 % using the analytic bond-order potential. At this, triple dislocation complexes induce smaller perturbations. These findings demonstrate that extended defect networks can measurably modify the elastic response of 3C-SiC and should be considered in further scientific research and practical applications of this material.

</details>

### [Nonlinear Hall quantum oscillations to probe topological Brown-Zak fermions in graphene moiré systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05681v1)

**Authors:** Jinrui Zhong, Huimin Peng, Yuqing Hu, Qi Feng, Qiuli Li, Shihao Zhang, Qinsheng Wang, Jinhai Mao, Junxi Duan, Yugui Yao  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05681v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</details>

</details>

<img src="2605.05681_figures/2605.05681_fig3.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 3. Independence of in-plane magnetic field component and displacement fields in</sub>

<img src="2605.05681_figures/2605.05681_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

**Summary.** The researchers identify a new type of quantum oscillation in the nonlinear Hall effect of graphene moiré systems. These oscillations allow for the sensitive detection of Brown-Zak fermions at much lower magnetic fields than traditional methods. Crucially, the study proves that the nonlinear transport at the commensurability point is driven by quantum geometric properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty in detecting the quantum geometric properties and topological nature of Brown-Zak fermions in the linear response regime due to the dominance of Landau levels and high magnetic field requirements.

**Main result.** The discovery of Nonlinear Hall Brown-Zak Oscillations (NHBZO) and the first experimental demonstration that the nonlinear transport of Brown-Zak fermions is primarily governed by quantum geometric contributions.

**Method.** Second-harmonic transport measurements in twisted double bilayer graphene, utilizing scaling analysis of temperature-dependent conductivity and angle-dependent magnetic field sweeps.

**Model / system.** Twisted double bilayer graphene (TDBG) moiré superlattices with a twist angle greater than 2.5 degrees, operated in a regime where electronic correlations are minimized to focus on quantum geometry.

**Key observables.** Second-order nonlinear Hall voltage (V_y,2w), longitudinal resistance (R_xx), Hall resistivity (rho_xy), and the differential nonlinear Hall signal (dV_y,2w/dB).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field (B) and tilt angle (theta), carrier density (n), displacement field (D), temperature (T), and driving current (I_omega).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The twist angle is large enough to minimize electronic correlation effects, allowing the focus to remain on quantum geometric properties.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows TDBG characterization and the observation of nonlinear Hall oscillations; Fig 2 maps the oscillations in the (n, 1/B) plane; Fig 3 demonstrates the orbital nature and D-independence of the peaks; Fig 4 presents the temperature-dependent scaling analysis of the nonlinear Hall conductivity.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with device characterization and confirmation of nonlinear transport, moves to the identification of new quantum oscillations (NHBZO), proves their orbital and commensurability-driven nature, and concludes with a scaling analysis to isolate quantum geometric contributions.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Due to the deep connection with the quantum geometry of electronic Bloch wavefunctions, the second-order nonlinear Hall effect (NLHE) has been an attractive topic since its proposal. However, studies on NLHE under a magnetic field have been lacking. Given that quantum oscillations in the linear response regime have been proven to be useful tools in investigating electronic systems, searching for quantum oscillations in NLHE is of great interest and is expected to provide new avenues to unveil rich quantum geometric properties of novel quasiparticles. Here, we propose a new type of NLHE quantum oscillations and experimentally probe it in graphene moiré systems. It stems from the alternation of the dominant NLHE mechanisms with recurring Bloch states under magnetic field, which enables sensitive detection of Brown-Zak fermions, giving an onset field as low as 0.5 T. Most importantly, when the commensurability condition is satisfied, the nonlinear transport of Brown-Zak fermions is mainly governed by quantum geometric contributions. Our findings not only establish a new type of quantum oscillations, but also demonstrate the first experimental detection of the topological nature of Brown-Zak fermions, shedding light on the exploration of novel topological quasiparticles.

</details>

**Authors:** Andrei A. Shiryaev, Evgeni A. Ekimov  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06539v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<img src="2605.06539_figures/2605.06539_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>

<img src="2605.06539_figures/2605.06539_fig5.jpg" width="500"><br>

</details>

**Summary.** This study investigates the origin of the Fano resonance observed in the infrared spectra of hydrogenated nanodiamonds. By analyzing various grain sizes, the authors rule out C-H stretching vibrations as the cause and propose that the resonance arises from the coupling of diamond optical phonons with monohydride bending modes. This provides insight into the surface chemistry and electronic properties of nanodiamond-based materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the precise physical mechanism behind the Fano resonance (the 'transmission window') and surface conductivity in hydrogenated nanodiamonds.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that C-H stretch vibrations are not responsible for the Fano resonance, suggesting instead that the bending mode of monohydride termination on the (111) face couples with the diamond optical phonon.

**Method.** Infrared (IR) spectroscopy was performed on nanodiamonds of varying sizes (2.6-30 nm) using both transflection and transmission geometries, followed by spectral decomposition and Pearson correlation analysis.

**Model / system.** Hydrogenated nanodiamond grains with diameters ranging from 2.6 to 30 nm, featuring surface functional groups and potential graphitic islets.

**Key observables.** Fano-related absorption dips near the diamond Raman frequency (1332 cm-1), C-H stretching and bending vibrational modes, and surface conductivity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nanodiamond grain size (2.6-30 nm), temperature, and the presence of monohydride termination or graphitic domains.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes specific Lorentzian and Gaussian shapes for spectral decomposition and relies on the accuracy of polynomial fits for background subtraction.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows raw IR spectra and spectral decomposition; Fig 2 plots the lack of correlation between C-H and Fano-related bands; Fig 3 and 4 show the inverse relationship between C-H stretches and Fano peaks, and the positive correlation between C-H bending and the Fano component, respectively; Fig 5 shows a specific absorption peak at 2682 cm-1.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Fano resonance problem, describes the synthesis and spectroscopic methods, presents correlation analyses between various vibrational modes, proposes a coupling mechanism involving the (111) face, and discusses the role of grain size and morphology.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Hydrogenated nanodiamonds may show a "transmission window" in infra-red spectra in the vicinity of diamond Raman frequency. This phenomenon is a manifestation of resonance coupling of incident photons with continuum states (Fano resonance). Hpwever, precise mechanism of appearence of the resonance and of related conductivity - surface hydrogenation or specific type of surface reconstruction - remains debatable. We present detailed analysis of infra-red spectra of nanodiamonds of different sizes (2.6-30 nm) possessing the "transmission window" and show that the C-H stretch vibrations of adsorbed functional groups cannot be responsible the the Fano resonance. At the same time, it is suggested that a bending mode of monohydride termination on nanodiamond (111) face may couple with diamond optical phonon, explaining the Fano resonance in some cases. The relative importance of the monohydride contribution and of the graphitic islets to the appearence of the "transmission window" and conductivity is likely dependent on dominating morphology and size distribution of nanodiamond grains.

</details>

**Authors:** Reiley Dorrian, Mizuki Ohno, Elena Williams, Adrian Llanos, Joseph Falson  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06514v1>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.06514_figures/2605.06514_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>FIG. 4. Characteristic scattering times relevant to pair-breaking as estimated by KLB, KF, and</sub>

</details>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how different depairing mechanisms like orbital and magnetic scattering affect superconductivity in ultra-thin LaBi2 films. It demonstrates that using a more comprehensive model (KF) is essential to avoid significant errors in estimating spin-orbit scattering rates. The work provides a more accurate framework for interpreting the physics of two-dimensional, spin-orbit coupled superconductors.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Evaluating the accuracy of depairing models (KLB vs. KF) in describing superconductivity in ultra-thin, spin-orbit coupled films and understanding the mechanisms (orbital, paramagnetic, and magnetic) that suppress the superconducting state.

**Main result.** The KF model more accurately captures individual scattering channels and resolves a systematic artifact in the KLB model, which overestimates spin-orbit scattering time by neglecting orbital and magnetic effects. The study also identifies field-enhanced superconductivity in the ultra-thin limit due to the suppression of magnetic fluctuations.

**Method.** The researchers synthesized LaBi2 thin films via molecular beam epitaxy and performed low-temperature transport measurements in a vector magnet. They compared the KLB and KF theoretical frameworks by fitting temperature-dependent upper critical fields and using Drude analysis to estimate carrier properties.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of ultra-thin LaBi2 films (2.1 nm to 75 nm) with significant spin-orbit coupling. The study utilizes the multi-mechanism Kharitonov-Feigel'man (KF) model and the Klemm-Luther-Beasley (KLB) model to account for orbital pair-breaking, paramagnetic effects, and magnetic scattering.

**Key observables.** Zero-field critical temperature (Tc), in-plane upper critical field (Hc2), resistance transitions (R/R0), magnetoresistance, and Hall resistivity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Thickness (t), spin-orbit scattering time (tau_SO), transport scattering time (tau_tr), magnetic scattering rate (nu_s), and the Pauli limit violation ratio.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a dirty-limit regime (xi/l >> 1) and uses a free-electron approximation for estimating Fermi momentum, which may be limited by the actual effective mass in LaBi2.

**Figures summary.** XRD patterns and Laue fringe analysis for thickness estimation; normalized zero-field resistance transitions; comparison of KF and KLB model fits; and plots showing the sensitivity of scattering parameters to different fitting constraints.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of depairing mechanisms, describes the synthesis and characterization of LaBi2 films, presents transport measurement results, compares the KLB and KF models through fitting analysis, and discusses the implications for interpreting scattering rates in 2D superconductors.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

The response of ultra-thin superconducting materials under parallel magnetic fields is often leveraged to obtain insight into the nature of the condensate, including features attributable to unconventional forms of pairing. Despite there being multiple competing mechanisms responsible for suppressing superconductivity, it is common for these analyses to overlook certain depairing channels. Here we report an analysis of thickness dependent superconductivity in thin films of \ce{LaBi2} using the multi-mechanism Kharitonov-Feigel'man framework . By resolving field-enhanced superconductivity in the thin-limit, we obtain an estimate the role of spin exchange scattering, in addition to paramagnetic and orbital effects. Our analyses offer insight into how fundamental quantities such as the critical temperature as well as Pauli limit are defined, recasting the landscape for how scattering times in two-dimensional superconductors can be interpreted.

</details>

### [Physics-Grounded Understanding of Thermal Boundary Conductance between Ga$_2$O$_3$ and SiC from a Feedforward Neural Network Potential](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05620v1)

**Authors:** Nuohao Liu, Chen Shen, Yue Cao, Song Xue, Pingfan Wu, Zongfang Lin, Masood Mortazavi, Liang Peng, Izabela Szlufarska, Jiechen Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05620v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

In SiC, the main discrepancy appears near Γ around 25 THz, while in Ga2O3 several optical branches are slightly shifted, consistent with the fact that the DFT dispersions include non-analytical long-range dipole corrections, whereas local Machine-Learning Interatomic Potential (MLIPs) such as the present NEP describe interactions only within a finite cutoff. Importantly, at the...</sub>

</details>

<details><summary>📷 Fig 4</summary>

<img src="2605.05620_figures/2605.05620_fig4.jpg" width="500"><br>
<sub>Figure 3: Bulk vibrational and thermal validation of the learned potential. Phonon dispersions of (a) SiC and (b) Ga2O3 predicted by DFT and NEP. Temperature-dependent thermal conductivity for (c) SiC [8] and (d) Ga2O3 [5, 23] along different orientations from NEP (square markers) and pyhsical experiments (round markers)</sub>

</details>

<sub>Figure 4: Overall TBC trends from NEMD simulation. (a) Representative steady-state temperature profile. (b) Simulation cell and setup. (c) TBC versus transport length Lx. (d) TBC versus temperature. For both orientations, TBC decreases with increasing length and increases markedly with temperature.</sub>

**Summary.** This paper presents a machine-learning-based approach to predict and understand heat transfer at the Ga2O3/SiC interface. By using a neural network potential, the authors bridge the gap between expensive first-principles calculations and inaccurate empirical models, revealing how temperature and interface structure drive thermal conductance.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the lack of a unified, computationally efficient, and physically transparent understanding of thermal boundary conductance (TBC) at the Ga2O3/SiC interface, which is a bottleneck for power electronics.

**Main result.** The researchers developed a transferable Neural Network Potential (NEP) that accurately predicts TBC trends, showing that TBC increases with temperature due to enhanced anharmonic exchange and varies with interface orientation due to structural bonding strength.

**Method.** The study uses a Neuroevolution Potential (NEP) trained on DFT data, combined with large-scale Molecular Dynamics (NEMD, EMD, HNEMD) and lattice dynamics analysis (spectral conductance, coherence, and force-constant metrics).

**Model / system.** The system is a Ga2O3/SiC heterostructure, specifically focusing on beta-Ga2O3 and 4H-SiC interfaces with different orientations, modeled using a five-species neural network potential (Ga, O, Si, C, H).

**Key observables.** Thermal boundary conductance (TBC), thermal conductivity, frequency-resolved spectral conductance, cross-interface coherence, and interfacial force-constant (IFC) metrics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature ranges from 300 K to 900 K, simulation cell lengths up to 1200 Angstroms, and interface orientations (100) and (201).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interface is assumed to be atomically ordered (no defects or roughness), the transport analysis is classical (no quantum nuclear effects), and hydrogen is used only for surface passivation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 validates the NEP against DFT for phonon dispersions and thermal conductivity; Figure 4 shows NEMD setup and TBC trends; Figure 7 displays charge-density difference and spectral conductance; Figure 8 illustrates temperature-dependent covariance of coupling.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the heat removal problem, describes the development and validation of the NEP, presents large-scale transport simulations (TBC trends), provides a microscopic mechanistic analysis via spectral and structural metrics, and concludes with limitations.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ga$_2$O$_3$/SiC heterointegration is attractive for ultra-wide-bandgap power electronics, but interfacial thermal boundary conductance (TBC) remains a major heat-removal bottleneck. Direct experimental access to intrinsic atomistic interfacial transport remains limited, particularly for ideally synthesized materials with defect-free interfacial contact. First-principles simulations are too expensive at relevant length and time scales, while empirical Molecular Dynamics (MD) potentials often lack transferability across oxide and carbide bonding environments. We develop a unified feedforward neural network potential and validate it against density-functional data, bulk phonon dispersions, and anisotropic thermal-conductivity trends in both $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$ and SiC. Nonequilibrium simulations show that TBC decreases with transport length, increases with temperature, and is consistently higher for Ga$_2$O$_3$$(\bar{2}01)$/SiC(0001) than for Ga$_2$O$_3$(100)/SiC(0001). These trends are explained by attenuation of long-mean-free-path carriers, enhanced incoherent and anharmonic interfacial exchange within broadly unchanged spectral channels, and stronger bonding and vibrational coupling at the $(\bar{2}01)$ interface. The results show how a single transferable feedforward neural network potential can enable large-scale transport prediction and physics-grounded mechanistic understanding of thermal boundary conductance. Code for NEP training and simulation workflows is available at the project repository https://github.com/knowhow07/TBC_Ga2O3_SiC.git

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Polarizable atomic multipoles for learning long-range electrostatics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05746v1)

**Authors:** Dongjin Kim, Daniel S. King, Yoonjae Park, Roya Savoj, Sebastien Hamel, Xiaoyu Wang, Bingqing Cheng  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05746v1>

The test force root mean square errors (RMSEs) are compared between the baseline short-range models (hollow bars) and their long-range-augmented variants (solid bars) for different architectures (MACE [4], CACE [47], NequIP [3], and Allegro [49]). The colored bars represent the inclusion of long-range interactions: monopoles (-les, blue), monopoles and dipoles (-u, orange), monopoles, dipoles, and induced charges (-uiq, green), monopoles, dipoles, and induced...</sub>

<sub>Figure 2. Bulk water properties predicted by the MACE augmented with long-range electrostatics with latent charges, dipoles, and induced dipoles (MACELES-uiu) with anisotropic polarizability tensors, compared with DFT reference data and experi- ments. a: Parity plots of the Born effective charge (BEC) tensors predicted by the MACELES-uiu model (r = 4.5 ˚A, nl = 1) relative to RPBE-D3 DFT reference data for 100 bulk water configurations [50]. The left panel compares the diagonal compo- nents (Z∗ αα), while the right panel shows the off-diagonal components (Z∗ αβ with α ̸= β). The RMSE and R2 values are reported in each panel. b: Infrared (IR) absorption spectra of bulk water at different...</sub>

</details>

The lattice parameters a, b, and c correspond to the directions defined in panel b. Vertical orange and yellow bands mark the experimentally reported orthorhombic−tetragonal and tetragonal−cubic phase transition temperatures, respectively [74]. b: Illustrations of the orthorhombic, tetragonal, and cubic MAPbI3 phases. c: Parity plot of Born effective charge (BEC) tensor Z∗computed from MACELES-uiu...</sub>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new machine learning framework that incorporates polarizable atomic multipoles into interatomic potentials. This allows models to capture long-range electrostatic effects and predict complex properties like infrared and Raman spectra without the need for expensive electronic structure labels. It demonstrates significant improvements in accuracy for systems where long-range interactions are critical, such as water and perovskites.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Extending machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) to accurately capture long-range electrostatics and polarization in ionic, polar, and interfacial systems.

**Main result.** The introduction of a semi-local framework using polarizable atomic multipoles significantly improves potential energy surface accuracy and enables the prediction of physically meaningful electrical responses like Born effective charges and vibrational spectra.

**Method.** A semi-local framework using local equivariant descriptors to predict latent multipoles (monopole, dipole, quadrupole) combined with a non-self-consistent linear response approach for non-local charge transfer and polarization.

**Model / system.** The framework is applied to various systems including bulk liquid water, MAPbI3 perovskite, Na8/9Cl+8 clusters, and Au2 on MgO(001) surfaces, utilizing architectures like MACE, CACE, NequIP, and Allegro.

**Key observables.** Born effective charge (BEC) tensors, polarizabilities, infrared (IR) spectra, Raman spectra, and lattice constants.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Latent multipoles (q, u, Q), hardness (kappa), polarizability (alpha), and the electronic dielectric constant (epsilon_e).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes local atomic multipoles capture dominant interactions, treats non-local effects as small corrections, and neglects interactions between induced multipoles in the non-local response calculation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares force RMSE reduction across different systems; Figure 2/5/8 show parity plots for BEC tensors and polarizabilities; Figure 3 shows lattice constants and phase transitions in perovskites; Figure 3e/6 show predicted IR and Raman spectra.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the limitations of current MLIPs, introduces the multipole-based semi-local framework, provides the mathematical derivation for the multipole expansion and linear response, details the neural network implementation, and validates the model through benchmarks on various physical systems and spectroscopic properties.

</details>

<details><summary>Abstract</summary>

Long-range electrostatics and polarization remain central obstacles to extending machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) to ionic, polar, and interfacial systems. Here, we introduce a semi-local framework for learning electrostatics from energies and forces using polarizable atomic multipoles. Local equivariant descriptors predict environment-dependent latent monopoles, dipoles, and quadrupoles, while residual non-local charge transfer and polarization are captured by non-self-consistent linear response in induced charges and dipoles. Across four diverse benchmarks and four short-range MLIP architectures, the multipole hierarchy and response terms systematically improve potential energy surface accuracy, with the largest gains in systems where long-range effects are essential. More importantly, the learned latent variables recover physically meaningful electrical responses: accurate Born effective charge tensors, emergent polarizabilities, infrared spectra in close agreement with experiments, and semi-quantitative Raman spectra for bulk water and hybrid MAPbI$_3$ perovskite. This systematically improvable, physically transparent framework enables MLIPs trained on standard energy and force labels to predict polarization-sensitive observables.

</details>

### [Quantum oscillations and nonsaturating magnetoresistivity in nodal-line semimetals](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06044v1)

**Authors:** Rui Min, Yi-Xiang Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06044v1>

The carrier density is fixed as ¯n0 = 0.02042 in (a) and (c), and ¯n0 = 0.08946 in (b) and (d). The legends are the same in all figures.</sub>
